@classmethod\n   294\t    def lookup(cls, code: types.CodeType, lineno: int, *key: Any) -> types.CodeType:\n   295\t        if code not in cls.cache:\n   296\t            cls.cache[code] = {}\n   297\t        key = tuple(key)\n   298\t        if key not in cls.cache[code]:\n   299\t            cls.cache[code][key] = cls.generate(code, lineno, *key)\n   300\t        return cls.cache[code][key]\n   301\t\n   302\t    @classmethod\n   303\t    def generate(\n   304\t        cls,\n   305\t        code: types.CodeType,\n   306\t        lineno: int,\n   307\t        offset: int,\n   308\t        setup_fn_target_offsets: tuple[int, ...],  # only used in Python 3.11+\n   309\t        nstack: int,\n   310\t        argnames: tuple[str, ...],\n   311\t        argnames_null: tuple[str, ...],\n   312\t        setup_fns: tuple[ReenterWith, ...],\n   313\t        stack_ctx_vars: tuple[tuple[int, tuple[Any, ...]], ...],\n   314\t        argnames_ctx_vars: tuple[tuple[str, tuple[Any, ...]], ...],\n   315\t        null_idxes: tuple[int, ...],\n   316\t        # mainly used to ensure distinct code objects per stack trace,\n   317\t        # which prevents excessive recompilation of inner frames\n   318\t        nested_code_objs: tuple[types.CodeType],\n   319\t    ) -> types.CodeType:\n   320\t        assert offset is not None\n   321\t        assert not (\n   322\t            code.co_flags\n   323\t            & (CO_GENERATOR | CO_COROUTINE | CO_ITERABLE_COROUTINE | CO_ASYNC_GENERATOR)\n   324\t        )\n   325\t        assert code.co_flags & CO_OPTIMIZED\n   326\t        if code in ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata:\n   327\t            return cls.generate_based_on_original_code_object(\n   328\t                code,\n   329\t                lineno,\n   330\t                offset,\n   331\t                setup_fn_target_offsets,\n   332\t                nstack,\n   333\t                argnames,\n   334\t                argnames_null,\n   335\t                setup_fns,\n   336\t                stack_ctx_vars,\n   337\t                argnames_ctx_vars,\n   338\t                null_idxes,\n   339\t update(\n   346\t            instructions: list[Instruction], code_options: dict[str, Any]\n   347\t        ) -> None:\n   348\t            meta.instructions = copy.deepcopy(instructions)\n   349\t\n   350\t            args = [\"__nested_resume_fns\", \"__nested_frame_values\"]\n   351\t            args += [f\"___stack{i}\" for i in range(nstack)]\n   352\t            args.extend(v for v in argnames if v not in args)\n   353\t            freevars = tuple(code_options[\"co_cellvars\"] or []) + tuple(\n   354\t                code_options[\"co_freevars\"] or []\n   355\t            )\n   356\t            freevars = tuple(sorted(freevars))\n   357\t            code_options[\"co_name\"] = (\n   358\t code_options[\"co_qualname\"].rsplit(\".\", maxsplit=1)\n   362\t                if len(qualified_path) == 1:\n   363\t                    code_options[\"co_qualname\"] = code_options[\"co_name\"]\n   364\t                else:\n   365\t                    assert len(qualified_path) == 2\n   366\t                    module_name, co_name = qualified_path\n   367\t                    code_options[\"co_qualname\"] = (\n   368\t                        f\"{module_name}.{TORCH_DYNAMO_RESUME_IN_PREFIX}_{co_name}_at_{lineno}\"\n   369\t                    )\n   370\t            code_options[\"co_firstlineno\"] = lineno\n   371\t            code_options[\"co_cellvars\"] = ()\n   372\t            code_options[\"co_freevars\"] = freevars\n   373\t            code_options[\"co_argcount\"] = len(args)\n   374\t            code_options[\"co_posonlyargcount\"] = 0\n   375\t            code_options[\"co_kwonlyargcount\"] = 0\n   376\t            code_options[\"co_varnames\"] = tuple(\n   377\t                args\n   378\t                + [v for v in argnames_null if v not in args]\n   379\t                + [v for v in 386\t\n   387\t            prefix = []\n   388\t            if is_py311_plus:\n   389\t                if freevars:\n   390\t                    prefix.append(\n   391\t Set is_tracing_resume_prologue to prevent graph breaks.\n   396\t            # This doesn't really do anything at runtime, but dynamo will trace this\n   397\t            # and will know that we're in remove starts_line for any instructions before the graph break instruction\n   534\t            # this will ensure the instructions after the break have the correct line numbers\n   535\t            for inst in instructions:\n   536\t                if inst.offset == target.offset:\n   537\t                    break\n   538\t                inst.starts_line = None\n   539\t                if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\n   540\t                    inst.positions = None\n   541\t\n   542\t            if cleanup:\n   543\t                prefix.extend(cleanup)\n   544\t                prefix.extend(cls.unreachable_codes(code_options))\n   545\t\n   546\t            # remap original instructions' exception table entries\n   547\t            if old_hook_target_remap:\n   548\t                assert is_py311_plus\n   549\t                for inst in instructions:\n   550\t                    if (\n   551\t                        inst.exn_tab_entry\n   552\t                        and inst.exn_tab_entry.target in old_hook_target_remap\n   553\t                    ):\n   554\t                        inst.exn_tab_entry.target = old_hook_target_remap[  # type: ignore[assignment]\n   555\t                            inst.exn_tab_entry.target\n   556\t                        ]\n   557\t\n   558\t            # TODO(jansel): add dead code elimination here\n   559\t            instructions[:] = prefix + instructions\n   560\t\n   561\t        new_code, _ = transform_code_object(code, update)\n   562\t        ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata[new_code] = meta\n   563\t        return new_code\n   564\t\n   565\t    @staticmethod\n   566\t    def unreachable_codes(code_options: dict[str, Any]) -> list[Instruction]:\n   567\t        \"\"\"Codegen a `raise None` to make analysis work for unreachable code\"\"\"\n   568\t        return [\n   569\t            create_load_const(None),\n   570\t            create_instruction(\"RAISE_VARARGS\", arg=1),\n   571\t        ]\n   572\t\n   573\t    @classmethod\n   574\t    def generate_based_on_original_code_object(\n   575\t        cls,\n   576\t        code: types.CodeType,\n   577\t        lineno: int,\n   578\t        offset: int,\n   579\t        setup_fn_target_offsets: tuple[int, ...],\n   580\t        *args: Any,\n   581\t    ) -> types.CodeType:\n   582\t        \"\"\"\n   583\t        This handles the case of generating a resume into code generated\n   584\t        to resume something else.  We want to always generate starting\n   585\t        from the original code object so that if control flow paths\n   586\t        converge we only generated 1 resume function (rather than 2^n\n   587\t        resume functions).\n   588\t        \"\"\"\n   589\t\n   590\t        meta: ResumeFunctionMetadata = ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata[\n   591\t            code\n   592\t        ]\n   593\t        new_offset = -1\n   594\t\n   595\t        def find_new_offset(\n   596\t            instructions: list[Instruction], code_options: dict[str, Any]\n   597\t        ) -> None:\n   598\t            nonlocal new_offset\n   599\t            (target,) = (i for i in instructions if i.offset == offset)\n   600\t            # match the functions starting at the last instruction as we have added a prefix\n   601\t            (new_target,) = (\n   602\t                i2\n   603\t                for i1, i2 in zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions))\n   604\t                if i1 is target\n   605\t            )\n   606\t            assert target.opcode == new_target.opcode\n   607\t            assert new_target.offset is not None\n   608\t            new_offset = new_target.offset\n   609\t\n   610\t        transform_code_object(code, find_new_offset)\n   611\t        assert new_offset >= 0\n   612\t\n   613\t        if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\n   614\t            # setup_fn_target_offsets currently contains the target offset of\n   615\t            # each setup_fn, based on `code`. When we codegen the resume function\n   616\t            # based on the original code object, `meta.code`, the offsets in\n   617\t            # setup_fn_target_offsets must be based on `meta.code` instead.\n   618\t            if new_offset not in meta.block_target_offset_remap:\n   619\t                block_target_offset_remap = meta.block_target_offset_remap[\n   620\t                    new_offset\n   621\t                ] = {}\n   622\t\n   623\t                def remap_block_offsets(\n   624\t                    instructions: list[Instruction], code_options: dict[str, Any]\n   625\t                ) -> None:\n   626\t                    # NOTE: each prefix block generates exactly one PUSH_EXC_INFO,\n   627\t                    # so we can tell which block a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO belongs to,\n   628\t                    # by counting. Then we can use meta.prefix_block-target_offset_remap\n   629\t                    # to determine where in the original code the PUSH_EXC_INFO offset\n   630\t                    # replaced.\n   631\t                    prefix_blocks: list[Instruction] = []\n   632\t                    for inst in instructions:\n   633\t                        if len(prefix_blocks) == len(\n   634\t                            meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap\n   635\t                        ):\n   636\t                            break\n   637\t                        if inst.opname == \"PUSH_EXC_INFO\":\n   638\t                            prefix_blocks.append(inst)\n   639\t\n   640\t                    # offsets into prefix\n   641\t                    for inst, o in zip(\n   642\t                        prefix_blocks, meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap\n   643\t                    ):\n   644\t                        block_target_offset_remap[cast(int, inst.offset)] = o\n   645\t\n   646\t                    # old bytecode targets are after the prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO's\n   647\t                    old_start_offset = (\n   648\t                        cast(int, prefix_blocks[-1].offset) if prefix_blocks else -1\n   649\t                    )\n   650\t                    # offsets into old bytecode\n   651\t                    old_inst_offsets = sorted(\n   652\t                        n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset\n   653\t                    )\n   654\t                    targets = _filter_iter(\n   655\t                        instructions, old_inst_offsets, lambda inst, o: inst.offset == o\n   656\t                    )\n   657\t                    new_targets = _filter_iter(\n   658\t                        zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions)),\n   659\t                        targets,\n   660\t                        lambda v1, v2: v1[0] is v2,\n   661\t                    )\n   662\t                    for new, old in zip(new_targets, targets):\n   663\t                        block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset\n   664\t\n   665\t                transform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)\n   666\t\n   667\t            # if offset is not in setup_fn_target_offsets, it is an error\n   668\t            setup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n   669\t                meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n   670\t                for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n   671\t            )\n   672\t        return ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(\n   673\t            meta.code, lineno, new_offset, setup_fn_target_offsets, *args\n   674\t        )\n"

{
  "thought": "The task is to implement a fix, and tests need to run. But since torch isn't fully installed (it's a source checkout), I need to understand what files I need to modify based on the description and existing code.\n\nThe task description says:\n1. Fix offset remapping logic in `torch/_dynamo/resume_execution.py`\n2. Adjust `symbolic_convert.py` for correct exception table entry and STORE_ATTR handling\n3. Move and update test cases in test_ctx_manager.py (from test_repros.py)\n\nThe evaluation compares against a ground-truth diff. Let me look at the existing code more carefully to understand what needs to change.\n\nKey information:\n- Python 3.13+ (we're on 3.13)\n- KeyError in `resume_execution.py` when processing `setup_fn_target_offsets` at line 669: `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]` - this fails when `n` is not in `block_target_offset_remap`\n- The issue is around how offsets are remapped for nested context managers\n\nLet me look at GitHub for the actual fix. The PR description mentions this is related to fixing KeyError in resume_execution and fixing STORE_ATTR in symbolic_convert.py.\n\nLet me look at the issue more carefully. The problem is that when there are nested context managers (e.g., nested `torch.no_grad()` blocks), the `setup_fn_target_offsets` contain offsets that can appear in either the prefix (new blocks from hooks) or in the original bytecode. But the current `remap_block_offsets` function only handles offsets that are in the \"old bytecode\" section (after the prefix blocks). \n\nLooking at line 650-663:\n```python\nold_inst_offsets = sorted(\n    n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset\n)\n```\n\nThis filters out offsets that are <= `old_start_offset` (i.e., offsets that are in the prefix area). But some setup_fn_target_offsets might point to instructions that ARE in the prefix (the new blocks generated by hooks).\n\nThe fix should also handle offsets that point into prefix blocks.\n\nLet me look at what offsets are from prefix blocks. After line 644:\n```python\nblock_target_offset_remap[cast(int, inst.offset)] = o\n```\n\nThis maps the offset of a PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction (in prefix) to its original offset. So for prefix blocks, the mapping goes: current prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset -> original hook target offset.\n\nBut the issue is when `n in setup_fn_target_offsets` points to a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset (because the previous resume generation used a prefix block as the target), that offset is now `<= old_start_offset` so it's filtered out by the `if n > old_start_offset` condition.\n\nWait, let me re-read. The prefix block target offsets could ALSO be in setup_fn_target_offsets. When they ARE in setup_fn_target_offsets AND they're in prefix blocks, they should already be in block_target_offset_remap from lines 641-644.\n\nSo the fix might be: instead of filtering `n > old_start_offset`, we should include ALL setup_fn_target_offsets that are NOT already in block_target_offset_remap (from the prefix mapping).\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The `block_target_offset_remap` maps: current offset -> original offset. But line 663:\n```python\nblock_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset\n```\nThis maps: current offset in `code` -> offset in `meta.code` (original code).\n\nWait, let me re-read more carefully. The `code` here is a resume function (generated from meta.code), and `meta.code` is the original code.\n\nSo `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains offsets in `code` (the resume function), and we need to map them to offsets in `meta.code`.\n\nFor prefix blocks:\n- Lines 641-644 map `inst.offset` (a PUSH_EXC_INFO in `code`) -> `o` (original offset in `meta.code`)\n\nFor old bytecode blocks:\n- Lines 662-663 map `old.offset` (offset in `code`) -> `new[1].offset` (offset in `meta.code`)\n\nSo the combined remap covers both prefix and old bytecode offsets.\n\nThe KeyError at line 669 happens when `n` (from `setup_fn_target_offsets`) is not found in `block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`.\n\nThis could happen if:\n1. `n` is a prefix block offset that was NOT correctly captured\n2. The old_start_offset comparison is wrong\n\nLet me look at what `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` contains. It's a list of original offsets, one per PUSH_EXC_INFO in the prefix. After line 443-444, it's reversed because \"targets of later/nested contexts are inserted into the mapping later, but show up earlier in the prefix.\"\n\nThen in `remap_block_offsets`, line 641-644:\n```python\nfor inst, o in zip(prefix_blocks, meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n    block_target_offset_remap[cast(int, inst.offset)] = o\n```\n\nThis pairs up PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions (found in order) with original offsets (already reversed).\n\nThe issue might be that the PUSH_EXC_INFO count doesn't match up, or that some offsets in `setup_fn_target_offsets` are from prefix blocks but pointing to different prefix blocks.\n\nActually, I think the real issue might be different. When we have nested context managers in a resume function, the `setup_fn_target_offsets` for the second resume might include an offset that points into the prefix of the FIRST resume function (which is `code` here). But when we try to find this offset in `meta.instructions` (the original code object), there's no such offset because it was generated by the first resume.\n\nLet me look at PR #162455 referenced in the git log message (which might be related): \"Apply the fix from #162455\".\n\nActually, the fix I need to implement is described in the issue. Let me focus on understanding the exact issue and implementing a fix.\n\nThe KeyError on line 669 means `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]` fails. This means `n` (one of the `setup_fn_target_offsets`) is not in `block_target_offset_remap`.\n\nLooking at the `remap_block_offsets` function:\n1. It collects PUSH_EXC_INFO from prefix (lines 631-638)\n2. Maps them to original offsets (641-644)\n3. Filters `old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset)`\n4. Then maps those to original offsets (662-663)\n\nThe problem: if `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains an offset that is:\n- Not in the prefix blocks, AND\n- Not in the old bytecode after prefix\n\nThis could happen with nested context managers where the offset in the new code points to a block that's between prefix blocks.\n\nActually, I think the problem might be simpler. Let me look at the exact condition that causes the KeyError.\n\nThe `old_start_offset` is set to the offset of the last prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO (or -1 if no prefix blocks). Then:\n```python\nold_inst_offsets = sorted(\n    n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset\n)\n```\n\nIf an offset `n` is exactly equal to `old_start_offset` (a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO), it would be excluded from `old_inst_offsets`. But it should already be in `block_target_offset_remap` from the prefix mapping in lines 641-644. However, those lines map the `inst.offset` (PUSH_EXC_INFO in `code`), not the original target offset.\n\nWait, wait. Let me re-read the code again.\n\n`setup_fn_target_offsets` contains the **target offsets** of exception table entries in `code`. In Python 3.11+, these are offsets of PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions.\n\nLooking at prefix_blocks: it collects PUSH_EXC_INFO from `instructions` (the current code `code`). Their offsets are mapped to original offsets via `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap`.\n\nThen `old_inst_offsets` is filtered to only include offsets `> old_start_offset` (the last prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset). This is assuming that all offsets in `setup_fn_target_offsets` that are <= `old_start_offset` are already covered by prefix mapping.\n\nBut what if:\n1. One of the `setup_fn_target_offsets` is a PUSH_EXC_INFO from the prefix, \n2. AND that PUSH_EXC_INFO was NOT included in `prefix_blocks` because the count already reached `len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap)`?\n\nThis could happen if there are MORE PUSH_EXC_INFO in the prefix than `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` has entries.\n\nOr, what if the `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains an offset that IS a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO (so <= old_start_offset), but was NOT captured in `block_target_offset_remap` because the loop broke early?\n\nActually wait - in lines 631-638:\n```python\nfor inst in instructions:\n    if len(prefix_blocks) == len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n        break\n    if inst.opname == \"PUSH_EXC_INFO\":\n        prefix_blocks.append(inst)\n```\n\nThis collects EXACTLY `len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap)` PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions, stopping when that count is reached. \n\nNow what if `code` (the current resume function being analyzed) was ITSELF generated with a prefix that has MORE PUSH_EXC_INFO than meta expects? This shouldn't happen since we generate based on `meta.code`.\n\nActually, I think the real issue is with `n > old_start_offset`. If `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains an offset that happens to equal one of the PREFIX PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets (i.e., `n == old_start_offset` for the last one, or `n` for one of the earlier ones but > old_start_offset for an earlier PUSH_EXC_INFO)...\n\nHmm wait, `n > old_start_offset` only excludes n <= old_start_offset. For prefix blocks, the PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets would all be <= old_start_offset (since old_start_offset is the offset of the LAST prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO, which has the greatest offset).\n\nBut what about offsets that are in setup_fn_target_offsets AND are offset of a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO (not the last one)?\n\nFor example: if prefix has PUSH_EXC_INFO at offsets 10 and 20, then `old_start_offset = 20`. `block_target_offset_remap` has entries for 10 -> orig_offset_A and 20 -> orig_offset_B.\n\nIf `setup_fn_target_offsets` = (10, 20), then:\n- 10 <= 20 -> excluded from old_inst_offsets \u2713 (already in block_target_offset_remap as 10 -> orig_A)\n- 20 <= 20 -> excluded from old_inst_offsets \u2713 (already in block_target_offset_remap as 20 -> orig_B)\n\nThis seems fine.\n\nBut what if `setup_fn_target_offsets` includes an offset that's NOT in the prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets but IS <= old_start_offset? Then it would be:\n- Excluded from old_inst_offsets (because <= old_start_offset)\n- NOT in block_target_offset_remap from prefix mapping\n- NOT in block_target_offset_remap from old bytecode mapping\n\nThis would cause a KeyError!\n\nSo the question is: when would `setup_fn_target_offsets` contain an offset that is <= the last prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset but is NOT a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset?\n\nThis could happen if the offset is an instruction that appears in the code between prefix PUSH_EXC_INFOs but is not itself a PUSH_EXC_INFO. But setup_fn_target_offsets should contain exception table entry targets, which in Python 3.11+ are always PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions...\n\nWait, actually in Python 3.11+, exception table entry targets point to PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions. But what about try/finally? Let me think about the template code in `try_finally`:\n\n```python\ndef _template(ctx: AbstractContextManager[Any], dummy: Any) -> None:\n    ctx.__enter__()\n    try:\n        dummy\n    finally:\n        ctx.__exit__(None, None, None)\n```\n\nIn Python 3.13, try/finally might use different opcodes. Let me think...\n\nActually, I think a key insight is this: in Python 3.11+, the exception table entries for the `with` block generated by the template may target different instructions. Specifically, the `with` block's exception handler might be a `PUSH_EXC_INFO` instruction, but the cleanup code might have a different structure in Python 3.13.\n\nI need to look at the actual fix. Let me search for any related issues/PRs in the codebase comments or check what the expected changes are.\n\nLooking at the description again:\n- \"The `KeyError` occurs in `resume_execution.py` when Dynamo tries to reconstruct bytecode for resuming execution after a graph break inside nested context managers\"\n- \"The fix restructures the offset remapping logic in `torch/_dynamo/resume_execution.py` and adjusts `symbolic_convert.py` to correctly handle exception table entries and store-attribute operations\"\n\nKey change areas:\n1. `remap_block_offsets` in `resume_execution.py` - needs to handle case where an offset in `setup_fn_target_offsets` is not found in the current mappings\n2. `symbolic_convert.py` - STORE_ATTR handling in with blocks\n\nFor test_repros.py to test_ctx_manager.py: tests are being MOVED, not just added.\n\nLet me look at what tests might be in test_repros.py that relate to nested context managers, and what's in test_ctx_manager.py that might be new.\n"

We do not push blocks here since\n  1376\t            # with blocks are pushed when handling BEFORE_WITH.\n  1377\t            entry = inst.exn_tab_entry\n  1378\t            if entry:\n  1379\t                # Detect when we have exited the top with block.\n  1380\t                # The with blocks on the block stack are not enclosed in try\n  1381\t                # blocks, so a with block's cleanup code should be in the\n  1382\t                # previous with block (if any).\n  1383\t                if (\n  1384\t                    len(self.block_stack) >= 2\n  1385\t                    and entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n  1386\t                    and entry.target is self.block_stack[-2].target\n  1387\t                ):\n  1388\t                    # exit the current block\n  1389\t                    self.block_stack.pop()\n  1390\t            else:\n  1391\t                # no longer in any block\n  1392\t                # It is possible for NOPs to be between two instructions\n  1393\t                # in the same block, but the NOPs are not covered by an\n  1394\t                # exception table entry. As I analyzed above, they should be found...\n\nUnless there's an issue with how the STORE_ATTR's exn_tab_entry affects the block stack.\n\nLet me look at the update_block_stack function and how it handles STORE_ATTR:\n\n```python\ndef update_block_stack(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:\n    entry = inst.exn_tab_entry\n    if entry:\n        if (\n            len(self.block_stack) >= 2\n            and entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n            and entry.target is self.block_stack[-2].target\n        ):\n            self.block_stack.pop()\n    else:\n        if self.block_stack and inst.opname not in (\"NOP\", \"JUMP_BACKWARD\"):\n            assert len(self.block_stack) == 1\n            self.block_stack.pop()\n```\n\nIf STORE_ATTR has exn_tab_entry.target pointing to some PUSH_EXC_INFO, and that doesn't match the top of the block stack, the block stack might be incorrectly modified.\n\nHere's a possible issue: in `code1`, after some prefix setup, when we reach the STORE_ATTR in the old bytecode section, its `exn_tab_entry` might still point to the ORIGINAL code's PUSH_EXC_INFO (if the exn_tab_entry remapping in `generate` didn't handle it). So the output code DOES have the PUSH_EXC_INFO. The STORE_ATTR's exn_tab_entry target should point to this PUSH_EXC_INFO in the output.\n\nBut here's the problem: in `store_attr_graph_break`, the cleanup code from the block stack is NOT generated! This is because `compile_subgraph` calls `block.exit()` for each block, which generates the exit code, but the PUSH_EXC_INFO that wraps it is not explicitly generated in `store_attr_graph_break`.\n\nActually, let me look at what `handle_graph_break` does. It:\n1. Calls `compile_subgraph`\n2. Generates cleanup code for the block stack (PUSH_EXC_INFO + cleanup)\n3. Copies the instruction (clearing exn_tab_entry)\n4. Adds cleanup code\n5. Creates call to resume function\n\nIn `store_attr_graph_break`, steps 2 and 4 are missing. The block stack cleanup is NOT generated. But `compile_subgraph` already handles the block_stack by calling `block.exit()`. So maybe the cleanup isn't needed in the output? Or maybe it IS needed?\n\nActually, looking more carefully at `compile_subgraph` line 1375-1377:\n```python\nfor block in reversed(cur_tx.block_stack):\n    block.exit(cur_tx, is_graph_break=reason.graph_break)\n```\n\nThis exits context managers for the compiled graph part. The output graph returns values without being in the context managers. That's correct for the compiled part.\n\nThen in the output code (after the compiled graph), we need to RESTORE the context managers before calling the resume function. That's what `handle_graph_break`'s cleanup code does - it sets up try/finally blocks so that if an exception occurs in the resume function, the context managers are properly cleaned up.\n\nFor `store_attr_graph_break`, this cleanup code is missing! The STORE_ATTR itself doesn't need the context manager because it's a direct Python operation. But the RESUME function after STORE_ATTR needs the context managers. And more importantly, if an exception propagates through the STORE_ATTR, the context managers need to be cleaned up.\n\nBut the really relevant part for the bug: the STORE_ATTR instruction's exn_tab_entry might cause issues.\n\nLet me think again. In the OUTPUT code (which includes the compiled graph call + STORE_ATTR + resume function call), what does the exception table look like?\n\nWith `copy.copy(inst)` including the exn_tab_entry:\n- The STORE_ATTR has exn_tab_entry.target pointing to some PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- But that PUSH_EXC_INFO might not exist in the output code\n\nWait, but `handle_graph_break` generates the cleanup code with PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions. These would be the targets. If the exn_tab_entry points to the SAME PUSH_EXC_INFO, this would work.\n\nBut in `store_attr_graph_break`, there's no cleanup code with PUSH_EXC_INFO! So the exn_tab_entry of STORE_ATTR points to a non-existent instruction.\n\nActually, `compile_subgraph` might generate PUSH_EXC_INFO as part of the block exit code... let me look at how `block.exit()` works.\n\nLet me search for how exit() generates bytecode for context managers in the output.\n\nActually, I think the fix for STORE_ATTR is simply to clear the exn_tab_entry (like in handle_graph_break), because:\n1. The output of the compiled graph + STORE_ATTR doesn't need to have exception handlers for within-with-block exceptions (that's handled by the resume function)\n2. Having an invalid exn_tab_entry would cause bytecode generation failures or incorrect behavior\n\nSo the fix for STORE_ATTR is:\n```python\ninst_copy = copy.copy(inst)\ninst_copy.exn_tab_entry = None\nself.output.add_output_instructions([inst_copy])\n```\n\nNow for the KeyError in resume_execution.py, let me think about a scenario where STORE_ATTR is in a with block and causes a graph break.\n\nAfter the STORE_ATTR graph break:\n- The compiled graph output includes STORE_ATTR (now with exn_tab_entry cleared)\n- A resume function code1 is generated\n\nWhen code1 is later executed and encounters ANOTHER graph break (inside the with block), we need to generate code2 from meta.code (original).\n\nIn code1, the block stack would have entries for the context managers. Their targets point to PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1's prefix. \n\nBut HERE'S THE ISSUE: when STORE_ATTR caused the first graph break, the `block_stack` at that time had entries for the context managers. Let me look at what happens in `store_attr_graph_break`:\n\n1. `compile_subgraph` is called with the current `self` (which has the block_stack with context managers)\n2. The block stack's entries are examined\n3. BUT: there's NO cleanup code generation for the context managers!\n\nLooking at `handle_graph_break` more carefully, it generates cleanup code for the OUTPUT. But `store_attr_graph_break` doesn't do this for the OUTPUT.\n\nNow when `create_call_resume_at` is called:\n```python\nself.output.add_output_instructions(\n    self.create_call_resume_at(\n        self.next_instruction, all_stack_locals_metadata, False\n    )\n)\n```\n\nThis generates the resume function. In `create_call_resume_at`, `setup_fn_target_offsets` = `tuple(b.target.offset for b in cur_tx.block_stack)`. These are the offsets of the block stack targets IN THE ORIGINAL CODE.\n\nWait, actually no. When tracing the original function, the block stack entries have targets pointing to instructions in the ORIGINAL code. When `BEFORE_WITH` is processed:\n```python\ntarget = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\nThis target is from `self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`, which is an instruction object in the ORIGINAL code's instruction list (the ones set up by `transform_code_object`).\n\nSo `b.target.offset` IS the offset in the original code. And `setup_fn_target_offsets` for the first resume generation uses these original offsets. This is fine.\n\nBut when tracing `code1` (the first resume function), the BEFORE_WITH in the OLD BYTECODE SECTION of code1 has been REMAPPED. Its exn_tab_entry.target now points to a PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's PREFIX (due to the remapping in lines 546-556 of resume_execution.py). So when code1 is traced, `b.target.offset` = code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset.\n\nThis prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset should be in `block_target_offset_remap` after `remap_block_offsets` runs... so no KeyError.\n\nUNLESS the STORE_ATTR's exn_tab_entry copy was KEEPING the reference to the ORIGINAL code's PUSH_EXC_INFO, which was then included in the output. When the output code is compiled, this exn_tab_entry would point to the wrong instruction.\n\nActually, I think the issue is more subtle. Let me look at what exn_tab_entry contains:\n\nThe exn_tab_entry.target is an INSTRUCTION OBJECT (not just an offset). It references a specific instruction object. In the original code, when we `copy.copy(inst)` for STORE_ATTR, we get a shallow copy. The `exn_tab_entry` in the copy still references the SAME target instruction object as in the original.\n\nWhen this is added to the output instructions, the bytecode generation (in `bytecode_transformation.py`) would process the output instructions and try to compute exception table entries. It would need to find the target instruction in the output instructions to get its compiled offset. But if the target instruction (PUSH_EXC_INFO from original code) is NOT in the output instructions, it might either be ignored or cause an error.\n\nActually, I think in Python 3.11+, if the target instruction is not in the output, it might just be ignored (the entry might be dropped). But if it IS somehow referenced, it could cause issues.\n\nOK, I think I need to look at this differently. The task says:\n1. Fix KeyError in resume_execution.py\n2. Fix STORE_ATTR in symbolic_convert.py\n\nAnd these are related bugs. The most likely scenario is:\n\n1. STORE_ATTR in a with block causes a graph break\n2. The STORE_ATTR instruction is copied to the output WITH its exn_tab_entry\n3. This exn_tab_entry causes the generated output bytecode to have an incorrect exception table entry\n4. When this output bytecode is executed and hit a SECOND graph break inside the with block (from the resume function), the offset remapping in generate_based_on_original_code_object fails because the exn_tab_entry was not properly handled\n\nOR alternatively:\n\n1. There's a nested with block where STORE_ATTR or another instruction has its exn_tab_entry pointing to a PUSH_EXC_INFO at a certain offset\n2. This offset is in the range that gets filtered by `n > old_start_offset` but is NOT properly in `block_target_offset_remap`\n\nLet me look at the handle_graph_break more carefully for the CALL instruction case vs non-CALL:\n\nFor CALL (lines 987-1002):\n```python\nif sys.version_info >= (3, 11) and inst.opname == \"CALL\":\n    ...\n    call_insts = create_call_function(inst.arg, False)\n    ...\n```\n\nFor non-CALL (lines 1003-1008):\n```python\nelse:\n    # copy instruction, but without exception table data\n    assert inst.target is None\n    inst_copy = copy.copy(inst)\n    inst_copy.exn_tab_entry = None\n    self.output.add_output_instructions([inst_copy])\n```\n\nSo for non-CALL, exn_tab_entry is cleared. For STORE_ATTR (going through store_attr_graph_break), it's NOT cleared.\n\nBut there's also this at line 1005: `assert inst.target is None`. For STORE_ATTR, `inst.target` should indeed be None (STORE_ATTR is not a jump instruction). So this assertion would pass.\n\nBut in `store_attr_graph_break`, we just do `copy.copy(inst)` without clearing exn_tab_entry.\n\nThe question is: does the exn_tab_entry of STORE_ATTR being NOT cleared cause the KeyError?\n\nHere's my hypothesis for how the KeyError occurs:\n\n1. Original function has:\n```\nwith ctx1:          # BEFORE_WITH_1, exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO_1\n    with ctx2:      # BEFORE_WITH_2, exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO_2\n        obj.attr = val  # STORE_ATTR, exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO_2\n        graph_break()  # will be a CALL graph break\n```\n\n2. STORE_ATTR causes graph break first (speculation fails for STORE_ATTR). \n\nActually, STORE_ATTR graph break uses speculation (`speculation.failed(self)`). Let me think about whether the graph break happens during the FIRST trace or after.\n\nIn the first dynamo trace of the original function, when BEFORE_WITH_1 and BEFORE_WITH_2 are processed, blocks are pushed. Then STORE_ATTR is encountered. If it triggers a graph break, `compile_subgraph` is called, STORE_ATTR is added to output, and a resume function is generated.\n\nThe resume function (code1) starts at `next_instruction` after STORE_ATTR. code1 is generated from the original function. In code1:\n- Prefix sets up ctx1 and ctx2 (via hooks)\n- Old bytecode starts at next_instruction (after STORE_ATTR)\n\nWhen code1 is TRACED by Dynamo:\n- The prefix prologue is traced (with is_tracing_resume_prologue=True)\n- After the prologue, the old bytecode is traced\n- graph_break() is encountered -> another graph break\n\nAt this point, for the SECOND graph break:\n- block_stack has entries for ctx1 and ctx2\n- Their targets point to PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1's prefix\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (P1, P2) where P1 and P2 are code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets\n\nIn generate_based_on_original_code_object:\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap should have the original offsets for P1 and P2\n- remap_block_offsets collects prefix_blocks and maps them\n\nLet's say meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O2, O1] (reversed, inner first)\nAnd instructions have prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO at: P_inner (= P2, for ctx2) and P_outer (= P1, for ctx1)\n\nAfter prefix mapping:\n- block_target_offset_remap[P2] = O2 (inner ctx2's original offset)\n- block_target_offset_remap[P1] = O1 (outer ctx1's original offset)\n\nNow setup_fn_target_offsets = (P1, P2). Both should be found in block_target_offset_remap. No KeyError!\n\nHmm. So my analysis says there should be NO KeyError. But the bug says there IS a KeyError.\n\nOK let me think about what's different when STORE_ATTR is involved.\n\nActually, WAIT. Let me reconsider whether the block_stack entries correctly point to the PREFIX PUSH_EXC_INFOs.\n\nWhen code1 is TRACED:\n1. Prefix is executed (prologue)\n2. In the prefix, the hooks call `__enter__()` for ctx1 and ctx2\n3. The hooks generate PUSH_EXC_INFO in the generated code - but these are PREFIX instructions, not traced by Dynamo as BEFORE_WITH\n\nSo when Dynamo traces code1's prefix, it sees:\n- LOAD_FAST for ctx1 class, CALL_FUNCTION, etc.\n- The try/finally template instructions including PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nBut BEFORE_WITH is NOT in the prefix. BEFORE_WITH is in the OLD BYTECODE of the original function.\n\nSo when does `block_stack` get populated in code1's trace?\n\nFor the ctx1 and ctx2 that were set up in the PREFIX of code1... they're NOT pushed to block_stack via BEFORE_WITH! They're set up via try/finally template. When Dynamo traces the prefix, it processes:\n- LOAD_FAST, etc. - traced as normal\n- But the context managers ARE re-entered\n\nBut the BLOCK STACK for the DYNAMO BLOCK STACK needs the targets to come from BEFORE_WITH instructions... which are NOT in the prefix of code1.\n\nUnless... when Dynamo traces the JUMP to the old bytecode's first instruction, and then processes the old bytecode, does it encounter BEFORE_WITH?\n\nIf the original function has:\n```\nBEFORE_WITH_1    # offset 10, exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO_1 at offset 100\n...\nBEFORE_WITH_2    # offset 20, exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO_2 at offset 200\n...\nSTORE_ATTR       # offset 30, graph break point 1\n...\ngraph_break()    # offset 40, graph break point 2\n```\n\ncode1 starts at offset 40 (next_instruction after STORE_ATTR). The prefix of code1 re-enters ctx1 and ctx2 without BEFORE_WITH. Then the old bytecode starts at offset 40 (graph_break() call).\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1, it processes:\n1. Prefix (prologue, is_tracing_resume_prologue=True)\n2. JMP to graph_break() at offset 40\n\nThere is NO BEFORE_WITH_1 or BEFORE_WITH_2 being processed! So the block_stack is NEVER POPULATED with ctx1 and ctx2 entries!\n\nThis means when graph_break() at offset 40 triggers a graph break, `block_stack` is empty, and `setup_fn_target_offsets` = (). So there's no KeyError... but there might be an issue with the context managers not being properly handled.\n\nBut wait, BUT the exception table entries of the instructions in code1's old bytecode section HAVE BEEN REMAPPED! The graph_break() call instruction's exn_tab_entry has been remapped to point to one of code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions.\n\nBut `update_block_stack` uses the exn_tab_entry to manage the block stack! Let me re-read:\n\n```python\ndef update_block_stack(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:\n    entry = inst.exn_tab_entry\n    if entry:\n        if (\n            len(self.block_stack) >= 2\n            and entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n            and entry.target is self.block_stack[-2].target\n        ):\n            self.block_stack.pop()\n    else:\n        if self.block_stack and inst.opname not in (\"NOP\", \"JUMP_BACKWARD\"):\n            assert len(self.block_stack) == 1\n            self.block_stack.pop()\n```\n\nThis function only POPS from block_stack, never pushes. PUSHING happens in BEFORE_WITH.\n\nSo if there's no BEFORE_WITH executed in code1's trace, block_stack remains empty even though the instructions have exn_tab_entry entries pointing to prefix PUSH_EXC_INFOs.\n\nThen when graph_break() causes a second graph break:\n- block_stack is EMPTY\n- `should_compile_partial_graph` might return True (since block_stack is empty, `all(b.can_restore() for b in self.block_stack)` is trivially True for empty block_stack)\n- A second resume function (code2) is generated with setup_fn_target_offsets = ()\n\nAnd in generate_based_on_original_code_object with empty setup_fn_target_offsets:\n- No remapping needed (the tuple is empty)\n- No KeyError\n\nSo this doesn't cause a KeyError either!\n\nOK, I'm clearly missing something. Let me look at the actual test case for the bug more carefully.\n\nActually, wait. Let me reconsider. What if the graph break is INSIDE a nested context manager in code1's old bytecode section? That is, code1 starts BEFORE the BEFORE_WITH instructions, not after them.\n\nScenario:\n```\nOriginal function:\n  graph_break_1   # offset 10 - first graph break\n  BEFORE_WITH_1   # offset 20, pushes ctx1 onto stack\n  ...\n  BEFORE_WITH_2   # offset 30, pushes ctx2 onto stack\n  ...\n  graph_break_2   # offset 40 - second graph break (if code1 is compiled)\n```\n\ncode1 starts at offset 20 (next instruction after graph_break_1).\n- Prefix of code1: empty (no context managers at the first graph break)\n- Old bytecode starts at offset 20\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1:\n1. Processes BEFORE_WITH_1 at offset 20 -> pushes to block_stack with target = remapped target\n2. Processes BEFORE_WITH_2 at offset 30 -> pushes to block_stack\n3. Processes graph_break_2 at offset 40 -> second graph break\n\nAt this point, block_stack has 2 entries. setup_fn_target_offsets = (T1, T2) where T1 and T2 are targets in code1.\n\nSince code1 was just generated from original (no prior resume), meta = metadata for code1. If code1 is the FIRST resume, there should be no issue with generate_based_on_original_code_object (it wouldn't be called since code1 is not in generated_code_metadata yet?).\n\nActually wait - code1 IS in generated_code_metadata: `ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata[new_code] = meta` at line 562. So when code2 is being generated from code1, `generate_based_on_original_code_object` IS called.\n\nIn this case:\n- meta.code = original function\n- code = code1 (first resume, starting at offset 20)\n- In code1, the old bytecode section includes BEFORE_WITH_1 and BEFORE_WITH_2\n- These instructions' exn_tab_entries point to prefix PUSH_EXC_INFOs IF the hooks remapped them\n\nBUT WAIT: code1 was generated without any hooks (since there were no context managers at the first graph break). So:\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [] (empty, no hooks)\n- prefix = [] (empty prefix, no PUSH_EXC_INFO in prefix)\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1 and hits graph_break_2:\n- block_stack has entries for ctx1 and ctx2\n- Their targets are from `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` in code1's old bytecode\n- These targets are NOT remapped (no hooks), so they point to instructions from the ORIGINAL code (offset 50, offset 60, or whatever PUSH_EXC_INFO_1 and PUSH_EXC_INFO_2 are at)\n\nActually, when code1 is generated from the original, the old bytecode instructions are DEEP COPIES (line 349: `meta.instructions = copy.deepcopy(instructions)`). The exn_tab_entry.target references are to instruction objects in this deep copy. These instruction objects have offsets that correspond to the ORIGINAL code's offsets (before the offset was reassigned).\n\nHmm, actually in `transform_code_object`, offsets are assigned at the END of the transformation. So when `update` is called (line 347), `meta.instructions = copy.deepcopy(instructions)` captures instructions that may not have had their offsets assigned yet (or have their PRE-transformation offsets).\n\nOK I'm going too deep into the weeds. Let me just look at what changes are ACTUALLY needed based on the description.\n\nThe description says:\n1. Fix offset remapping logic in resume_execution.py\n2. Adjust symbolic_convert.py for exception table entry and STORE_ATTR handling\n\nLet me look at the actual test_repros test that's being moved:\n- test_with_on_graph_break_nested (line 1854)\n\nAnd what new tests would be added to test_ctx_manager.py.\n\nLet me also look at the `should_compile_partial_graph` and `update_block_stack` more carefully with respect to STORE_ATTR.\n\nOK here's another thought: `store_attr_graph_break` handles STORE_ATTR in with blocks (when `should_compile_partial_graph` is True). But there's no separate handling for the cleanup code that `handle_graph_break` does. \n\nIn `handle_graph_break`, lines 970-984:\n```python\ncg = PyCodegen(self)\ncleanup: list[Instruction] = []\nfor b in self.block_stack:\n    if isinstance(b.with_context, TorchFunctionModeVariable):\n        cg.extend_output(b.resume_fn().try_except_torch_function_mode(...))\n        continue\n    assert b.with_context is not None\n    assert isinstance(b.with_context, (ContextWrappingVariable))\n    b.with_context.reconstruct_type(cg)\n    cg.extend_output(b.resume_fn().try_finally(cg.code_options, cleanup))\nself.output.add_output_instructions(cg.get_instructions())\ndel cg\n...\nself.output.add_output_instructions(cleanup)\n```\n\nThis generates try/finally blocks in the output code to restore context managers. This is critical for exception propagation through the with blocks.\n\nIn `store_attr_graph_break`, this is COMPLETELY ABSENT. The STORE_ATTR happens inside with blocks (otherwise `should_compile_partial_graph` would return False), but the output code doesn't have the cleanup for those with blocks.\n\nThis means the context managers are NOT properly restored if an exception occurs during STORE_ATTR or in the resume function. This would be a correctness bug but not necessarily a KeyError.\n\nBUT, there's more: without the cleanup code being generated, there are no PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in the output. So when STORE_ATTR's exn_tab_entry is copied (pointing to a non-existent PUSH_EXC_INFO), the bytecode generation might fail or silently drop the entry.\n\nIf the entry IS silently dropped, the resulting output code has no exception handler for the STORE_ATTR. This might be OK if we don't care about exceptions...\n\nBut here's the KEY INSIGHT: The exn_tab_entry of STORE_ATTR (copied with copy.copy) references an instruction object that is in the ORIGINAL code's instruction list. When this is added to the output, the bytecode generator tries to find this instruction object in the output instructions list. It's NOT there. So the exception table entry might reference `None` or some garbage, causing incorrect behavior.\n\nLooking at bytecode_transformation.py to see how exception table entries are handled:\n\nActually, let me look at what `transform_code_object` does with exn_tab_entry:\n"
}

Input

{
  "thought": "Now I understand the structure better. The `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` is built by iterating over stack positions from 0 to nstack-1. For each stack position that has a hook (context manager), the hook_target_offset is appended to `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap`.\n\nThen at line 440-445, this list is REVERSED.\n\nThe hooks are processed in ascending order of `stack_index`. For nested context managers:\n- Outer context manager has lower stack index\n- Inner context manager has higher stack index\n\nSo `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` before reversing = [outer_original_offset, inner_original_offset]\nAfter reversing = [inner_original_offset, outer_original_offset]\n\nNow in `remap_block_offsets`, the `prefix_blocks` are collected by iterating through instructions IN ORDER. In the prefix, hooks are added in ascending order of stack_index too. But the comment says \"targets of later/nested contexts are inserted into the mapping later, but show up earlier in the prefix.\"\n\nWait, this seems contradictory. Let me re-read. The hooks are processed in ascending order of stack_index (lines 418-434). For each hook at stack position `i`, hook_insts are added to prefix and meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap gets the hook_target_offset appended.\n\nSo the PREFIX has hooks in order: hook[0], hook[1], ..., hook[n-1] (ascending stack index).\n\nAnd `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` BEFORE reversing = [hook[0]_target, hook[1]_target, ..., hook[n-1]_target].\nAfter reversing = [hook[n-1]_target, ..., hook[1]_target, hook[0]_target].\n\nNow, each hook generates a `try_finally` or similar, which includes a PUSH_EXC_INFO. The PUSH_EXC_INFO for hook[0] comes in hook[0]'s instructions, then hook[1]'s PUSH_EXC_INFO, etc.\n\nBut the comment says \"targets of later/nested contexts are inserted into the mapping later, but show up earlier in the prefix.\" \n\nIf hooks are added in ascending stack_index order:\n- hook[0] (outer, lower stack index) is added FIRST -> appears EARLIER in prefix\n- hook[1] (inner, higher stack index) is added LATER -> appears LATER in prefix\n\nSo outer context's PUSH_EXC_INFO comes BEFORE inner context's PUSH_EXC_INFO in the prefix. The comment must be wrong or I'm misunderstanding something.\n\nWait, let me re-read try_finally:\n\nActually, the \"target\" in hook is the exception handler target. For a try/finally block, the structure is:\n```\n[try body]\nPUSH_EXC_INFO  <-- exception handler\n[cleanup code]\nRERAISE\n```\n\nThe PUSH_EXC_INFO is the EXCEPTION HANDLER TARGET. When an exception occurs in the try body, the IP jumps to PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nIn the prefix generation, hooks are called as `hook(code_options, cleanup)`. This generates:\n- For outer hook: try/finally wrapping the ENTIRE remaining prefix + old bytecode\n- For inner hook: try/finally wrapping the part after the outer hook starts\n\nSo the structure is:\n```\nOUTER TRY START\n...\nINNER TRY START  \n...\nLOAD_FAST ___stack_i (for i in nstack)\n...\nJUMP to target\n\nINNER PUSH_EXC_INFO (inner cleanup)\n...\n\nOUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO (outer cleanup)\n...\n```\n\nBecause the try/finally blocks are NESTED: inner cleanup comes before outer cleanup! So the INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO is at a LOWER offset than the OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO!\n\nBut wait, `cleanup` list is shared across hooks. Let me think...\n\n`hook(code_options, cleanup)` returns `(hook_insts, exn_target)`. The hook_insts are added to prefix, and the PUSH_EXC_INFO (exn_target) goes somewhere in the cleanup.\n\nActually, looking at the try_finally or try_except templates, the pattern is usually:\n```\n[setup code] -> add to prefix\n[exception handler (PUSH_EXC_INFO + cleanup)] -> add to cleanup\n```\n\nSo for nested contexts:\n- hook[0] (outer): adds OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO + OUTER_CLEANUP to cleanup\n- hook[1] (inner): adds INNER_PUSH_EXC_INFO + INNER_CLEANUP to cleanup\n\nThen cleanup = [OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO, OUTER_CLEANUP, INNER_PUSH_EXC_INFO, INNER_CLEANUP]? That doesn't sound right for nested handlers.\n\nActually, let me look at the actual try_finally implementation.\n\nLet me look at `resume_fn().try_finally()`:\n"
}

expect only 1 PUSH_EXC_INFO in epilogue\n   235\t        assert next(push_exc_info_gen, None) is None\n   236\t\n   237\t        return create_ctx + setup_with, push_exc_info_inst\n   238\t\n   239\t\n   240\t@dataclasses.dataclass\n   241\tclass ResumeFunctionMetadata:\n   242\t    code: types.CodeType\n   243\t    instructions: list[Instruction] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)\n   244\t    # Python 3.11+ fields\n   245\t    # NOTE: Python 3.11 removed blocks, but for our purposes, a \"block\" consists\n   246\t    # of instructions of all exception table entries that have the same target.\n   247\t\n   248\t    # map from PUSH_EXC_INFO's in the prefix to original block target offset\n   249\t    prefix_block_target_offset_remap: list[int] = dataclasses.field(\n   250\t        default_factory=list\n   251\t    )\n   252\t    # {
  "thought": "Now I understand the structure better. The `__call__` method returns `(create_ctx + setup_with, push_exc_info_inst)`. The `push_exc_info_inst` is the PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction in the epilogue. This is returned as `exn_target` in line 428 of the generate loop:\n```python\nhook_insts, exn_target = hook(code_options, cleanup)\n```\n\nAnd then:\n```python\nold_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target] = exn_target\n```\n\nSo `old_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target]` = PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object from the epilogue.\n\nNow, the `cleanup` is:\n- After hook[0] (outer): cleanup = [OUTER_EPILOGUE]  # outer cleanup added first\n  - `cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup` means cleanup = outer_epilogue + []\n- After hook[1] (inner): cleanup = [INNER_EPILOGUE, OUTER_EPILOGUE]\n  - `cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup` means cleanup = inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue\n\nSo the cleanup list is ordered: INNER_EPILOGUE first, then OUTER_EPILOGUE. This means:\n- Inner PUSH_EXC_INFO (from inner_epilogue) comes BEFORE outer PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- So inner PUSH_EXC_INFO has a LOWER offset in the generated code\n\nAnd `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` before reversing:\n- Outer is processed first (lower stack_index), so outer_hook_target_offset is appended first\n- Then inner is processed, so inner_hook_target_offset is appended\n- Before reversing: [outer_original_offset, inner_original_offset]\n- After reversing: [inner_original_offset, outer_original_offset]\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets`, prefix_blocks are collected by iterating through instructions (which now includes prefix + old bytecode). The prefix comes first, and the cleanup is appended to prefix (lines 542-544):\n```python\nif cleanup:\n    prefix.extend(cleanup)\n    prefix.extend(cls.unreachable_codes(code_options))\n```\n\nAnd then `instructions[:] = prefix + instructions`. So in the final code, the order is:\n- [setup code for outer hook] (outer setup_with/setup_try_finally)\n- [setup code for inner hook]\n- [load stack vars]\n- [jump to target]\n- [INNER_EPILOGUE] (inner PUSH_EXC_INFO + inner cleanup)\n- [OUTER_EPILOGUE] (outer PUSH_EXC_INFO + outer cleanup)\n- [unreachable codes]\n- [OLD BYTECODE]\n\nSo in the instructions:\n- INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO appears FIRST (lower offset) - it's in inner_epilogue which comes first\n- OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO appears AFTER (higher offset) - it's in outer_epilogue which comes second\n\nAnd `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` after reversing = [inner_original_offset, outer_original_offset].\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets`, prefix_blocks are collected by scanning for PUSH_EXC_INFO:\n- First PUSH_EXC_INFO found: INNER (lower offset) -> prefix_blocks[0]\n- Second PUSH_EXC_INFO found: OUTER (higher offset) -> prefix_blocks[1]\n\nzip(prefix_blocks, meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n- (INNER_PUSH_EXC_INFO, inner_original_offset): block_target_offset_remap[INNER_offset] = inner_original_offset \u2713\n- (OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO, outer_original_offset): block_target_offset_remap[OUTER_offset] = outer_original_offset \u2713\n\nold_start_offset = OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset (the LAST prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO).\n\nOK so this seems correct. The inner context's PUSH_EXC_INFO has a lower offset, and the outer has a higher offset. The mapping works.\n\nBut wait, there might be an issue with the NESTING. In the SECOND resume function (generated from code1), the block stack has entries whose targets point to PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1. When code1 is traced by Dynamo, how does the block stack get populated?\n\nIf code1 starts BEFORE the BEFORE_WITH instructions (i.e., the graph break happened BEFORE entering the context managers), then when Dynamo traces code1, it processes BEFORE_WITH. But if code1 starts INSIDE the context managers (AFTER BEFORE_WITH), then Dynamo never processes BEFORE_WITH for those contexts.\n\nIn the SECOND case (code1 starts after entering contexts):\n- The prefix sets up the contexts via hooks\n- The old bytecode starts at the graph break point, INSIDE the context managers\n- When Dynamo traces code1's old bytecode, it processes the graph break\n- The block_stack entries come from... where?\n\nIn Dynamo's `update_block_stack` for Python 3.11+, the block stack is updated based on `exn_tab_entry`. The old bytecode's instructions have their `exn_tab_entry` remapped to point to code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions.\n\nWhen Dynamo traces the old bytecode:\n- It processes instructions with remapped exn_tab_entries\n- `update_block_stack` is called for each instruction\n\nBut `update_block_stack` only POPS, never PUSHES! Pushing happens in BEFORE_WITH.\n\nBut there IS no BEFORE_WITH in code1's old bytecode (since we start after them). So block_stack remains empty when tracing code1's old bytecode.\n\nHmm, but if block_stack is empty, then `should_compile_partial_graph` returns True (empty block_stack means all can_restore is vacuously true). And the second graph break would generate code2 with `setup_fn_target_offsets = ()`. No KeyError.\n\nOK so when would the KeyError happen? Let me think again...\n\nWhat if code1 starts RIGHT BEFORE the NESTED context manager but AFTER the OUTER one? Like:\n```\nwith outer_ctx:     # offset 10, BEFORE_WITH -> not in code1\n    # graph break 1 here -> code1 starts at next instruction\n    with inner_ctx:  # BEFORE_WITH at some offset -> IS in code1's old bytecode\n        ...\n        graph_break_2  # second graph break\n```\n\nThen code1:\n- prefix: sets up outer_ctx (1 hook)\n- old bytecode: starts right after graph_break_1, includes BEFORE_WITH for inner_ctx\n- When Dynamo traces code1's old bytecode, it processes BEFORE_WITH for inner_ctx\n- This pushes inner_ctx to block_stack with target = ???\n\nWhat target does BEFORE_WITH use? In Python 3.11+, from lines 3481-3483:\n```python\nassert self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None\ntarget = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\n\nThe `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` is an instruction object in code1's instruction list. If code1's old bytecode had its exn_tab_entry remapped (by old_hook_target_remap in lines 546-556), then `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` now points to code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nBut `old_hook_target_remap` only remaps entries that target `old_hook_target` - i.e., the OUTER context's exception table target. The INNER context's BEFORE_WITH instruction's exn_tab_entry was NOT remapped (it still points to the INNER context's PUSH_EXC_INFO in the ORIGINAL code)!\n\nSo `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` points to the ORIGINAL code's INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction at offset, say, O_INNER.\n\nThen `block_stack` has one entry for inner_ctx with `target.offset = O_INNER` (original code offset).\n\nWhen graph_break_2 triggers a second graph break:\n- `setup_fn_target_offsets = (O_INNER,)`\n- This is the ORIGINAL code's offset!\n\nIn `generate_based_on_original_code_object` with code = code1:\n- meta = metadata for code1\n- `remap_block_offsets` runs:\n  - prefix_blocks = [OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO] (only one, since meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap has one entry for outer)\n  - block_target_offset_remap[OUTER_offset] = outer_original_offset\n  - old_start_offset = OUTER_offset\n  - old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in (O_INNER,) if n > OUTER_offset)\n  \n  Now, O_INNER is an offset in the ORIGINAL code. OUTER_offset is an offset in code1 (prefix area). These are completely different code objects!\n  \n  If O_INNER > OUTER_offset (numerically), then it's included in old_inst_offsets, and the lookup tries to find instruction at offset O_INNER in code1's instructions. If such an instruction exists (unlikely since code1's offset space is different), we might find a wrong instruction. If not found, _filter_iter returns empty, and the mapping doesn't include O_INNER.\n  \n  Then: `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][O_INNER]` -> KeyError!\n\nAH HA! THAT'S the bug!\n\nThe issue is:\n1. code1 starts after entering outer_ctx but before entering inner_ctx\n2. code1's prefix sets up outer_ctx and remaps ONLY outer_ctx's exception table entries\n3. When code1's old bytecode includes BEFORE_WITH for inner_ctx, the BEFORE_WITH instruction's next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is NOT remapped (still points to original code's inner PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n4. When BEFORE_WITH pushes to block_stack, the target is the ORIGINAL code's inner PUSH_EXC_INFO offset\n5. When the second graph break occurs, setup_fn_target_offsets contains this original offset\n6. remap_block_offsets can't find this offset in code1's instructions\n7. KeyError!\n\nNow I understand the root cause. The fix should be to ALSO remap the exception table entries of BEFORE_WITH instructions within code1's old bytecode that target the INNER contexts.\n\nBut actually, the issue is more subtle. In code1, the old_hook_target_remap only remaps entries that target the OUTER context's PUSH_EXC_INFO. But the INNER context's PUSH_EXC_INFO is in the OLD BYTECODE (not in the prefix of code1). Its exception table target is the INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO in the ORIGINAL code.\n\nWait, but the INNER context is NOT being set up in code1's prefix! code1 only sets up outer_ctx. The INNER context is set up by BEFORE_WITH in code1's old bytecode. So the old_hook_target_remap CORRECTLY doesn't remap the inner context's targets, because the inner hook is in the old bytecode section.\n\nSo when remap_block_offsets is called:\n- It needs to map O_INNER (original inner context's PUSH_EXC_INFO offset) to the corresponding offset in meta.code (which IS the original code, so it's unchanged!)\n\nWait, meta.code IS the original code! So the mapping should be identity for original-code offsets that are already in the original code's address space.\n\nHmm, but that's not what the remap does. The remap is trying to go from code1's offset space to meta.code's offset space. O_INNER is already in meta.code's offset space. But remap_block_offsets treats it as code1's offset and tries to find the corresponding meta.instructions instruction.\n\nThe issue is: O_INNER is a VALID meta.code offset, but it's being treated as a code1 offset and looked up in code1's instruction list.\n\nWAIT. Actually, when `BEFORE_WITH` is executed in code1's old bytecode, `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` is an instruction from code1's instruction list. If the exn_tab_entry was NOT remapped, `target` is still from the original code... But that can't be, because when code1 was generated using `transform_code_object(code, update)`, the instructions are the ORIGINAL instructions deep-copied and modified. The `old_hook_target_remap` remaps the targets of SOME entries, but not the INNER context's entries.\n\nSo in code1's instruction list (after transform_code_object), the BEFORE_WITH instruction for the INNER context has `exn_tab_entry.target` = original code's INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object.\n\nBut wait, this instruction object is the ONE FROM meta.instructions (deep copy of original), not the actual original. When code1 is later disassembled (via transform_code_object(code1, remap_block_offsets)), the instructions are the compiled instructions of code1, which are newly created from the bytecode of code1. They would NOT have the same instruction objects as meta.instructions.\n\nHmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about it differently.\n\nWhen code1 is compiled (as a CodeType object), the exception table is computed from the instructions' exn_tab_entry fields. If inner_before_with's next_instruction has an exn_tab_entry.target pointing to the original INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO, then code1's compiled exception table has an entry for those instructions pointing to the INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO's COMPILED OFFSET in code1.\n\nThe INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO IS in code1 (it's in the old bytecode section). Its compiled offset in code1 is some value, say, E_INNER_code1.\n\nSo code1's exception table says: \"instructions in range [inner_before_with_range] have exception handler at E_INNER_code1\".\n\nWhen code1 is executed at runtime and BEFORE_WITH runs, it pushes to block_stack with target = instruction at E_INNER_code1 in code1's instruction objects. This instruction object HAS offset E_INNER_code1 (a code1 offset).\n\nWhen the second graph break occurs:\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (E_INNER_code1,)\n\nIn remap_block_offsets on code1:\n- prefix_blocks = [OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO at some offset in prefix]\n- old_start_offset = OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset\n- E_INNER_code1 > old_start_offset? It's in the old bytecode section, so yes!\n- old_inst_offsets = [E_INNER_code1]\n- _filter_iter finds the instruction at E_INNER_code1 in code1's instructions \u2713\n- zip with reversed meta.instructions finds the corresponding meta.instructions instruction\n- block_target_offset_remap[E_INNER_code1] = corresponding_meta_offset \u2713\n\nNo KeyError!\n\nSo E_INNER_code1 IS properly in the old bytecode section (> old_start_offset) and IS found.\n\nOK so now I'm confused again. When would the KeyError happen?\n\nLet me look at this from yet another angle. Maybe the issue is with MULTIPLE hops. Like a THIRD-level graph break.\n\nScenario:\n1. Original function has nested contexts: outer, inner1, inner2\n2. First graph break inside inner2 -> generates code1 (with 3 hooks in prefix: outer, inner1, inner2)\n3. code1 is traced, another graph break occurs -> generates code2\n\nFor code2 generation, `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains targets from code1's block_stack. But code1 starts AFTER BOTH context managers were set up (BEFORE_WITH is before the graph break point). So code1's old bytecode does NOT include any BEFORE_WITH. When code1 is traced by Dynamo, no BEFORE_WITH is processed, block_stack is empty.\n\nSo code2 is generated with empty setup_fn_target_offsets. No KeyError.\n\nBut what if the second graph break is in code1's prefix itself? That shouldn't happen (is_tracing_resume_prologue prevents it).\n\nHmm... Let me look at the test `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` more carefully:\n\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():         # outer\n        with torch.enable_grad():  # inner\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)     # graph break here (torch._dynamo.graph_break())\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nWhen tracing fn:\n1. BEFORE_WITH for torch.no_grad() -> block_stack: [{outer_ctx, target=O_OUTER}]\n2. BEFORE_WITH for torch.enable_grad() -> block_stack: [{outer_ctx, target=O_OUTER}, {inner_ctx, target=O_INNER}]\n3. `reversible(a)` which calls graph_break() -> CALL graph break\n\nFor the CALL graph break, `handle_graph_break` is called. The block_stack has two entries.\n\ncode1 is generated with:\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (O_OUTER, O_INNER) [offsets in original code]\n- Two hooks in prefix: hook for outer_ctx at stack_index corresponding to its position, hook for inner_ctx\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [inner_original, outer_original] (reversed after build)\n\nWhen code1 is traced by Dynamo, the old bytecode starts at the instruction after graph_break(). This is:\n```\nc = torch.sigmoid(b)  # LOAD_GLOBAL, etc.\nc.sum().backward()\nreturn x.grad\n```\n\nNo more graph breaks in this, assuming reversible() was the only graph break. So code1 doesn't generate code2. No issue.\n\nBut what if reversible() is called INSIDE A LOOP or there are MULTIPLE graph breaks? Let me think of a scenario that would fail.\n\nActually, maybe the scenario is:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            graph_break1()  # first graph break: still inside both contexts\n            graph_break2()  # second graph break: inside both contexts\n```\n\nFirst graph break:\n- block_stack has entries for outer and inner\n- code1 is generated (prefix with 2 hooks)\n\nWhen code1 is traced:\n- Prefix (prologue): sets up outer and inner contexts\n- Old bytecode: starts at graph_break2() call\n- graph_break2() causes second graph break\n\nFor the SECOND graph break in code1's tracing:\n- block_stack should have entries for outer and inner from the PREFIX setup\n\nBUT! The prefix doesn't use BEFORE_WITH. It uses hooks. So when Dynamo traces code1, the prefix is processed with `is_tracing_resume_prologue=True`.\n\nDuring the prologue tracing, what happens when Dynamo encounters the hook instructions (context manager setup code)?\n\nLooking at the BEFORE_WITH handler vs the prefix hook handler:\n- In the original function, BEFORE_WITH is an explicit instruction\n- In code1's prefix, the context is set up via LOAD_FAST + try_finally/with template\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1's PREFIX:\n- It processes the hook instructions\n- These are a sequence of LOAD_FAST, CALL_FUNCTION, etc., followed by the context manager setup\n- At some point, it hits BEFORE_WITH (if using `__call__` template which generates BEFORE_WITH from `with ctx:`)\n\nAH HA! The `__call__` method generates a `with ctx:` block, which in Python 3.11+ generates BEFORE_WITH. So when Dynamo traces code1's prefix and hits BEFORE_WITH, it processes it!\n\nAnd at that point, `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` would point to the PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's prefix (the one generated by the hook template).\n\nSo when tracing code1 and reaching BEFORE_WITH in the prefix, block_stack gets an entry with target = PREFIX_PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nBut BEFORE_WITH is being processed DURING THE PROLOGUE (is_tracing_resume_prologue=True). This is before the `JUMP to target`. After the jump, the prologue ends and the old bytecode is processed.\n\nIf BEFORE_WITH is processed during the prologue AND block_stack gets populated, then when the old bytecode's graph_break2() causes a second graph break, block_stack has entries with targets pointing to PREFIX PUSH_EXC_INFOs of code1.\n\nThen code2 is generated with:\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_INNER, P_OUTER) [code1's prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offsets]\n- These should be in remap via meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap\n\nLet me trace through:\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [inner_original, outer_original] (after reversing)\n- In remap_block_offsets on code1:\n  - Scan for PUSH_EXC_INFO in instructions\n  - prefix_blocks[0] = INNER_PUSH_EXC_INFO (lower offset, from inner_epilogue)\n  - prefix_blocks[1] = OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO (higher offset, from outer_epilogue)\n  - block_target_offset_remap[P_INNER] = inner_original\n  - block_target_offset_remap[P_OUTER] = outer_original\n  - old_start_offset = P_OUTER (OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO offset)\n  - setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_INNER, P_OUTER) or (P_OUTER, P_INNER)?\n\nActually, the block_stack ordering... In `BEFORE_WITH` processing during code1's prologue:\n- First BEFORE_WITH in prefix: outer ctx? inner ctx?\n\nLooking at the prefix structure:\n- Hooks are processed in ascending stack_index order (outer first, inner second)\n- The outer hook's `__call__` generates BEFORE_WITH for outer ctx\n- Then the inner hook's `__call__` generates BEFORE_WITH for inner ctx\n\nSo BEFORE_WITH for outer is processed BEFORE BEFORE_WITH for inner. block_stack: [{outer, P_OUTER}, {inner, P_INNER}].\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = (P_OUTER, P_INNER) [outer first, inner second].\n\nBut wait, what is P_OUTER vs P_INNER? Looking at the structure:\n- Outer hook generates setup_with (including BEFORE_WITH and try body setup) and epilogue (PUSH_EXC_INFO + cleanup)\n- Then inner hook generates its setup_with and epilogue\n- cleanup is: inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue (as analyzed above)\n- Instructions in prefix (after `prefix.extend(cleanup)`): ... [jump] [INNER_EPILOGUE] [OUTER_EPILOGUE]\n\nSo INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO comes BEFORE OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO in the instructions.\n\nP_INNER < P_OUTER.\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = (P_OUTER, P_INNER) [outer has higher offset, inner has lower offset].\n\nIn remap_block_offsets:\n- prefix_blocks[0] = INNER_PUSH_EXC_INFO (lower offset P_INNER)\n- prefix_blocks[1] = OUTER_PUSH_EXC_INFO (higher offset P_OUTER)\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_INNER] = inner_original \u2713\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_OUTER] = outer_original \u2713\n- old_start_offset = P_OUTER\n- old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in (P_OUTER, P_INNER) if n > P_OUTER) = [] (empty)\n  - P_OUTER is NOT > P_OUTER (it's ==)\n  - P_INNER < P_OUTER, so P_INNER is NOT > P_OUTER\n\nBoth are handled by prefix mapping, so both ARE in block_target_offset_remap!\n\nThen:\n- meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][P_OUTER] = outer_original \u2713\n- meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][P_INNER] = inner_original \u2713\n\nNo KeyError!\n\nOK so this scenario also works. I'm really struggling to find when the KeyError occurs.\n\nLet me try yet another scenario. What if the `BEFORE_WITH` during the prologue tracing doesn't use the remapped exn_tab_entry but the ORIGINAL one?\n\nWait, I said the hooks generate BEFORE_WITH. But do they? Looking at `__call__` which generates `with ctx:`. In Python 3.11, this generates BEFORE_WITH. The BEFORE_WITH instruction generated by the hook has an exn_tab_entry pointing to the hook's PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nWhen Dynamo processes this BEFORE_WITH:\n```python\ntarget = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\n\nThe `self.next_instruction` is the instruction after BEFORE_WITH in code1. Its `exn_tab_entry.target` is the hook's PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object.\n\nBut when code1 is compiled as a CodeType, the exn_tab_entry objects are NEW objects created by `virtualize_exception_table`. These are NOT the same objects as the ones in the prefix_blocks list.\n\nHmm, so when Dynamo traces code1 (the COMPILED code object), it virtualizes the exception table (via virtualize_exception_table). The resulting exn_tab_entry objects have `.target` pointing to instruction objects IN THE VIRTUALIZED (disassembled) code1 instruction list. These are NOT the same objects as any other instruction list.\n\nOK so when BEFORE_WITH is processed:\n```python\ntarget = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\n`target` is an instruction object from code1's VIRTUALIZED instruction list, with `target.offset` = the compiled offset of the PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1.\n\nSo `block_stack[-1].target.offset` = PUSH_EXC_INFO's compiled offset in code1.\n\nNow, is this the same as P_INNER or P_OUTER that I defined? Let's say:\n- Inner PUSH_EXC_INFO is at compiled offset 50 in code1\n- Outer PUSH_EXC_INFO is at compiled offset 60 in code1\n- P_INNER = 50, P_OUTER = 60\n\nWhen `remap_block_offsets` is called via `transform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)`, it again virtualizes code1's bytecode. The resulting instructions have the SAME compiled offsets (50 and 60). The PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions would be found at offsets 50 and 60.\n\nSo prefix_blocks[0] (first PUSH_EXC_INFO found) has offset 50 = P_INNER.\nprefix_blocks[1] (second PUSH_EXC_INFO found) has offset 60 = P_OUTER.\n\nAnd block_target_offset_remap is built correctly.\n\nBUT: when the SECOND graph break occurs in code1 (in old bytecode), the block_stack was populated during the PROLOGUE tracing. During prologue tracing, Dynamo traced the FIRST code1 run, not a second transform. The instruction objects from the first transform are DIFFERENT from the instruction objects in remap_block_offsets' transform.\n\nBut the OFFSETS should be the SAME (both from compiled code1). So `block_stack[-1].target.offset` = 50 (P_INNER's offset), and remap_block_offsets finds PUSH_EXC_INFO at offset 50 in its own transform.\n\nEverything seems consistent! No KeyError.\n\nI must be missing something fundamental. Let me try to actually run one of these test cases or look for related test failures.\n\nActually, let me look at `_bytecode_from_template_with_split` to understand exactly what instructions are generated and where PUSH_EXC_INFO ends up.\n"
