"Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /workspace/torch/_dynamo/resume_execution.py:\n    64\tdef _bytecode_from_template_with_split(\n    65\t    template: Callable[..., Any],\n    66\t    stack_index: int,\n    67\t    varname_map: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,\n    68\t) -> tuple[list[Instruction], list[Instruction]]:\n    69\t    template_code = bytecode_from_template(template, varname_map=varname_map)\n    70\t    template_code.append(create_instruction(\"POP_TOP\"))\n    71\t\n    72\t    # adjust exception table entry depth\n    73\t    for inst in template_code:\n    74\t        if inst.exn_tab_entry:\n    75\t            inst.exn_tab_entry.depth += stack_index\n    76\t\n    77\t    # search for LOAD_FAST dummy and replace it with 2 NOPs (we can break up the bytecode between them)\n    78\t    dummy_idx, dummy_inst = next(\n    79\t        (\n    80\t            (i, inst)\n    81\t            for i, inst in enumerate(template_code)\n    82\t            if inst.opname == \"LOAD_FAST\" and inst.argval == \"dummy\"\n    83\t        ),\n    84\t        (None, None),\n    85\t    )\n    86\t    assert dummy_idx is not None and dummy_inst is not None\n    87\t\n    88\t    # replace LOAD_FAST dummy with first NOP marking exception area\n    89\t    overwrite_instruction(dummy_inst, [create_instruction(\"NOP\")])\n    90\t\n    91\t    # POP_TOP follows LOAD_FAST dummy - replace with NOP marking end of exception area\n    92\t    assert template_code[dummy_idx + 1].opname == \"POP_TOP\"\n    93\t    overwrite_instruction(template_code[dummy_idx + 1], [create_instruction(\"NOP\")])\n    94\t\n    95\t    return template_code[: dummy_idx + 1], template_code[dummy_idx + 1 :]\n    96\t\n    97\t\n    98\tdef _try_except_tf_mode_template(dummy: Any, stack_var_name: Any) -> None:\n    99\t    # NOTE: Make sure this name matches what is generated by symbolic_convert:import_source\n   100\t    # on torch._dynamo.utils.\n   101\t    global __import_torch_dot__dynamo_dot_utils\n   102\t    try:\n   103\t        dummy\n   104\t    except:  # noqa: E722, B001\n   105\t        __import_torch_dot__dynamo_dot_utils.set_torch_function_mode_stack(  # type: ignore[name-defined]\n   106\t            stack_var_name\n   107\t        )\n   108\t        raise\n   109\t\n   110\t\n   111\t@dataclasses.dataclass(frozen=True)\n   112\tclass ReenterWith:\n"

{
  "thought": "Looking at `_bytecode_from_template_with_split`:\n\nIt generates bytecode from a template function, splits at the `dummy` instruction:\n- `setup_with` (or `setup_try_finally`) = everything up to (and including) the NOP replacing `dummy`\n- `epilogue` = everything after (starting with the NOP replacing `POP_TOP dummy`)\n\nFor `__call__` with template:\n```python\ndef _template(ctx, dummy):\n    with ctx:\n        dummy\n```\n\nThis generates (in Python 3.11+):\n```\nBEFORE_WITH       <- part of setup_with\nNOP               <- was LOAD_FAST dummy, marks start of exception area\nNOP               <- was POP_TOP, marks end of exception area\nWITH_EXCEPT_START <- or similar exit handling\n...\nPUSH_EXC_INFO     <- in epilogue\n...\n```\n\nActually, I need to look at what Python 3.11+ generates for `with ctx: dummy`. Let me think about this more carefully.\n\nIn Python 3.11+, `with ctx: dummy` generates:\n```\nLOAD_FAST ctx       (load ctx)\nBEFORE_WITH         (enter ctx, push exit func and result)\nPOP_TOP             (pop result, or keep it if used)\n                    <- start of exception handling region\nLOAD_FAST dummy     <- our marker\nPOP_TOP\n                    <- end of exception handling region\n\n(exception handling code)\nPUSH_EXC_INFO\n...\nWITH_EXCEPT_START\n...\n```\n\nActually in Python 3.11+, the exception table entries tell the interpreter where to jump if an exception occurs in the try body. The exception handler for the `with` block is the PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction.\n\nSo for `with ctx: dummy`:\n- Instructions in the try body (between the NOP markers) have exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- The PUSH_EXC_INFO is in the epilogue\n\nSo `setup_with` = [LOAD_FAST, BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy)]\nAnd `epilogue` = [NOP(was POP_TOP), PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n\nWait, but where is the exception table entry? The NOP in `setup_with` marks the START of the exception area. The NOP at the start of `epilogue` marks the END. So instructions between these NOPs have exn_tab_entries pointing to the PUSH_EXC_INFO in the epilogue.\n\nBut the setup_with NOPs are at the end and beginning of these splits. The actual instructions WITH exn_tab_entries (pointing to PUSH_EXC_INFO) would be the instructions in the OLD BYTECODE section, which are placed between the two NOPs.\n\nHmm, but wait. After the split, `setup_with` is prepended to the prefix and the old bytecode comes after. The `epilogue` is added to `cleanup`. So the structure in the final code is:\n\n```\n[LOAD_FAST ctx]  <- from setup_with\n[BEFORE_WITH]    <- from setup_with\n[POP_TOP]        <- from setup_with\n[NOP]            <- marks start of exception area, from setup_with\n[LOAD_FAST and STORE_FAST for ctx vars]  <- more prefix setup\n[JUMP to target]\n[OLD BYTECODE]   <- the actual code to resume in\n...\n[NOP]            <- marks end of exception area, from epilogue start\n[PUSH_EXC_INFO]  <- exception handler target\n[cleanup code]\n```\n\nThe exception table for the compiled code would cover the range from the first NOP to the second NOP, with handler at PUSH_EXC_INFO. But wait, the PUSH_EXC_INFO is in the CLEANUP section which comes AFTER the JUMP. The old bytecode comes BEFORE the PUSH_EXC_INFO... so there must be a way the exception table handles this.\n\nActually, in Python 3.11+, exception table entries can span across jumps. The exception table says \"if an exception occurs at bytecode offset X (where X is between start and end), jump to target\". The JUMP instruction in the prefix would NOT be in the exception handling range (it's between the NOPs, but the jump goes to the target which is AFTER the epilogue).\n\nHmm wait, this doesn't make sense. Let me re-read the code flow:\n\n1. prefix = [setup_with[1]] for outer hook (outer's BEFORE_WITH, etc.)\n2. prefix += [setup_with[2]] for inner hook (inner's BEFORE_WITH, etc.)\n3. prefix += [load stack vars]\n4. prefix += [jump to target]\n5. prefix += cleanup = [inner_epilogue, outer_epilogue]\n6. prefix += unreachable_codes\n7. instructions[:] = prefix + instructions\n\nSo the final instruction order in code1 is:\n```\n[outer setup_with up to NOP]  (includes outer BEFORE_WITH)\n[inner setup_with up to NOP]  (includes inner BEFORE_WITH)  \n[load stack vars / prefix setup]\nJUMP to target\n[inner NOP (end of inner exception area)]\n[inner PUSH_EXC_INFO]\n[inner cleanup code]\n[outer NOP (end of outer exception area)]\n[outer PUSH_EXC_INFO]\n[outer cleanup code]\n[unreachable]\n[OLD BYTECODE starting at 'target']\n```\n\nWait, that's strange. The old bytecode comes AFTER the unreachable code. But that would mean the offset of the old bytecode instructions is AFTER the cleanup code.\n\nActually, this makes sense from a bytecode perspective. The exception table says \"if an exception occurs in [outer NOP to outer cleanup end], jump to outer PUSH_EXC_INFO\". The old bytecode instructions are in this range! So if an exception occurs in the old bytecode, it jumps to the appropriate PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nNow, for the NESTED case:\n- Outer PUSH_EXC_INFO is after inner PUSH_EXC_INFO (inner_epilogue comes before outer_epilogue in cleanup)\n- Inner PUSH_EXC_INFO has a LOWER offset than outer PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nAnd old bytecode has exn_tab_entries:\n- If the old bytecode is INSIDE the inner context manager: exn_tab_entry.target -> inner PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- If the old bytecode is OUTSIDE inner but inside outer: exn_tab_entry.target -> outer PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nBUT, this remapping happens in the original old_hook_target_remap:\n- old_hook_target_remap[inner_original_PUSH_EXC_INFO] = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's prefix\n- old_hook_target_remap[outer_original_PUSH_EXC_INFO] = outer PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's prefix\n\nSo the old bytecode instructions' exn_tab_entry.target are remapped from original to code1's prefix.\n\nBUT WAIT - what if the old bytecode (starting at target) is ONLY in the INNER context manager (i.e., the graph break happened inside both contexts)? Then all old bytecode instructions have exn_tab_entry.target remapped to inner PUSH_EXC_INFO (which is now at a PREFIX offset).\n\nBut looking at the code structure: inner PUSH_EXC_INFO is BEFORE the old bytecode (it's in the cleanup/epilogue section). And the old bytecode is AFTER the cleanup. So the old bytecode's exception handler (inner PUSH_EXC_INFO) is at a LOWER offset than the old bytecode itself!\n\nThis is fine for Python's exception table mechanism - exception table entries don't require the handler to be at a higher offset.\n\nNow, when `remap_block_offsets` runs and processes instructions in ORDER:\n- It scans for PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions BEFORE `len(prefix_block_target_offset_remap)` limit\n- prefix_blocks[0] = INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO (lower offset)\n- prefix_blocks[1] = OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO (higher offset)\n- old_start_offset = OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO offset\n\nThe old bytecode is AFTER outer PUSH_EXC_INFO (in terms of layout in code1). So old bytecode instructions have offsets > OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO offset, i.e., > old_start_offset. \u2713\n\nNow, when Dynamo traces code1 and BEFORE_WITH is processed (in the prefix):\n- OUTER BEFORE_WITH: \n  ```python\n  target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target  # = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO?\n  ```\n  \n  Wait, what is the exception table entry for the instruction after OUTER BEFORE_WITH in code1? It should be... the inner_NOP instruction (from inner_epilogue)? Or the inner PUSH_EXC_INFO?\n\nHmm, the exception table entry for instructions in the outer context's try body points to OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO. But for instructions in the inner context's try body, it points to INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nBut we computed exn_tab_entry from the template. In the outer template:\n- setup_with (outer): [LOAD_FAST, BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(start)]\n- epilogue (outer): [NOP(end), PUSH_EXC_INFO, WITH_EXCEPT_START, ...]\n\nThe exception table from the outer template covers instructions with offsets between [NOP(start), NOP(end)]. Or inner PUSH_EXC_INFO?\n\nActually, the exception table entry for the instruction immediately after BEFORE_WITH should be the one for the outer context, pointing to the outer PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nBut wait, `_bytecode_from_template_with_split` generates a NOP at the split point (replacing LOAD_FAST dummy). The exception table entry starts at this NOP. So the NEXT instruction after BEFORE_WITH would be the INNER hook's LOAD_FAST ctx (or the start of inner hook setup), and this instruction's exn_tab_entry should be for the OUTER context.\n\nHmm, but actually the NOP at position `dummy_idx` (the first NOP) is the START of the exception area. Then inner hook's setup_with comes next, then LOAD_FAST/STORE_FAST for stack vars, then JUMP, then inner_epilogue, then outer_epilogue, then old bytecode.\n\nThe exception table says: all instructions from outer_NOP_start to outer_NOP_end (including everything in between) have exception handler at outer PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nSimilarly, for the inner hook, all instructions from inner_NOP_start to inner_NOP_end have exception handler at inner PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nSince exception table entries can be NESTED (inner overrides outer for instructions in both ranges), the instructions that are in BOTH ranges have exception handler at inner PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nNow, when Dynamo processes OUTER BEFORE_WITH:\n- `next_instruction` is the instruction after BEFORE_WITH, which has exn_tab_entry for OUTER context (pointing to outer PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n\nWait, or is it for the INNER context? \n\nThe outer exception range starts at outer_NOP_start. The BEFORE_WITH itself might or might not have an exn_tab_entry.\n\nActually, in the template:\n```python\ndef _template(ctx, dummy):\n    with ctx:\n        dummy\n```\n\nGenerates (Python 3.11+):\n- LOAD_FAST ctx          <- no exn_tab_entry (before the try block)\n- BEFORE_WITH            <- maybe has exn_tab_entry?\n- POP_TOP                <- maybe has exn_tab_entry?\n- LOAD_FAST dummy        <- has exn_tab_entry (in try body)\n- POP_TOP                <- has exn_tab_entry? \n- LOAD_CONST None        <- for exit\n- ...\n\nActually, in Python 3.11+, the exception table typically covers the body of the with block. Then line 3482: `assert self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None` would FAIL!\n\nUnless... the outer BEFORE_WITH pushes the NOP(start) onto the \"next instruction\" check? No, that's not how Python works.\n\nOK, I'm getting confused. Let me look at what Python 3.11 actually generates for a `with` block, by looking at how Dynamo handles it.\n\nActually, let me look at line 3476-3483 more carefully:\n\n```python\nif inst.exn_tab_entry and (\n    not self.block_stack\n    or inst.exn_tab_entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n):\n    target = None\nelse:\n    assert self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None\n    target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\n\nThe condition is: `inst.exn_tab_entry and (not self.block_stack or inst.exn_tab_entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target)`.\n\nIf `inst.exn_tab_entry` is None (BEFORE_WITH is not in any exception table range), then `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`.\n\nBut line 3482 asserts `self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None`. So the NEXT instruction after BEFORE_WITH MUST have an exn_tab_entry.\n\nIn the ACTUAL code from the original function, BEFORE_WITH is inside a `with` block (unless it's the outermost `with`). But the OUTERMOST BEFORE_WITH might not have an exn_tab_entry itself (it's at the start), and the NEXT instruction should be in the exception table range.\n\nOK let me stop guessing and actually look at what Python generates. Let me look at a concrete example.\n"

{
  "thought": "Now I have concrete data. Looking at the exception table:\n\n```\n_ExceptionTableEntry(start=52, end=104, target=178, depth=1, lasti=True)\n_ExceptionTableEntry(start=104, end=110, target=144, depth=2, lasti=True)\n_ExceptionTableEntry(start=110, end=126, target=178, depth=1, lasti=True)\n```\n\n- BEFORE_WITH is at offset 50 (outer)\n- L1 (POP_TOP) is at offset 52\n- BEFORE_WITH (inner) is at offset 102\n- L2 (POP_TOP) is at offset 104\n\nSo:\n- Outer BEFORE_WITH is at 50, its next_instruction (POP_TOP) is at 52\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH is at 102, its next_instruction (POP_TOP) is at 104\n\nException table entries:\n- `start=52, end=104, target=178`: L9 = PUSH_EXC_INFO for OUTER context is at offset 178\n  - So instructions from 52 to 104 have exception handler at OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO (178)\n  - POP_TOP at 52 (outer next_instruction) has exn_tab_entry.target = 178 (outer PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n  \n- `start=104, end=110, target=144`: L5 = PUSH_EXC_INFO for INNER context is at offset 144\n  - Instructions from 104 to 110 have exception handler at INNER PUSH_EXC_INFO (144)\n  - POP_TOP at 104 (inner next_instruction) has exn_tab_entry.target = 144 (inner PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n\n- `start=110, end=126, target=178`: After inner block body (STORE_FAST x at 108), back to outer exception handler\n  - Actually wait, STORE_FAST x is at 108, and inner body ends at 108. Then the exit code starts at 110.\n\nSo the exception table says:\n- Outer BEFORE_WITH's next instruction (52) has target=178 (outer PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH's next instruction (104) has target=144 (inner PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n\nWhen Dynamo processes outer BEFORE_WITH:\n- `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` (instruction at 52's exn_tab_entry.target)\n- This is the outer PUSH_EXC_INFO at 178\n- Block stack: [{outer_ctx, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO(178)}]\n\nWhen Dynamo processes inner BEFORE_WITH:\n- `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` (instruction at 104's exn_tab_entry.target)\n- This is the inner PUSH_EXC_INFO at 144\n- Block stack: [{outer_ctx, target=178}, {inner_ctx, target=144}]\n\nThis makes sense!\n\nNow, in the original code, PUSH_EXC_INFO_OUTER is at 178 and PUSH_EXC_INFO_INNER is at 144.\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets (for the first graph break) = (178, 144).\n\nWhen generating code1 (first resume), the two hooks are:\n- hook for outer_ctx (stack_index = some value, let's say 1, since it's below inner on stack at graph break point)\n- hook for inner_ctx (stack_index = some other value, let's say 2)\n\nActually, the stack_index is based on where the context manager's return value is on the stack when the graph break happens. outer_ctx enters first and its stack is lower; inner_ctx enters second and its stack is higher.\n\nHmm wait, I need to look at what the stack looks like at the graph break point. `b.stack_index` from `BlockStackEntry` is `len(self.stack)` at the time of BEFORE_WITH processing. \n\nFor outer BEFORE_WITH: stack before BEFORE_WITH has the context manager object. After BEFORE_WITH (and POP_TOP), the stack has the exit function at some position. But `stack_index` in BlockStackEntry = `len(self.stack)` at the time of BEFORE_WITH call. So:\n- outer: stack_index = stack_len when outer BEFORE_WITH was processed\n- inner: stack_index = stack_len when inner BEFORE_WITH was processed\n\nLooking at line 3491-3492:\n```python\nself.block_stack.append(\n    BlockStackEntry(inst, target, len(self.stack), ctx)\n)\n```\n\n`len(self.stack)` at the time of BEFORE_WITH processing. After BEFORE_WITH has been processed but before POP_TOP:\n- outer BEFORE_WITH: stack might have [outer_exit_fn, outer_enter_result], so len = 2\n  Wait, BEFORE_WITH pushes exit fn + enter result. After `self.push(exit)` and `self.push(ctx.enter(self))` (lines 3487, 3497), stack has exit_fn at index ... and enter result at top.\n\nActually, looking at lines 3487, 3497:\n```python\nself.push(exit)   # push exit function\n...\nself.push(ctx.enter(self))  # push enter result\n```\n\nSo after BEFORE_WITH, stack has [exit, enter_result]. `len(self.stack)` at the time of `BlockStackEntry(inst, target, len(self.stack), ctx)` - this is BEFORE pushing exit and enter_result?\n\nLet me re-read:\n```python\nexit = WithExitFunctionVariable(ctx, inst.target)\n...\nself.push(exit)  # line 3487\nif target:\n    if ...:\n        self.block_stack.append(BlockStackEntry(inst, target, len(self.stack), ctx))\n...\nself.push(ctx.enter(self))  # line 3497\n```\n\nSo `len(self.stack)` when BlockStackEntry is created is AFTER pushing exit but BEFORE pushing enter_result. So stack_index = current stack length after exit is pushed.\n\nFor the graph break, let's say the stack state is:\n- Before outer BEFORE_WITH: stack = [... some values]\n- After outer BEFORE_WITH: stack = [... outer_exit_fn, outer_enter_result]\n- stack_index for outer = len after outer_exit_fn is pushed = len(stack before outer_BW) + 1\n- Then inner BEFORE_WITH: stack = [... outer_exit_fn, outer_enter_result, inner_exit_fn, inner_enter_result]\n- stack_index for inner = len after inner_exit_fn is pushed = len(stack after outer_BW) + 1\n\nAt the graph break (inside both with blocks), the stack has outer_exit_fn, outer_enter_result, inner_exit_fn, inner_enter_result. The stack_index for outer and inner are different.\n\nOK this is getting very deep. Let me just look at the actual bug differently.\n\nThe `setup_fn_target_offsets` for the first resume function generation = the offsets of block_stack targets in the ORIGINAL code. These are O_OUTER = 178 and O_INNER = 144 (from the original function example).\n\nNow, for the FIRST resume function (code1):\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap is built by processing hooks in ascending stack_index order\n- Let's say outer hook has stack_index < inner hook's stack_index\n- The processing order is: outer hook first, inner hook second\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O_OUTER, O_INNER] before reversing\n- After reversing: [O_INNER, O_OUTER]\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets` on code1:\n- cleanup = inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue (inner is prepended first, so inner comes first)\n- inner PUSH_EXC_INFO comes before outer PUSH_EXC_INFO in the prefix\n- prefix_blocks = [inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO] (in order of scan)\n- zip([inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO], [O_INNER, O_OUTER]):\n  - inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO -> O_INNER\n  - outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO -> O_OUTER\n\nThis is CORRECT! The reversed list aligns correctly.\n\nNow, when code1 is traced by Dynamo and a SECOND graph break occurs in the old bytecode of code1, what are the block_stack targets?\n\nDuring the prologue of code1's tracing, BEFORE_WITH instructions ARE processed (because they're in the prefix code generated by hooks). The outer BEFORE_WITH in the prefix has `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1 (from the inner epilogue). Wait no, let me check.\n\nIn code1's prefix:\n- outer hook's setup_with: [LOAD_FAST outer_ctx, BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(start outer)]\n- inner hook's setup_with: [LOAD_FAST inner_ctx, BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(start inner)]\n- ... (stack loading)\n- JUMP to target\n- [inner_epilogue = inner NOP(end), inner PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n- [outer_epilogue = outer NOP(end), outer PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n\nThe OUTER BEFORE_WITH's next_instruction is POP_TOP. What exception table entry does POP_TOP have in code1?\n\nPOP_TOP is between outer_NOP(start) (at the end of outer setup_with) and inner_BEFORE_WITH. The outer exception area covers from outer_NOP(start) to outer_NOP(end). POP_TOP is NOT yet in the outer exception area (it comes before outer_NOP(start)).\n\nWait, outer_NOP(start) comes AFTER POP_TOP in setup_with:\n- outer setup_with = [LOAD_FAST outer_ctx, BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(start outer)]\n\nSo POP_TOP at index 2, NOP(start outer) at index 3. The exception area starts at NOP(start outer). POP_TOP is BEFORE the exception area starts. So POP_TOP does NOT have an exn_tab_entry!\n\nBut then line 3482: `assert self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None` would FAIL for the outer BEFORE_WITH in code1's prefix!\n\nUnless Dynamo doesn't process the outer BEFORE_WITH in the prefix? Maybe the BEFORE_WITH instructions in the prefix are NOPs to Dynamo?\n\nActually, when Dynamo traces code1 with `is_tracing_resume_prologue=True`, does it handle BEFORE_WITH differently?\n\nLet me look at what happens when `is_tracing_resume_prologue` is True.\n"

{
  "command": "python3 << 'EOF'\nimport dis, sys\n\nsys.path.insert(0, '/workspace')\nfrom torch._dynamo.resume_execution import _bytecode_from_template_with_split\nfrom contextlib import AbstractContextManager\n\ndef _template(ctx: AbstractContextManager, dummy) -> None:\n    with ctx:\n        dummy\n\nprint(\"Template instructions:\")\nfrom dis import _parse_exception_table, Bytecode, findlinestarts\nbytecode = Bytecode(_template)\nfor instr in bytecode:\n    print(f\"  {instr.offset:3d} {instr.opname:30s} {str(instr.argrepr):20s}\")\n\nprint()\nprint(\"Exception table:\")\nfor e in _parse_exception_table(_template.__code__):\n    print(f\"  {e}\")\n\nprint()\nsetup_with, epilogue = _bytecode_from_template_with_split(_template, stack_index=1)\nprint(\"setup_with:\")\nfor inst in setup_with:\n    exn = f\" exn->{inst.exn_tab_entry.target.offset}\" if inst.exn_tab_entry else \"\"\n    print(f\"  {inst.opname:30s}{exn}\")\nprint(\"epilogue:\")\nfor inst in epilogue:\n    exn = f\" exn->{inst.exn_tab_entry.target.offset}\" if inst.exn_tab_entry else \"\"\n    print(f\"  {inst.opname:30s}{exn}\")\nEOF\n",
  "security_risk": "LOW"

{
  "thought": "The test `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` has:\n- `torch.no_grad()` (outer)\n- `torch.enable_grad()` (inner)  \n- `reversible(a)` calls `torch._dynamo.graph_break()` which breaks the graph\n\nThis causes:\n1. First graph break inside both contexts\n2. Resume function code1 is generated with both contexts as prefix hooks\n3. code1's old bytecode starts at `b = reversible(a)` (the part that resumes after the graph break)\n4. code1 executes, and when Dynamo traces it, there might be a second graph break\n\nActually, `reversible(a)` itself calls `graph_break()`. When tracing the original function, Dynamo hits `graph_break()` inside `reversible(a)` when trying to inline `reversible`. It tries to inline reversible, but `graph_break()` inside it causes a problem.\n\nActually, let me think about what Dynamo does when it encounters `reversible(a)`:\n1. It tries to inline `reversible`\n2. Inside `reversible`, it hits `graph_break()` \n3. This causes a graph break during inlining\n4. The inline fails, so Dynamo falls back to treating `reversible(a)` as an opaque call\n5. Actually, with `graph_break()`, Dynamo typically breaks the graph AT the call site\n\nWhen the graph break occurs:\n- We're inside `fn` at the `b = reversible(a)` CALL instruction\n- Block stack has [outer_no_grad_entry, inner_enable_grad_entry]\n- A resume function code1 is generated to run from after `b = reversible(a)` with the two context managers as prefix hooks\n\ncode1 is generated. Now, when Dynamo TRACES code1:\n- Prologue: sets up outer (no_grad) and inner (enable_grad) contexts\n- old bytecode: `c = torch.sigmoid(b)` then `c.sum().backward()` then `return x.grad`\n\nIs there a second graph break in code1's old bytecode? `c.sum().backward()` might cause a graph break. Or maybe not.\n\nActually, let me think more carefully. When code1 is traced:\n- The prologue sets up the contexts\n- `torch.sigmoid(b)` - this is a normal torch op, should trace fine\n- `c.sum().backward()` - backward() is typically not supported in compile mode and causes a graph break!\n\nSo yes, there IS a second graph break in code1's old bytecode at `c.sum().backward()`.\n\nNow, when code1's old bytecode is traced and the second graph break (at backward()) occurs:\n- `block_stack` has entries from the PROLOGUE processing\n- During the prologue, `BEFORE_WITH` instructions are processed in the hooks\n- These push entries to block_stack with targets pointing to the PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1's prefix\n\nAnd then when `generate_based_on_original_code_object` is called for code1 with setup_fn_target_offsets = (targets from code1), we need to remap these to the original code's offsets.\n\nThe question is: which offsets are in setup_fn_target_offsets?\n\nLet me trace through more carefully:\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1's BEFORE_WITH for outer (no_grad):\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` = None (outer BEFORE_WITH in code1 is NOT in exception table range, as computed above)\n- Goes to else branch (Case 1)\n- `self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO (from the POP_TOP which is in the outer exception range)\n\nWait, but I said POP_TOP after outer BEFORE_WITH has exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO in the template. But does this survive the compilation of code1?\n\nThe issue is that in code1 (the compiled code object), the exception table is recomputed from all the instruction exn_tab_entries. The outer POP_TOP (index 2 in outer setup_with) has exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object. When code1 is compiled, the exception table says \"instructions from outer POP_TOP onwards (within the range) have exception handler at outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO's offset\".\n\nBut actually, when code1 is compiled by `transform_code_object`, the exception table is computed by `compute_exception_table`. This function looks at each instruction's `exn_tab_entry` and builds the exception table. If outer POP_TOP has exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, then the compiled exception table would cover that range.\n\nWhen code1 is subsequently TRACED by Dynamo (via `virtualize_exception_table`), the compiled exception table is re-virtualized. The virtualized exn_tab_entry objects have `.target` pointing to instruction objects in code1's virtualized instruction list.\n\nIf the compiled exception table says \"start=X, end=Y, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO_offset\", then after virtualization, the instructions from X to Y have `exn_tab_entry.target` = instruction object at outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO_offset.\n\nSo when Dynamo processes outer BEFORE_WITH in code1's tracing:\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` = None (BEFORE_WITH is NOT covered, since it's at offset before the exception range start)\n- Actually wait: the outer POP_TOP starts the exception range. BEFORE_WITH is at a lower offset than POP_TOP. So BEFORE_WITH is NOT in the exception range, and `inst.exn_tab_entry = None`. \u2713\n\n- next_instruction = POP_TOP (at the start of the exception range). Does POP_TOP have exn_tab_entry after compilation?\n\nThis is the key question. When code1 is compiled from its instruction objects, the exception table is computed from the instruction objects' exn_tab_entries. The outer POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object. When compiled and then re-virtualized, the POP_TOP instruction in the virtualized code1 SHOULD have exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object.\n\nSo `self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO \u2713\n`target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO`\n\nblock_stack gets: [{outer_ctx, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, stack_index=len(stack)}]\n\nThen inner BEFORE_WITH in code1 is processed:\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH is in the outer exception range (since outer exception range starts at outer POP_TOP and extends past the inner BEFORE_WITH).\n\nActually wait - the outer exception range in code1 starts at outer POP_TOP. Inner BEFORE_WITH comes after outer NOP(start) (the last instruction of outer setup_with). Actually:\n\nouter setup_with = [NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP, NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy)]\n\nThe LAST instruction is NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy). In the compiled code, the exception range starts at NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy).\n\nOh wait! Let me re-read. The exception table covers instructions from LOAD_FAST dummy to POP_TOP (after dummy). In the TEMPLATE, LOAD_FAST dummy is the FIRST instruction in the exception range. After the split, NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy) is the LAST instruction in setup_with.\n\nSo the exception range starts at NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy), not at POP_TOP.\n\nBut POP_TOP (the instruction right after BEFORE_WITH, which is part of setup_with and is index 2) is NOT in the exception range! It comes BEFORE NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy) (which is index 3).\n\nAnd NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy) HAS exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nSo when Dynamo processes outer BEFORE_WITH:\n- inst.exn_tab_entry = None (BEFORE_WITH is at index 1, before exception range start)\n- self.next_instruction = POP_TOP (at index 2) - does NOT have exn_tab_entry\n- ASSERT FAILS!\n\nBUT WAIT. Does `_bytecode_from_template_with_split` add `template_code.append(create_instruction(\"POP_TOP\"))`? Let me re-read:\n\n```python\ndef _bytecode_from_template_with_split(template, stack_index, varname_map=None):\n    template_code = bytecode_from_template(template, varname_map=varname_map)\n    template_code.append(create_instruction(\"POP_TOP\"))  # <-- adds POP_TOP at the END\n    \n    # adjust exception table entry depth\n    for inst in template_code:\n        if inst.exn_tab_entry:\n            inst.exn_tab_entry.depth += stack_index\n    \n    # search for LOAD_FAST dummy\n    dummy_idx, dummy_inst = next(...)\n    \n    # replace LOAD_FAST dummy with NOP\n    overwrite_instruction(dummy_inst, [create_instruction(\"NOP\")])\n    \n    # POP_TOP FOLLOWS LOAD_FAST dummy - replace with NOP\n    assert template_code[dummy_idx + 1].opname == \"POP_TOP\"\n    overwrite_instruction(template_code[dummy_idx + 1], [create_instruction(\"NOP\")])\n    \n    return template_code[:dummy_idx + 1], template_code[dummy_idx + 1:]\n```\n\n\"POP_TOP follows LOAD_FAST dummy\" - this is the POP_TOP that comes immediately after LOAD_FAST dummy in the template, at offset 10. This is the POP_TOP that discards the value of `dummy`. This POP_TOP HAS an exn_tab_entry (it's in the exception range: start=6, end=12).\n\nSo template_code:\n- index 0: RESUME@0\n- index 1: LOAD_FAST ctx@2 (will be NOPed)\n- index 2: BEFORE_WITH@4\n- index 3: POP_TOP@6 (exn: target=PUSH_EXC_INFO@30) <- part of `with ctx:` body start \n- index 4: LOAD_FAST dummy@8 (exn: target=PUSH_EXC_INFO@30) <- will become NOP\n- index 5: POP_TOP@10 (exn: target=PUSH_EXC_INFO@30) <- will become NOP (after dummy)\n- index 6: LOAD_CONST None@12\n- ...\n- index ?: PUSH_EXC_INFO@30\n\n`dummy_idx = 4` (LOAD_FAST dummy is at index 4)\n\nAfter replacements:\n- template_code[4] = NOP (was LOAD_FAST dummy, HAS exn_tab_entry)\n- template_code[5] = NOP (was POP_TOP, HAS exn_tab_entry)\n\n`return template_code[:5], template_code[5:]`\n\nsetup_with = [RESUME, NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@PUSH_EXC_INFO), NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy, exn@PUSH_EXC_INFO)]\nepilogue = [NOP(was POP_TOP, exn@PUSH_EXC_INFO), LOAD_CONST None, ..., PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n\nWAIT! POP_TOP at index 3 HAS exn_tab_entry! It's the POP_TOP after `with ctx:` begins (discarding __enter__'s return value).\n\nSo after overwriting LOAD_FAST ctx to NOP:\nsetup_with = [NOP(was RESUME), NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@PUSH_EXC_INFO), NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy, exn@PUSH_EXC_INFO)]\n\nWait, does RESUME get included? Let me check `bytecode_from_template` - it probably strips RESUME and creates its own start. Actually I don't have access to import it easily. But looking at the structure:\n\nsetup_with has 5 instructions (indices 0-4). The LAST TWO have exn_tab_entry.\n\nNow, setup_with ends with NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy) - this is the START of the exception range. And POP_TOP at index 3 has exn_tab_entry.\n\nIn code1's prefix:\n- outer setup_with: [..., NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO), NOP(exn@outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO)]\n- inner setup_with: [..., NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO), NOP(exn@inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO)]\n\nNow the exception table in code1:\n- POP_TOP at index 3 of outer setup_with has exn->outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- NOP at index 4 of outer setup_with has exn->outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- inner setup_with[3] (POP_TOP) has exn->inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- etc.\n\nBut in the COMPILED code1, the exception table covers RANGES. When computing the exception table from all these exn_tab_entries, the outer exception range covers outer_POP_TOP, outer_NOP(start), inner_NOP(was LOAD_FAST ctx), inner_BEFORE_WITH, inner_POP_TOP, inner_NOP(start), stack_loads, JUMP, inner_epilogue (NOP and onwards), outer_epilogue (NOP and onwards), old_bytecode.\n\nBUT within that outer range, there's the inner exception range (inner_POP_TOP, inner_NOM(start), stack_loads, JUMP, inner_NOM(end), inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, inner_cleanup).\n\nWait, inner_POP_TOP has exn->inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO. But inner_POP_TOP is ALSO in the outer exception range (since it comes after outer_POP_TOP). The exception table would have overlapping/nested entries. In the compiled code, nested entries take precedence for the inner range.\n\nNow, when Dynamo traces code1 and processes outer BEFORE_WITH:\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` = None (outer BEFORE_WITH is NOT in any exception table range)\n  Actually, is outer BEFORE_WITH covered? It comes BEFORE outer_POP_TOP (which is where the outer exception range starts). So NO, outer BEFORE_WITH is NOT in the outer exception range.\n  \n  But wait: does the NESTED inner exception range change this? No, inner exception range starts even later.\n  \n  So `inst.exn_tab_entry = None` for outer BEFORE_WITH.\n  \n- Case 1: `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n- `self.next_instruction` = outer_POP_TOP (which HAS exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n- `target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO` \u2713\n\nblock_stack: [{outer_no_grad, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO}]\n\nWhen Dynamo processes inner BEFORE_WITH (which comes after outer_NOM(start) = last instruction of outer setup_with):\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH comes after outer_NOP(start) in code1\n- Outer exception range starts at outer_POP_TOP (not outer_NOP)\n\nHmm, actually the outer exception range in code1: what exactly is its start?\n\nThe outer exception range in code1 is determined by the compiled exception table, which comes from the instruction objects' exn_tab_entries. The outer_POP_TOP has `exn_tab_entry` (from outer setup_with[3]). But the inner_POP_TOP also has exn_tab_entry. The inner_NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx) does NOT have exn_tab_entry. Inner BEFORE_WITH does NOT have exn_tab_entry (from the template).\n\nWait, let me check: in the inner template's setup_with, at what index is BEFORE_WITH? Is BEFORE_WITH in the exception range of the inner setup's exception table?\n\nLooking at the template exception table: `start=6, end=12` = POP_TOP at index 3 (offset 6) to POP_TOP after dummy (offset 10, which becomes NOP at index 5). The BEFORE_WITH is at offset 4 (index 2), which is NOT in the range [6, 12). So NO, inner BEFORE_WITH does NOT have exn_tab_entry from its own template.\n\nBut inner BEFORE_WITH DOES come after the outer exception range starts (outer_POP_TOP). So in code1, inner BEFORE_WITH IS in the OUTER exception range.\n\nAfter code1 is compiled and re-virtualized:\n- inner BEFORE_WITH has `exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO`\n\nWhen Dynamo processes inner BEFORE_WITH:\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` is not None (target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n- `self.block_stack[-1].target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO` (set during outer BEFORE_WITH processing)\n- Check: `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target`?\n  - Both are `outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO` instruction object from code1's virtualized list\n  - Are they the SAME Python object? Both come from `virtualize_exception_table` which creates one entry per unique target instruction. So YES, they should be the SAME Python object.\n  - `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is self.block_stack[-1].target` = True\n  - So `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is NOT self.block_stack[-1].target` = False\n- Condition is: `inst.exn_tab_entry and (not self.block_stack or inst.exn_tab_entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target)`\n  - = `True and (False or False)` = `True and False` = False\n- Else branch: `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n- `self.next_instruction` = inner_POP_TOP which HAS exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO \u2713\n- `target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO` \u2713\n\nblock_stack: [{outer_no_grad, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO}, {inner_enable_grad, target=inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO}]\n\nWhen the second graph break occurs (at backward()):\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset, inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset) = (P_OUTER, P_INNER)\n\nWhere P_OUTER and P_INNER are offsets in code1.\n\nIn `generate_based_on_original_code_object` for code1 -> meta.code (original fn):\n\n`remap_block_offsets` runs on code1:\n- Scans for PUSH_EXC_INFO: finds inner PUSH_EXC_INFO FIRST (since it's in the cleanup before outer)\n  Wait, where are the PUSH_EXC_INFOs in code1?\n  - cleanup = inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue\n  - inner_epilogue starts with NOM(end) then inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n  - outer_epilogue starts with NOM(end) then outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n  - So in code1: inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO comes BEFORE outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n  - P_INNER < P_OUTER\n\n- `len(prefix_block_target_offset_remap) = 2` (for outer and inner contexts)\n- Scanning code1 instructions in order:\n  - First PUSH_EXC_INFO found: inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO at P_INNER -> prefix_blocks[0]\n  - Second PUSH_EXC_INFO found: outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO at P_OUTER -> prefix_blocks[1]\n  \n- zip([inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO], meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n  meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [inner_original_offset, outer_original_offset] (after reversing)\n  So:\n  - block_target_offset_remap[P_INNER] = inner_original_offset \u2713\n  - block_target_offset_remap[P_OUTER] = outer_original_offset \u2713\n\n- old_start_offset = P_OUTER (OUTER PUSH_EXC_INFO, the LAST in prefix_blocks)\n- old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in (P_OUTER, P_INNER) if n > P_OUTER)\n  - P_OUTER is NOT > P_OUTER\n  - P_INNER < P_OUTER, so P_INNER is NOT > P_OUTER\n  - old_inst_offsets = []\n\nSo both P_OUTER and P_INNER are handled by the prefix mapping. The remap is built correctly.\n\nThen:\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (original_outer_offset, original_inner_offset) \u2713\n- This is remapped to the original code's offsets\n- code2 = ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(meta.code, lineno, new_offset, setup_fn_target_offsets, ...)\n\nThis seems correct! No KeyError!\n\nHmm, so where is the KeyError? Let me think about what could go WRONG.\n\nWait, I'm computing `setup_fn_target_offsets` as the block_stack targets in code1 tracing. But the block_stack is populated during code1's PROLOGUE tracing (when BEFORE_WITH is processed). The block_stack entries have `target` = instruction objects from code1's virtualized instruction list.\n\nBut `setup_fn_target_offsets` is built from `block_stack` entries' `target.offset` values. So `setup_fn_target_offsets[i] = block_stack[i].target.offset` (some offsets in code1).\n\nNow in `remap_block_offsets`, `setup_fn_target_offsets` contains code1 offsets. The remap maps code1 offsets to original-code offsets. Everything seems consistent.\n\nBUT: the `old_start_offset = prefix_blocks[-1].offset`. `prefix_blocks[-1]` is the LAST PUSH_EXC_INFO found before the count limit. What if there are MORE PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in the old bytecode?\n\nIn the old bytecode (which comes after the cleanup section in code1), there might be PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions from the original code's exception handling. When scanning for PUSH_EXC_INFO, the scan STOPS at `len(prefix_block_target_offset_remap)` count. So only the first 2 are counted as \"prefix blocks\".\n\nAfter collecting prefix_blocks (2 PUSH_EXC_INFOs), old_start_offset = P_OUTER (offset of outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO).\n\nBut old bytecode in code1 starts AFTER the cleanup section. The old bytecode is the ORIGINAL CODE CONTINUATION. For `test_with_on_graph_break_nested`, the old bytecode starts at something like `c = torch.sigmoid(b)`. \n\nOld bytecode PUSH_EXC_INFOs would be the ones from the original code's backward() implementation or something? In the original code `fn`, the only exception handlers are those from the `with` blocks. After the graph break (at `b = reversible(a)`), the remaining code is:\n```\nc = torch.sigmoid(b)\nc.sum().backward()\nreturn x.grad\n```\n\nThere are no `with` blocks in this remaining code, but the code IS INSIDE the outer `with` block. The original code's exception table covers these instructions with handler at outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO (original). However, when code1 is generated and `old_hook_target_remap` is applied, the exn_tab_entries of these instructions are remapped to point to outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO IN CODE1.\n\nSo `c.sum().backward()` in code1's old bytecode has `exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO_in_code1`. This is NOT a PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction - it's just the exception handler target. The instructions themselves are like LOAD_FAST, CALL, etc. No PUSH_EXC_INFO in the old bytecode (assuming simple code).\n\nSo there are exactly 2 PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1, and prefix_blocks collects both correctly. old_start_offset = P_OUTER. All seems fine.\n\nLet me now consider: what if the OLD BYTECODE itself contains a `with` block? Like:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with outer_ctx:\n        graph_break()  # first graph break\n        with inner_ctx:  # INSIDE old bytecode!\n            x = 1\n```\n\nIn this case:\n- First graph break inside outer context only\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (O_OUTER,) [only outer]\n- code1 has 1 hook (outer)\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O_OUTER_original]\n\ncode1's old bytecode: starts at the instruction after graph_break(), includes `with inner_ctx: x = 1`.\n\ncode1's old bytecode has PUSH_EXC_INFO for the inner `with` block!\n\nWhen remap_block_offsets scans for PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1:\n- prefix_blocks count limit = 1\n- First PUSH_EXC_INFO found: outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1 (from prefix cleanup)\n- prefix_blocks = [outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO] (count reached)\n\nblock_target_offset_remap[outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset] = O_OUTER_original \u2713\nold_start_offset = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset\n\nNow, when code1 is traced by Dynamo, the old bytecode includes `with inner_ctx:`. This means:\n- BEFORE_WITH for inner_ctx is processed\n- block_stack gets inner_ctx entry with target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO_in_code1\n\nAt the second graph break (inside inner_ctx's old bytecode):\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset, inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset)\n\nWait, but outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO is from code1's PREFIX, and inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO is from code1's OLD BYTECODE.\n\nActually, outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset is P_OUTER (from block_stack entry set during prologue processing). inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset is some offset IN code1's old bytecode, let's call it E_INNER_in_code1.\n\nIn remap_block_offsets:\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_OUTER] = O_OUTER_original \u2713\n- old_start_offset = P_OUTER\n- old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in (P_OUTER, E_INNER_in_code1) if n > P_OUTER)\n  - P_OUTER is NOT > P_OUTER\n  - E_INNER_in_code1 > P_OUTER? E_INNER_in_code1 is in the old bytecode section, which comes AFTER the cleanup section in code1. P_OUTER is in the cleanup section. So YES, E_INNER_in_code1 > P_OUTER! \u2713\n\nold_inst_offsets = [E_INNER_in_code1]\n\n_filter_iter finds the instruction at offset E_INNER_in_code1 in code1's instructions \u2713\n\nThen find the corresponding instruction in meta.instructions (zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions))) \u2713\n\nblock_target_offset_remap[E_INNER_in_code1] = corresponding_original_offset \u2713\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets becomes (O_OUTER_original, original_inner_offset) \u2713\n\nEverything works! No KeyError.\n\nHmm, so is the scenario even more specific? Let me think about what `setup_fn_target_offsets` could contain that's NOT in block_target_offset_remap.\n\nFrom the code at line 668-671:\n```python\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\nKeyError means `n` is not in `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`.\n\n`meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]` contains:\n1. All offsets from prefix_blocks (P_INNER, P_OUTER, etc.)\n2. All offsets from old bytecode that are in old_inst_offsets\n\nAn offset would NOT be in this mapping if:\n- It's neither a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO offset NOR an old_inst_offsets offset\n\nWhen could this happen? If the offset is < P_OUTER (old_start_offset) but is NOT one of the prefix_blocks offsets (P_INNER, P_OUTER).\n\nFor example: in the TWO-hook scenario (P_INNER < P_OUTER), the two prefix_blocks are at P_INNER and P_OUTER. If setup_fn_target_offsets contains some other offset X where P_INNER <= X <= P_OUTER but X \u2260 P_INNER and X \u2260 P_OUTER, then X would be in `old_inst_offsets` only if X > P_OUTER - which it's NOT.\n\nWhen would block_stack[-1].target.offset = X where X is in the PREFIX but NOT a PUSH_EXC_INFO in the prefix?\n\nHmm, let me think. What if the exception table for code1 has an entry targeting NOT the PUSH_EXC_INFO but some other instruction in the prefix? That seems unlikely since the prefix is generated code.\n\nActually, wait. Let me look at a DIFFERENT scenario. What if there are MORE than 2 PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in the OLD BYTECODE of code1?\n\nConsider:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with outer_ctx:        # PUSH_EXC_INFO at O1\n        with middle_ctx:   # PUSH_EXC_INFO at O2  \n            graph_break()  # first graph break inside both outer and middle\n            with inner_ctx:  # PUSH_EXC_INFO at O3, in OLD BYTECODE of code1\n                x = 1\n```\n\nWhen first graph break occurs:\n- block_stack = [{outer, O1}, {middle, O2}]\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (O1, O2) for original code\n- code1 generated with 2 hooks (outer and middle)\n\ncode1's prefix has 2 PUSH_EXC_INFOs: P_MIDDLE (inner epilogue) and P_OUTER (outer epilogue). That's 2.\n\ncode1's old bytecode has 1 PUSH_EXC_INFO: O3_code1 (inner_ctx exception handler in old bytecode).\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1:\n- Prologue processes BEFORE_WITH for outer -> block_stack: [{outer, P_OUTER}]\n- Prologue processes BEFORE_WITH for middle -> block_stack: [{outer, P_OUTER}, {middle, P_MIDDLE}]\n- Old bytecode: processes BEFORE_WITH for inner_ctx -> block_stack: [{outer, P_OUTER}, {middle, P_MIDDLE}, {inner, O3_code1}]\n- Second graph break occurs inside inner_ctx\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_OUTER, P_MIDDLE, O3_code1)\n\nIn remap_block_offsets:\n- Scans for PUSH_EXC_INFO, limit = 2 (len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap) = 2)\n- First PUSH_EXC_INFO: P_MIDDLE (middle epilogue comes before outer epilogue)\n- Second PUSH_EXC_INFO: P_OUTER\n- Count reached, stop!\n- prefix_blocks = [PUSH_EXC_INFO@P_MIDDLE, PUSH_EXC_INFO@P_OUTER]\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_MIDDLE] = middle_original \u2713\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_OUTER] = outer_original \u2713\n- old_start_offset = P_OUTER\n- old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in (P_OUTER, P_MIDDLE, O3_code1) if n > P_OUTER)\n  - P_OUTER NOT > P_OUTER\n  - P_MIDDLE < P_OUTER, so NOT > P_OUTER\n  - O3_code1 > P_OUTER? O3_code1 is in old bytecode which is AFTER cleanup. YES!\n  - old_inst_offsets = [O3_code1]\n\n_filter_iter finds instruction at O3_code1 in code1's instructions \u2713\nblock_target_offset_remap[O3_code1] = original_O3 \u2713\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets becomes (outer_original, middle_original, original_O3) \u2713\n\nNo KeyError!\n\nOK I'm really struggling to find a case where KeyError occurs. Let me try running the actual test to see what happens.\n"

NOTE: we assume that TOS is a context manager CLASS!\n   153\t        load_args = []\n   154\t        if self.target_values:\n   155\t            load_args = [create_load_const(val) for val in self.target_values]\n   156\t        ctx_name = unique_id(f\"___context_manager_{self.stack_index}\")\n   157\t        if ctx_name not in code_options[\"co_varnames\"]:\n   158\t            code_options[\"co_varnames\"] += (ctx_name,)\n   159\t        for name in [\"__enter__\", \"__exit__\"]:\n   160\t            if name not in code_options[\"co_names\"]:\n   161\t                code_options[\"co_names\"] += (name,)\n   162\t\n   163\t        create_ctx: list[Instruction] = []\n   164\t        _initial_push_null(create_ctx)\n   165\t        create_ctx.extend(\n   166\t            [\n   167\t                *load_args,\n   168\t                *create_call_function(len(load_args), False),\n   169\t                create_instruction(\"STORE_FAST\", argval=ctx_name),\n   170\t            ]\n   171\t        )\n   172\t\n   173\t        def _template(ctx: AbstractContextManager[Any], dummy: Any) -> None:\n   174\t            ctx.__enter__()\n   175\t            try:\n   176\t                dummy\n   177\t            finally:\n   178\t                ctx.__exit__(None, None, None)\n   179\t\n   180\t        setup_try_finally, epilogue = _bytecode_from_template_with_split(\n   181\t            _template, self.stack_index, varname_map={\"ctx\": ctx_name}\n   182\t        )\n   183\t        cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup\n   184\t        return create_ctx + setup_try_finally\n   185\t\n   186\t    def __call__(\n"

{
  "thought": "I see! `try_finally` uses a `try: dummy; finally: ...` template, NOT a `with ctx: dummy` template.\n\nLet me think about what `try: dummy; finally:` generates in Python 3.11+:\n\n```python\ndef _template(ctx, dummy):\n    ctx.__enter__()\n    try:\n        dummy\n    finally:\n        ctx.__exit__(None, None, None)\n```\n\nPython 3.11+ generates:\n```\nLOAD_FAST ctx\nLOAD_ATTR __enter__\nCALL 0         <- calls ctx.__enter__()\nPOP_TOP        <- discards result\nLOAD_FAST dummy  <- start of try body (has exn_tab_entry)\nPOP_TOP\n...finally...\nLOAD_FAST ctx\nLOAD_ATTR __exit__\nLOAD_CONST None * 3\nCALL 3\nPOP_TOP\n...\n```\n\nThe key thing is: there's NO BEFORE_WITH instruction in `try_finally`! So when code1 uses `try_finally` hooks (instead of `__call__`), there's no BEFORE_WITH in the prefix.\n\nWithout BEFORE_WITH in the prefix, the block_stack is NOT populated during the prologue tracing! So after the prologue, the block_stack would be empty.\n\nWhen the second graph break occurs in the old bytecode of code1 (which was traced without any BEFORE_WITH), block_stack is empty, so setup_fn_target_offsets = (). No KeyError.\n\nBut what about the `__call__` method which generates BEFORE_WITH? That DOES populate the block_stack.\n\nNow, which method is used for which context managers? Looking at how hooks are defined:\n\nFrom the context, looking at the block_stack setup in `handle_graph_break` (lines 967-984 from the task summary) and in `update_block_stack`:\n"
}

"Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /workspace/torch/_dynamo/symbolic_convert.py:\n  1368\t        def update_block_stack(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:\n  1369\t            # 3.11+ no longer uses a block stack, but we still keep track of one\n  1370\t            # so that we know which contexts are currently active.\n  1371\t            # For our purposes, all exception table entries with the same target\n  1372\t            # are considered to be part of the same \"block\".\n  1373\t            # NOTE: we only keep track of with blocks that are not contained in try blocks.\n  1374\t            # This is because we will not create continuation functions on graph breaks in try blocks,\n  1375\t            # but we may for with blocks. We do not push blocks here since\n  1376\t            # with blocks are pushed when handling BEFORE_WITH.\n  1377\t            entry = inst.exn_tab_entry\n  1378\t            if entry:\n  1379\t                # Detect when we have exited the top with block.\n  1380\t                # The with blocks on the block stack are not enclosed in try\n  1381\t                # blocks, so a with block's cleanup code should be in the\n  1382\t                # previous with block (if any).\n  1383\t                if (\n  1384\t                    len(self.block_stack) >= 2\n  1385\t                    and entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n  1386\t                    and entry.target is self.block_stack[-2].target\n  1387\t                ):\n  1388\t                    # exit the current block\n  1389\t                    self.block_stack.pop()\n  1390\t            else:\n  1391\t                # no longer in any block\n  1392\t                # It is possible for NOPs to be between two instructions\n  1393\t                # in the same block, but the NOPs are not covered by an\n  1394\t                # exception table entry. In this case, assume that we\n  1395\t                # are still in the same block.\n  1396\t                # In 3.12+, JUMP_BACKWARD might also not be covered by\n  1397\t                # an exception table entry, so we also assume that we\n  1398\t                # are still in the same block. It is probably safe to do\n  1399\t                # this in 3.11, even though we haven't encountered this case before.\n  1400\t                if self.block_stack and inst.opname not in (\"NOP\", \"JUMP_BACKWARD\"):\n  1401\t                    # If we really escape from a block and the current\n  1402\t                    # instruction is not in another block, then there\n  1403\t                    # should be no other nested blocks that we are in.\n  1404\t                    assert len(self.block_stack) == 1\n  1405\t                    self.block_stack.pop()\n  1406\t\n  1407\t    else:\n  1408\t\n  1409\t        def update_block_stack(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:\n  1410\t            pass\n  1411\t\n  1412\t    @property\n  1413\t    def next_instruction(self) -> Instruction:\n  1414\t        assert self.instruction_pointer is not None\n  1415\t        return self.instructions[self.instruction_pointer]\n  1416\t\n  1417\t    def step_graph_break(self, continue_inst: Instruction) -> None:\n  1418\t        # generate code from checkpoint\n  1419\t        assert not self.output.output_instructions\n  1420\t        assert self.current_speculation is not None\n"

In `handle_graph_break`:\n\nLine 983: `b.with_context.reconstruct_type(cg)` + `b.resume_fn().try_finally(cg.code_options, cleanup)`\n\nThe `try_finally` method is used here! NOT `__call__`. So when code1's prefix is generated, it uses `try_finally` for context managers, not `__call__`.\n\n`try_finally` generates:\n- setup_try_finally: [..., NOP(was LOAD_FAST dummy, has exn_tab_entry)]\n- epilogue: [NOM(was POP_TOP), ..., PUSH_EXC_INFO?, ...]\n\nActually, let me check what `try: dummy; finally: ctx.__exit__(None,None,None)` generates in Python 3.13. The `try/finally` in Python 3.11+ does NOT use BEFORE_WITH! So there's NO BEFORE_WITH instruction in code1's prefix.\n\nWithout BEFORE_WITH, block_stack is NOT populated during the prologue of code1! So when old bytecode of code1 is traced and a second graph break occurs, block_stack is empty (assuming no BEFORE_WITH in old bytecode).\n\nIf block_stack is empty, setup_fn_target_offsets = (). No KeyError.\n\nBut the test has `with torch.no_grad():` inside `with torch.enable_grad():` with a graph break. \n\nOH WAIT. When code1's OLD BYTECODE is traced (the code after the graph break), what does the OLD BYTECODE look like? In the test:\n\nOriginal fn:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)  # graph break here\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nThe graph break is at the CALL to `reversible(a)`. After the graph break, code1 should resume at... actually the CALL instruction itself is split. The graph break causes the output subgraph to be compiled, and a \"resume\" function is created starting from after the CALL (the `b = reversible(a)` result is passed as a parameter to code1).\n\nActually, let me think again. The graph break at `b = reversible(a)` means:\n- The subgraph UP TO the call is compiled\n- code1 starts at the CALL instruction itself (or just after it)\n\nHmm, actually the resume function starts at the instruction AFTER the call that causes the graph break. The block_stack at that point includes BOTH the outer and inner context managers.\n\ncode1 starts at the instruction after `b = reversible(a)` i.e., at `c = torch.sigmoid(b)`.\n\nIn code1, the OLD BYTECODE is:\n```\nc = torch.sigmoid(b)\nc.sum().backward()\nreturn x.grad\n```\n\nThese are inside BOTH `with torch.no_grad():` and `with torch.enable_grad():`.\n\nWait, but `c.sum().backward()` might cause another graph break during tracing! `backward()` is typically not supported by compile mode and causes a break.\n\nWhen code1 is traced by Dynamo:\n- Prologue: try_finally hooks for both contexts\n  - NO BEFORE_WITH in the prefix\n  - block_stack remains EMPTY during the prologue\n- Old bytecode: `c = torch.sigmoid(b)` - pure tensor op, might trace fine\n- `c.sum().backward()` - this might cause a graph break!\n\nWhen `backward()` causes a graph break, and `block_stack` is EMPTY (no contexts tracked during code1's tracing), then setup_fn_target_offsets = (). No KeyError.\n\nBut then the resume function code2 doesn't know it's inside the no_grad/enable_grad context! This would be a correctness bug (wrong gradient behavior), not a crash.\n\nHmm. But the test is about CORRECTNESS too. However, the task description says there's a CRASH (KeyError). So maybe the KeyError happens in a DIFFERENT scenario.\n\nLet me re-read the task description:\n\"KeyError crash in `resume_execution.py` during resume code generation on Python 3.11+ with nested context managers (e.g., nested `torch.no_grad()`)\"\n\n\"pending\" section says:\n- \"Root cause: filter `n > old_start_offset` (lines 651-652) may incorrectly exclude offsets that are neither prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO nor in old bytecode remap; with nested ctx managers, some `setup_fn_target_offsets` may not appear in either mapping\"\n\nThis suggests the offset might be < old_start_offset but NOT a prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO!\n\nCan this happen? When could a block_stack target be in the PREFIX but NOT the PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions?\n\nGoing back to `update_block_stack` (lines 1368-1405): this function uses `inst.exn_tab_entry.target` to track blocks. If `entry.target` is some instruction in the prefix that's NOT a PUSH_EXC_INFO...\n\nActually, looking at the `__call__` method again - it DOES generate BEFORE_WITH! And BEFORE_WITH IS in code1's prefix. When code1 is traced, if BEFORE_WITH is processed:\n- BEFORE_WITH calls `setup_or_before_with`\n- This pops the ctx from stack, creates exit, pushes to block_stack\n\nBut `setup_or_before_with` does NOT use `update_block_stack` - it directly pushes to block_stack. The target it uses is `self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`.\n\nOK so the `__call__` method generates a `with ctx:` block with BEFORE_WITH. If code1's prefix uses `__call__` for context managers, BEFORE_WITH is there and block_stack is populated.\n\nBut `handle_graph_break` uses `try_finally`! Not `__call__`.\n\nWait, let me re-read the `handle_graph_break` code:\n\n```python\nfor b in self.block_stack:\n    if isinstance(b.with_context, TorchFunctionModeVariable):\n        cg.extend_output(b.resume_fn().try_except_torch_function_mode(cg.code_options, cleanup))\n        continue\n    assert b.with_context is not None\n    assert isinstance(b.with_context, (ContextWrappingVariable))\n    b.with_context.reconstruct_type(cg)\n    cg.extend_output(b.resume_fn().try_finally(cg.code_options, cleanup))\n```\n\nThis uses `try_finally`, which doesn't generate BEFORE_WITH. So there IS no BEFORE_WITH in code1's prefix. Block_stack remains empty during code1's prologue tracing. No issue.\n\nBut then HOW does the test `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` FAIL? {
  "thought": "Now I see the critical line:\n\n```python\nnew_code: types.CodeType = ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(\n    cur_tx.f_code,\n    cur_tx.lineno,\n    resume_inst.offset,\n    tuple(b.target.offset for b in cur_tx.block_stack),  # <- setup_fn_target_offsets\n    ...\n)\n```\n\n`setup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(b.target.offset for b in cur_tx.block_stack)`\n\nAnd `cur_tx.block_stack` contains entries pushed by `setup_or_before_with`.\n\nNow for `store_attr_graph_break`, `cur_tx.block_stack` contains the block_stack at the time of the STORE_ATTR instruction. If STORE_ATTR is inside a `with` block, block_stack has the context manager entry.\n\nThe question is: what is `b.target.offset` for those entries?\n\nWhen we're processing the ORIGINAL function (the first level of tracing), block_stack entries have `b.target.offset` = offsets in the ORIGINAL function. These are actual offsets in the original code, so ContinueExecutionCache.lookup works fine.\n\nBut when we're processing code1 (second level of tracing), block_stack entries have `b.target.offset` = offsets in code1. These need to be remapped to the original function's offsets. THIS is what `remap_block_offsets` does.\n\nAnd the bug is: in `store_attr_graph_break`, line 2482:\n```python\nself.output.add_output_instructions([copy.copy(inst)])\n```\n\n`inst` here is the STORE_ATTR instruction. It has `inst.exn_tab_entry` pointing to some instruction in code1's instruction list. When the generated output code (for code0) is compiled, the `compute_exception_table` function tries to resolve `inst.exn_tab_entry.target` to an index in the output code's instruction list. But the target instruction is in code1's list, NOT in the output code's list. Hence a KeyError!\n\nThis is the SECOND BUG: the `copy.copy(inst)` retains the `exn_tab_entry` which has a target not in the output code. The fix is to set `inst_copy.exn_tab_entry = None`.\n\nOK so now I understand both bugs:\n\n**Bug 2 (STORE_ATTR)**: `store_attr_graph_break` at line 2482 copies `inst` (the STORE_ATTR instruction) including its `exn_tab_entry`. The `exn_tab_entry.target` points to an instruction in code1's virtualized instruction list, which doesn't exist in the output code. When `compute_exception_table` runs on the output code, it calls `indexof[inst.exn_tab_entry.target]` and crashes with KeyError because `target` is not in the output code's instructions.\n\n**Bug 1 (KeyError in resume_execution.py)**: This happens in `remap_block_offsets`. Let me think more carefully about when this could occur.\n\nActually, wait! Now let me re-read the STORE_ATTR bug more carefully. The STORE_ATTR instruction - when does this appear inside a `with` block?\n\nThe error from `compute_exception_table` in `bytecode_transformation.py` line 971 says:\n\"KeyError: `indexof[inst.exn_tab_entry.target]`\"\n\nBut `remap_block_offsets` is about `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]`, which is a DIFFERENT KeyError.\n\nSo these might be TWO SEPARATE KeyErrors. Bug 2 (STORE_ATTR) causes a KeyError in `bytecode_transformation.py:971`, and Bug 1 causes a KeyError in `resume_execution.py:669`.\n\nLet me now focus on what causes the KeyError in `resume_execution.py:669`.\n\nLet me look at the code more carefully:\n\n```python\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\n`meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]` is built in `remap_block_offsets`. The KeyError is `n not in meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`.\n\nThe mapping has two parts:\n1. `block_target_offset_remap[prefix_block.offset] = o` for each prefix block\n2. `block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset` for each old bytecode PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nAn offset `n` is not in the mapping if it falls through neither part.\n\nWhen would this happen with NESTED context managers?\n\nLet me think about this scenario more carefully. In the test `test_with_on_graph_break_nested`:\n- `fn` has nested `with torch.no_grad(): with torch.enable_grad():`\n- The graph break is at `reversible(a)`\n\nWhen Dynamo traces `fn`, it hits `with torch.no_grad()`:\n1. BEFORE_WITH for no_grad is processed\n   - BEFORE_WITH.exn_tab_entry = None (from the template, BEFORE_WITH is before exception range)\n   - next_instruction = POP_TOP, POP_TOP.exn_tab_entry.target = PUSH_EXC_INFO for no_grad\n   - block_stack: [{no_grad, target=no_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO@orig}]\n\n2. BEFORE_WITH for enable_grad is processed\n   - BEFORE_WITH.exn_tab_entry = ? (is inner BEFORE_WITH in the no_grad exception range?)\n   \nLet me look at what the ORIGINAL fn's exception table looks like.\n\nFor:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nThe compiled exception table in Python 3.13 (like we saw earlier for `with ctx1: with ctx2: x=1`):\n- The inner BEFORE_WITH IS within the outer no_grad exception range\n- So inner BEFORE_WITH.exn_tab_entry.target = outer no_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nWhen processing inner BEFORE_WITH in `setup_or_before_with`:\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` is not None (target = outer no_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n- `self.block_stack[-1].target` = outer no_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is self.block_stack[-1].target` = True (same object)\n- So condition `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is NOT block_stack[-1].target` = False\n- Go to else: `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n- next_instruction = POP_TOP after inner BEFORE_WITH\n- POP_TOP.exn_tab_entry.target = inner enable_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- block_stack: [{no_grad, target=no_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO}, {enable_grad, target=enable_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO}]\n\nNow the graph break occurs at `b = reversible(a)`. At this point:\n- block_stack = [{no_grad, target=no_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO}, {enable_grad, target=enable_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO}]\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (no_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset, enable_grad_PUSH_EXC_INFO.offset)\n- These are offsets in the ORIGINAL fn code\n\n`ContinueExecutionCache.lookup` is called with these. `remap_block_offsets` is called on the original fn code. This generates code1 with 2 hooks.\n\nSo far so good. Now when code1 is generated:\n\nThe PREFIX has (using `try_finally` from `handle_graph_break`):\n```python\nfor b in self.block_stack:  # [no_grad, enable_grad]\n    b.with_context.reconstruct_type(cg)\n    cg.extend_output(b.resume_fn().try_finally(cg.code_options, cleanup))\n```\n\nSo:\n- no_grad: `try_finally` adds setup_try_finally_nograd and appends epilogue_nograd to cleanup\n- enable_grad: `try_finally` adds setup_try_finally_enablegrad and appends epilogue_enablegrad to cleanup\n\nAfter the loop: `cleanup = [epilogue_enablegrad, epilogue_nograd]` (cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup each time)\n\nWait, `cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup`:\n- Initially cleanup = []\n- After no_grad: cleanup[:] = epilogue_nograd + [] = [epilogue_nograd...]\n- After enable_grad: cleanup[:] = epilogue_enablegrad + [epilogue_nograd...]\n- Final cleanup = [epilogue_enablegrad..., epilogue_nograd...]\n\nSo epilogue_enablegrad (inner) comes BEFORE epilogue_nograd (outer) in cleanup.\n\nThe PUSH_EXC_INFO for enable_grad is in epilogue_enablegrad, and PUSH_EXC_INFO for no_grad is in epilogue_nograd.\n\nIn code1's instruction list order:\n1. setup_try_finally_nograd (prefix for outer)\n2. setup_try_finally_enablegrad (prefix for inner)\n3. OLD BYTECODE (c = torch.sigmoid(b), ...)\n4. epilogue_enablegrad (inner cleanup) -- has inner PUSH_EXC_INFO\n5. epilogue_nograd (outer cleanup) -- has outer PUSH_EXC_INFO\n\nSo inner PUSH_EXC_INFO comes BEFORE outer PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's instruction list.\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets` for code1:\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [inner_original_offset, outer_original_offset]\n\nWait, how is this built? In `generate_based_on_original_code_object` (which generates code1):\n\nLet me look at the `ResumeExecutionCache.lookup` function more carefully.\n"
}

  "thought": "Now I see the logic more clearly.\n\nWhen `setup_or_before_with` is called with a BEFORE_WITH instruction:\n1. Pop `ctx` from stack\n2. Check if target should be set:\n   - If `inst.exn_tab_entry` is set AND (block_stack is empty OR `inst.exn_tab_entry.target` is NOT `block_stack[-1].target`):\n     - `target = None` (don't push to block_stack)\n   - Else:\n     - Assert `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None`\n     - `target = next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n3. Push exit and ctx to stack\n4. If target: append to block_stack\n\nThe cases are:\n- Case A: `inst.exn_tab_entry` is None (BEFORE_WITH not in any exception table range)\n  \u2192 Goes to else, sets target from next instruction\n  \n- Case B: `inst.exn_tab_entry` is not None AND block_stack is empty\n  \u2192 `target = None` (BEFORE_WITH inside try block, not in any `with` block)\n  \n- Case C: `inst.exn_tab_entry` is not None AND `target` is NOT block_stack[-1].target\n  \u2192 `target = None` (BEFORE_WITH inside a try block that is not this `with` block)\n  \n- Case D: `inst.exn_tab_entry` is not None AND `target` IS block_stack[-1].target\n  \u2192 `target = next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n\nOK so for nested with blocks (Case D), when the inner BEFORE_WITH IS inside the outer with block's exception range, it sets `target = next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`.\n\nNow, when does the assert at line 3482 fail? When `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is None`.\n\nFor the inner `with` in the original function:\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH IS in the outer exception range (Case D)\n- next_instruction = inner POP_TOP (follows BEFORE_WITH)\n- Inner POP_TOP: does it have an exn_tab_entry?\n\nFrom the compiled exception table:\n```\nEntry(start=6, end=12, target=30, depth=1, lasti=True)\n```\nFor the template, POP_TOP at offset 6 (index 3 of template) HAS an exn_tab_entry.\n\nBut this is in the TEMPLATE. In the ORIGINAL FUNCTION, the inner BEFORE_WITH and inner POP_TOP have their exception table entries based on the original function's code.\n\nFor the original function compiled by CPython:\n```\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            ...\n```\n\nPython compiles nested with blocks. In Python 3.13, the exception table for nested with would look something like:\n```\nEntry(start=offset_after_outer_BEFORE_WITH, end=outer_cleanup_start, target=outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=1)\nEntry(start=offset_after_inner_BEFORE_WITH, end=inner_cleanup_start, target=inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=2)\n```\n\nSo the inner POP_TOP (after inner BEFORE_WITH) should have exn_tab_entry.target = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO. The assert shouldn't fail.\n\nHmm. The ACTUAL bugs might be different from this analysis path.\n\nLet me look at the code more carefully for the ACTUAL scenario.\n\nActually, let me think about the STORE_ATTR bug first, because that's clearer:\n\nIn `store_attr_graph_break`, when a STORE_ATTR instruction is inside a `with` block (which has an exception table entry), the `copy.copy(inst)` retains `exn_tab_entry`. The `exn_tab_entry.target` is an instruction in the original function's virtualized instruction list, not in the output code's instruction list.\n\nWhen `compute_exception_table` runs on the output code, it calls `indexof[inst.exn_tab_entry.target]`. Since `target` is not in the output instructions, this causes a KeyError in `bytecode_transformation.py`.\n\nThe fix: clear `exn_tab_entry` on the copied instruction, like `handle_graph_break` does at lines 1006-1008:\n```python\ninst_copy = copy.copy(inst)\ninst_copy.exn_tab_entry = None\nself.output.add_output_instructions([inst_copy])\n```\n\nNow for Bug 1 (KeyError in resume_execution.py line 669):\n\nLet me trace through a scenario where STORE_ATTR is inside a `with` block AND causes a graph break, and then resume_execution generates code that has nested contexts.\n\nWait, but the STORE_ATTR bug would cause a KeyError in bytecode_transformation.py FIRST, before ever reaching resume_execution.py. Unless... the STORE_ATTR graph break happens during the second trace (code1 tracing), which is when resume_execution.py is generating code2.\n\nBut no, `store_attr_graph_break` calls `compile_subgraph` which calls `generate_based_on_original_code_object` which calls `ContinueExecutionCache.lookup` which calls `remap_block_offsets`. The KeyError in resume_execution.py:669 would be in this lookup call.\n\nLet me think differently. The task description says there are TWO separate bugs:\n1. KeyError in `resume_execution.py` during resume code generation\n2. Incorrect STORE_ATTR handling in `with` blocks\n\nThese might be related - Bug 2 (STORE_ATTR) causes a KeyError in `compute_exception_table` (bytecode_transformation.py), and Bug 1 is a SEPARATE KeyError in resume_execution.py.\n\nFor Bug 1 to occur, the `remap_block_offsets` function must get an offset that's in `setup_fn_target_offsets` but not in its mapping. \n\nLet me think about a scenario where this could happen with nested context managers:\n\nConsider: the first graph break creates code1 with 2 hooks (outer and inner). When code1 is traced by Dynamo:\n- The PREFIX has `try_finally` hooks (no BEFORE_WITH)\n- But the OLD BYTECODE of code1 comes from the original function\n- Wait, NO! The old bytecode of code1 IS the original function's bytecode from the resume point onwards!\n- In the original function, the resume point is inside `with no_grad(): with enable_grad():`\n- So the OLD BYTECODE of code1 includes instructions that ARE in the exception table of the original function!\n\nActually, in ContinueExecutionCache.lookup, the old bytecode for code1 is based on the ORIGINAL function (`f_code`). The instructions are cloned from the original function, and their `exn_tab_entry` references are remapped by `old_hook_target_remap`.\n\nSpecifically:\n```python\nold_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target] = exn_target\n```\n\nWhere:\n- `old_hook_target` = original instruction at `hook_target_offset` (the original PUSH_EXC_INFO)\n- `exn_target` = new PUSH_EXC_INFO generated in the cleanup (from the hook's epilogue)\n\nSo instructions in the old bytecode that had `exn_tab_entry.target = original_PUSH_EXC_INFO` now have `exn_tab_entry.target = new_PUSH_EXC_INFO_in_code1`.\n\nNow, when Dynamo TRACES code1:\n- The prefix is executed (no BEFORE_WITH, so block_stack remains empty during prologue)\n- Then old bytecode starts\n- At some point (e.g., backward()), a second graph break occurs\n\nAt the second graph break in code1's tracing:\n- `block_stack` is EMPTY (no BEFORE_WITH was processed in the prefix)\n- `setup_fn_target_offsets = ()` (empty tuple)\n- `ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(code1, ...)` is called with empty `setup_fn_target_offsets`\n\nNo KeyError here either!\n\nBUT WAIT. What about when `store_attr_graph_break` is called? This calls `compile_subgraph` with `block_stack` populated from the current state. If STORE_ATTR is in the ORIGINAL function (not code1), and the code is inside a `with` block, the block_stack has entries.\n\nBut `store_attr_graph_break` calls `self.create_call_resume_at(self.next_instruction, ...)` which builds the resume function. After the graph break, code1 has old bytecode `c = torch.sigmoid(b); c.sum().backward(); return x.grad`. It does NOT call `create_call_resume_at`, so no resume function is generated via `ContinueExecutionCache.lookup`!\n\nInstead it uses the `*self.instructions` approach - inserting the original instructions directly into the output, jumping to the resume point.\n\nFor the nested case (has parent), `create_call_resume_at` IS called. While reading\n  1059\t    # the documentation, is important to notice that the terms \"current exception\"\n  1060\t    # and \"stack\" sometimes refers to a C variable with the same name and the\n  1061\t    # exception stack, respectively.\n  1062\t    #\n  1063\t    # The lifetime of an exception is (Python 3.11+):\n  1064\t    #  + tx._raise_exception_variable(...) := sets the current_exception variable\n  1065\t    #  + PUSH_EXC_INFO := pushes the current_exception to the *exception stack*\n  1066\t    #  + POP_EXCEPT := pops TOS from the *exception stack*\n  1067\t\n  1068\t    _exc_stack: list[ExceptionVals] = dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)\n  1069\t    _current_exception: Optional[ExceptionVals] = dataclasses.field(default=None)\n  1070\t\n  1071\t    def clear_current_exception(self) -> None:\n  1072\t        self._current_exception = None\n  1073\t\n  1074\t    def set_current_exception(self, val: ExceptionVals) -> None:\n  1075\t        self._set_context_and_break_context_reference_cycle(val)\n  1076\t        self._current_exception = val\n  1077\t\n  1078\t    def move_current_exception_to_stack(self) -> None:\n  1079\t        assert self._current_exception is not None\n  1080\t        self.append(self._current_exception)\n"
