The metadata was stored when code1 was GENERATED.\n\n```python\ntransform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)\n```\n\nThis transforms code1's bytecode. `remap_block_offsets` gets called with code1's (possibly transformed) instructions.\n\nInside `remap_block_offsets(instructions, ...)`:\n- `instructions` = code1's instructions\n- Scans for PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1\n- `setup_fn_target_offsets` = (P_outer_code1, P_inner_code1) (offsets in code1)\n\nAnd then:\n```python\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\nmaps code1 offsets \u2192 original code offsets. Then calls ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(meta.code, ..., setup_fn_target_offsets_remapped, ...) to generate code2.\n\nFor this to work, we need:\n- `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][P_outer_code1]` to exist\n- `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][P_inner_code1]` to exist\n\nAs I analyzed, both P_inner_code1 and P_outer_code1 are in `block_target_offset_remap` via the prefix_blocks mapping. So no KeyError.\n\nWAIT WAIT WAIT. I think I've been wrong about the ordering of prefix_blocks!\n\nLet me re-check the order of PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1:\n\nCode1's structure:\n```\nPREFIX:\n  [outer setup_with: NOP, NOP(ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@P_outer), NOM(exn@P_outer)]\n  [inner setup_with: LOAD_FAST inner_ctx_args, ..., BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@P_inner), NOM(exn@P_inner)]\nOLD BYTECODE:\n  [c = sigmoid(b), ...]\nCLEANUP:\n  [inner_epilogue: NOM(exn@P_inner), ..., PUSH_EXC_INFO(P_inner), ..., RERAISE]\n  [outer_epilogue: NOM(exn@P_outer), ..., PUSH_EXC_INFO(P_outer), ..., RERAISE]\n```\n\nWait, is the outer epilogue before or after the inner epilogue?\n\n`cleanup[:] = epilogue + cleanup`:\n- Initially: cleanup = []\n- Process outer hook (stack_index = 0): cleanup[:] = outer_epilogue + [] = outer_epilogue\n- Process inner hook (stack_index = 1): cleanup[:] = inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue\n\nFinal cleanup = [inner_epilogue, outer_epilogue]\n\nSo inner_epilogue (with P_inner PUSH_EXC_INFO) comes FIRST, then outer_epilogue (with P_outer PUSH_EXC_INFO).\n\nIn remap_block_offsets scanning CODE1's instructions (which end with [inner_epilogue, outer_epilogue]):\n- First PUSH_EXC_INFO found: P_inner (in inner_epilogue)\n- Second PUSH_EXC_INFO found: P_outer (in outer_epilogue)\n- prefix_blocks = [P_inner_inst, P_outer_inst]\n- old_start_offset = P_outer (the LAST one)\n\nmeta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [inner_O2, outer_O1] (REVERSED from build order)\n\nWait, build order: outer hook processed first \u2192 append(outer_O1). Inner hook processed second \u2192 append(inner_O2). List = [outer_O1, inner_O2]. After reversing: [inner_O2, outer_O1].\n\nZip([P_inner_inst, P_outer_inst], [inner_O2, outer_O1]):\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_inner] = inner_O2 \u2713\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_outer] = outer_O1 \u2713\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = (P_outer, P_inner) (from block_stack: outer first, inner second).\n\nFor remapping:\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_outer] = outer_O1 \u2713\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_inner] = inner_O2 \u2713\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets_remapped = (outer_O1, inner_O2) \u2713\n\nThen ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(meta.code=original_fn, ..., (outer_O1, inner_O2), ...) generates code2 correctly.\n\nSTILL NO KEYERROR!\n\nOK maybe the issue is with the `__call__` method generating BEFORE_WITH, which changes the exception table depth in nested contexts.\n\nRecall that in `_bytecode_from_template_with_split`:\n```python\nfor inst in template_code:\n    if inst.exn_tab_entry:\n        inst.exn_tab_entry.depth += stack_index\n```\n\nThe depth of exception table entries is adjusted by `stack_index`. For outer hook (stack_index=0), depth is not changed. For inner hook (stack_index=1), depth is increased by 1.\n\nAnd the depth of the outer POP_TOP (exn_tab_entry) in setup_with is 1 (from the template).\nAfter adjustment for inner hook (stack_index=1): inner POP_TOP depth = 1+1 = 2.\n\nThis affects how Python handles exceptions - the depth determines how many entries to pop from the exception stack. This is correct for nested contexts.\n\nOK let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at what the ACTUAL difference is between what code1 generates and what we need, by looking at the exception table depths and seeing if there's a case where `next_instruction.exn_tab_entry` could be None.\n\nFor BEFORE_WITH in code1's inner prefix:\n- Inner BEFORE_WITH is in the outer exception range (P_outer)\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry.target = P_outer`\n- `block_stack[-1].target = P_outer`\n- So condition `inst.exn_tab_entry.target is not block_stack[-1].target` = FALSE\n- Go to else: `target = next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target`\n- next_instruction = inner POP_TOP\n\nNow: does inner POP_TOP have `exn_tab_entry`?\n\nIn code1 compiled (via `compute_exception_table`), the exception table is built from all instruction exn_tab_entries. \n\nThe inner setup_with has [NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@P_inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO), NOM(exn@P_inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO)].\n\nPOP_TOP has exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO. This is reflected in the compiled code1's exception table. After virtualization, inner POP_TOP has exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object.\n\nBUT wait: compute_exception_table in bytecode_transformation.py - does it compute the exception table BEFORE or AFTER adjusting offsets? And does the compiled code1 preserve the exn_tab_entry.target pointers?\n\nActually, when `transform_code_object` generates a new code object, it:\n1. Creates a list of instructions\n2. Assigns offsets to instructions\n3. Calls `compute_exception_table` to build the exception table from exn_tab_entries\n4. When virtualized again, `virtualize_exception_table` reads the compiled exception table and creates new InstructionExnTabEntry objects for each instruction\n\nSo the key question is: after compilation of code1 and re-virtualization, does inner POP_TOP have exn_tab_entry?\n\nThe compiled exception table has ranges. If inner POP_TOP is at offset X and the compiled table has an entry covering [X, ...) with target P_inner, then after virtualization, inner POP_TOP has exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner instruction object.\n\nBUT: compute_exception_table might NOT create a separate entry for [X, inner_NOM+1) targeting P_inner if it's already covered by the outer range [outer_POP_TOP, ...) targeting P_outer.\n\nHmm, actually `compute_exception_table` creates entries based on EACH instruction's exn_tab_entry, not on ranges from the original exception table. It creates groups of consecutive instructions that have the SAME exn_tab_entry.target (same Python instruction object), and creates a range for each group.\n\nActually, it's more nuanced. The original template's exception table said \"start=6, end=12, target=30\". After splitting and assembling into code1, the inner POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry says: target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction object, depth = 2 (adjusted for stack_index).\n\nMeanwhile, the instructions BETWEEN outer POP_TOP and inner POP_TOP (like the inner NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx), BEFORE_WITH) DON'T have exn_tab_entry (since they're not covered in the inner template's exception table, and they're not in the outer's range start yet).\n\nActually wait, the outer setup_with ends with NOM(was LOAD_FAST dummy, exn@P_outer). Then the next instruction is the inner's [NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx)] which starts the inner setup_with. The inner NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx) does NOT have exn_tab_entry (it's not covered by any range in the inner template). But does it have exn_tab_entry from being inside the OUTER exception range?\n\nIn the OUTER template's exception table: start=6 (outer POP_TOP), end=12 (inner NOM+), target=PUSH_EXC_INFO.\n\nAfter splitting outer template, the outer setup_with ends with NOM(was LOAD_FAST dummy) at some position. The outer epilogue's NOM(was POP_TOP) starts the outer exception range in the epilogue.\n\nBut the OUTER exception range in code1 doesn't come from the outer template's \"start=6\" directly. It comes from the outer POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry in the outer setup_with.\n\nHmm, I think the issue is that instructions in the INNER setup_with that don't have an exn_tab_entry in the inner template MAY still need to be covered by the outer exception range.\n\nLet me look at this from the `compute_exception_table` perspective. When code1 is compiled:\n1. Outer setup_with:\n   - NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx): no exn_tab_entry\n   - BEFORE_WITH: no exn_tab_entry\n   - POP_TOP: exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=1\n   - NOM(was LOAD_FAST dummy): exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=1\n2. Inner setup_with:\n   - NOM(was LOAD_FAST ctx): no exn_tab_entry\n   - BEFORE_WITH: no exn_tab_entry\n   - POP_TOP: exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=2\n   - NOM(was LOAD_FAST dummy): exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=2\n3. Old bytecode:\n   - Various instructions, some with exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO or inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO (remapped from original)\n4. Inner epilogue:\n   - NOM(was POP_TOP): exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=2\n   - ... more inner cleanup ...\n   - inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO: no exn_tab_entry\n   - ...\n5. Outer epilogue:\n   - NOM(was POP_TOP): exn_tab_entry.target = outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=1\n   - ... more outer cleanup ...\n   - outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO: no exn_tab_entry\n   - ...\n\n`compute_exception_table` creates ranges by scanning instructions and grouping consecutive instructions with the same (target_offset, depth, lasti):\n- Group 1: [POP_TOP outer, NOM outer] \u2192 outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=1\n- Group 2: [POP_TOP inner, NOM inner] \u2192 inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO, depth=2\n- Group 3: [old bytecode instructions with outer target] \u2192 outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- Group 4: [old bytecode instructions with inner target] \u2192 inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- Group 5: [inner epilogue instructions] \u2192 ???\n- Group 6: [outer epilogue instructions] \u2192 ???\n\nThe actual grouping depends on consecutive runs of same target.\n\nAfter compilation and re-virtualization of code1:\n- Inner POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO \u2713\n\nSo when Dynamo traces code1 and processes inner BEFORE_WITH:\n- next_instruction = inner POP_TOP\n- inner POP_TOP HAS exn_tab_entry.target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO \u2713\n- assert passes \u2713\n- target = inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO \u2713\n\nEverything still works! I cannot find a case where the KeyError occurs!\n\nLet me try a completely fresh approach: look at what specific change would fix the issue by examining the code structure more carefully.\n\nThe task description mentions:\n- \"KeyError crash in resume_execution.py during resume code generation on Python 3.11+ with nested context managers\"\n- Line 669: `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]`\n\nFor KeyError at line 669, `n` is not in the remap. From my analysis, all offsets should be in the remap. So either my analysis is wrong, or there's a specific edge case I'm missing.\n\nLet me look at this from the code structure perspective. What values can `setup_fn_target_offsets` contain that would NOT be in `block_target_offset_remap`?\n\nActually, I just realized something! The `remap_block_offsets` function is a CLOSURE over `setup_fn_target_offsets`. And `setup_fn_target_offsets` is the OUTER SCOPE's variable. When `remap_block_offsets` runs, `setup_fn_target_offsets` might have ALREADY BEEN MODIFIED by some earlier code... but no, looking at the code:\n\n```python\nif new_offset not in meta.block_target_offset_remap:\n    block_target_offset_remap = meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset] = {}\n\n    def remap_block_offsets(instructions, code_options):\n        ...\n        old_inst_offsets = sorted(\n            n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset\n        )\n        ...\n    \n    transform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\n`remap_block_offsets` uses the ORIGINAL `setup_fn_target_offsets` (before remapping). Set is_tracing_resume_prologue back to allow graph breaks after the jump\n   521\t                prefix.extend(\n   522\t                    [\n   523\t                        create_instruction(\"LOAD_CONST\", argval=False),\n   524\t                        create_instruction(\n   525\t                            \"STORE_FAST\", argval=IS_TRACING_RESUME_PROLOGUE_VARNAME\n   526\t                        ),\n   527\t                    ]\n   528\t                )\n   529\t\n   530\t            prefix.append(create_jump_absolute(target))\n   531\t\n   532\t            # because the line number table monotonically increases from co_firstlineno\n   533\t            # remove starts_line for any instructions before the graph break instruction\n   534\t            # this will ensure the instructions after the break have the correct line numbers\n   535\t            for inst in instructions:\n   536\t                if inst.offset == target.offset:\n   537\t                    break\n   538\t                inst.starts_line = None\n   539\t                if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\n   540\t                    inst.positions = None\n   541\t\n   542\t            if cleanup:\n   543\t                prefix.extend(cleanup)\n   544\t                prefix.extend(cls.unreachable_codes(code_options))\n   545\t\n"

CALL 0` (creates enable_grad ctx): stack = [outer_exit_fn, enable_grad_ctx], len=2\n\nInner BEFORE_WITH:\n- `ctx = self.pop()` \u2192 stack = [outer_exit_fn], len=1, ctx = enable_grad_ctx\n- `exit = WithExitFunctionVariable(...)` \n- `self.push(exit)` \u2192 stack = [outer_exit_fn, inner_exit_fn], len=2\n- BlockStackEntry.stack_index = 2\n- resume_fn().stack_index = 2 - 1 = 1\n\nSo inner_fn.stack_index = 1, outer_fn.stack_index = 0.\n\nhooks = {0: outer_fn, 1: inner_fn}.\n\nIn `generate_based_on_original_code_object` for code1:\n```python\nfor i in range(nstack):\n    while null_idxes_i < ... and ...: prefix.append(PUSH_NULL); null_idxes_i += 1\n    prefix.append(LOAD_FAST(f\"___stack{i}\"))\n    if i in hooks:\n        hook = hooks.pop(i)\n        hook_insts, exn_target = hook(code_options, cleanup)\n        prefix.extend(hook_insts)\n        meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap.append(hook_target_offsets.pop(i))\n```\n\nWhen i=0: Process outer hook (stack_index=0)\n- LOAD_FAST ___stack0 (which is outer_exit_fn)\n- Execute outer hook (ReenterWith.__call__), get setup_with and push_exc_info\n- cleanup[:] = outer_epilogue + []\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O1] (outer original offset)\n\nWhen i=1: Process inner hook (stack_index=1)\n- LOAD_FAST ___stack1 (which is inner_exit_fn) \n- Execute inner hook (ReenterWith.__call__), get setup_with and push_exc_info\n- cleanup[:] = inner_epilogue + outer_epilogue\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O1, O2]\n\nAfter reversing: [O2, O1]\n\nIn code1's instruction list:\n- ...setup for outer...\n  - LOAD_FAST ___stack0 (outer_exit_fn)\n  - [outer setup_with: NOM(ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@P_outer), NOM(exn@P_outer)]\n- ...setup for inner...\n  - LOAD_FAST ___stack1 (inner_exit_fn)\n  - [inner setup_with: NOM(ctx), BEFORE_WITH, POP_TOP(exn@P_inner), NOM(exn@P_inner)]\n- ...IS_TRACING = False, JUMP_ABSOLUTE to target...\n- [outer_epilogue: NOM(exn@P_outer), ..., P_outer PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n- [inner_epilogue: NOM(exn@P_inner), ..., P_inner PUSH_EXC_INFO, ...]\n\nWait, I said cleanup = [inner_epilogue, outer_epilogue] earlier. IS_TRACING=False, JUMP_ABSOLUTE(old_bytecode_start)\n5. inner_epilogue (has inner_PUSH_EXC_INFO at P_inner)\n6. outer_epilogue (has outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO at P_outer)\n\nSo INNER epilogue comes BEFORE outer epilogue!\nTherefore P_inner < P_outer in code1.\n\nremap_block_offsets scans from beginning:\n- prefix_blocks expected: 2\n- Finds P_inner_inst first (step 5), then P_outer_inst (step 6)\n- prefix_blocks = [P_inner_inst, P_outer_inst]\n- old_start_offset = P_outer\n\nmeta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [O2, O1] (inner_original first)\n\nZip: (P_inner_inst, O2), (P_outer_inst, O1)\nblock_target_offset_remap[P_inner] = O2 \u2713 (inner code1 \u2192 inner original)\nblock_target_offset_remap[P_outer] = O1 \u2713 (outer code1 \u2192 outer original)\n\nsetup_fn_target_offsets at second graph break = (P_outer, P_inner) (from block_stack: outer first).\n\nAfter remap:\n- P_outer \u2192 O1 \u2713\n- P_inner \u2192 O2 \u2713\n\nResult: (O1, O2) = (outer_original, inner_original). Looking at the test:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)   # graph break here\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()  # possible second graph break here\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nThe first graph break is at `b = reversible(a)`. Dynamo traces into `backward()` and may cause a graph break at some point inside it.\n\nBUT: if Dynamo doesn't inline `backward()` (treats it as an opaque call), there might be no graph break at `backward()`.\n\nLet me look from a different angle. The second graph break (if any) would be after tracing `c = torch.sigmoid(b)`, `c.sum()`, then `backward()`.\n\nDynamo: `backward()` is a method that triggers autograd execution. When Dynamo encounters this, it DOES NOT trace through the backward pass. Instead, it:\n1. Compiles the subgraph (forward pass: `c = sigmoid(b); c.sum()`)\n2. Graph breaks at `.backward()`... actually wait, this would be a method call.\n\nHmm, but with eager backend and `with torch.no_grad(): with torch.enable_grad():`, the computation is in enable_grad mode. With `requires_grad=True`, `.backward()` computes gradients.\n\nActually, I realize I need to stop speculating and look at this from the other direction. Let me look at what EXACTLY the fix should be.\n\nFrom the TASK_CONTEXT, the bugs are:\n1. KeyError crash in `resume_execution.py` line 669 during resume code generation\n2. Incorrect STORE_ATTR handling: `copy.copy(inst)` at line 2482 retains `exn_tab_entry`\n\nLet me look at the ACTUAL code that has the problem, specifically around STORE_ATTR handling. Let me understand the STORE_ATTR scenario more precisely.\n\nIn `store_attr_graph_break`:\n```python\ndef store_attr_graph_break(self, inst):\n    ...\n    self.output.add_output_instructions([copy.copy(inst)])\n```\n\nThis copies the STORE_ATTR instruction, INCLUDING its `exn_tab_entry`. If STORE_ATTR is inside a `with` block, `exn_tab_entry.target` points to the PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction.\n\nBut `add_output_instructions` adds these to the OUTPUT code (the compiled graph's code). When `compute_exception_table` runs on the output code, it looks for `inst.exn_tab_entry.target` in the output instructions. Since the PUSH_EXC_INFO target is NOT in the output instructions (it's in the original function's instruction list), this causes a KeyError.\n\nThe fix is straightforward: `inst_copy.exn_tab_entry = None`.\n\nNow for the KeyError in `resume_execution.py`:\n\nLet me look at this from the perspective of what could be WRONG with `remap_block_offsets`. \n\nWait! I just realized something. There's a subtle issue:\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets`, `prefix_blocks` is built by scanning code1's COMPILED instructions (after `transform_code_object` compiled them). When `remap_block_offsets` gets called, it gets the ALREADY COMPILED instructions of code1 (or a transformed version?).\n\nActually, `transform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)` transforms the code object. The callback `remap_block_offsets` gets called with the VIRTUALIZED instructions of `code` (code1). These are instructions extracted from the compiled code1 bytecode.\n\nNow, after `transform_code_object` compiles, virtualizes, calls the callback, then recompiles:\n1. code1 is virtualized (instructions extracted from compiled bytecode)\n2. `remap_block_offsets` is called with these virtualized instructions\n3. The callback modifies the instructions (actually it doesn't modify them - it only builds `block_target_offset_remap`)\n\nWait, `remap_block_offsets` DOESN'T modify the instructions. It only reads them to build the mapping. Then `transform_code_object` recompiles the instructions. Since the callback doesn't modify anything, the output should be the same as the input.\n\nBut there's a side effect: `block_target_offset_remap[cast(int, inst.offset)] = o` - this stores the INSTRUCTION OFFSET (from the compiled code1 bytecode) mapped to an original code offset.\n\nThen at line 669:\n```python\nsetup_fn_target_offsets = tuple(\n    meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset][n]\n    for n in setup_fn_target_offsets\n)\n```\n\n`n` here is a COMPILED offset in code1. P_inner_original_offset_code1:\n- Is P_inner_original_offset_code1 > outer_offset_code1?\n- P_inner_original is in code1's instruction list as part of the ORIGINAL function's instructions appended to the prefix.\n- outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO_code1 is in code1's instruction list as part of the prefix's cleanup section.\n- Order: [prefix cleanup] THEN [original function instructions]\n- So outer_PUSH_EXC_INFO_code1 comes BEFORE P_inner_original in code1's instruction list.\n- Therefore: P_inner_original_offset_code1 > outer_offset_code1 \u2713\n\nSo P_inner_original_offset_code1 IS > old_start_offset, so it goes into old_inst_offsets!\n\nThen `_filter_iter` tries to find the corresponding instruction in meta.instructions (original function instructions) by matching code1's Python 3.11+ fields\n   245\t    # NOTE: Python 3.11 removed blocks, but for our purposes, a \"block\" consists\n   246\t    # of instructions of all exception table entries that have the same target.\n   247\t\n   248\t    # per-offset map from new block target offsets to original block target offsets\n   253\t    block_target_offset_remap: dict[int, dict[int, int]] = dataclasses.field(\n   254\t        default_factory=dict\n   255\t    )\n   256\t\n   257\t\n   258\tdef _filter_iter(\n   259\t    l1: Iterable[Any],\n   260\t    l2: Iterable[Any],\n   261\t    cond: Callable[[Any, Any], bool],\n   262\t) -> list[Any]:\n   263\t    \"\"\"\n   264\t    Two-pointer conditional filter.\n   265\t    e.g. _filter_iter(insts, sorted_offsets, lambda i, o: i.offset == o)\n   266\t    returns the instructions with offsets in sorted_offsets\n   267\t    \"\"\"\n   268\t    it = iter(l2)\n   269\t    res: list[Instruction] = []\n   270\t    try:\n   271\t        cur = next(it)\n   272\t        for val in l1:\n   273\t            if cond(val, cur):\n   274\t                res.append(val)\n   275\t                cur = next(it)\n   276\t    except StopIteration:\n   277\t        pass\n   278\t    return res\n   279\t\n   280\t\n   281\tdef _load_tuple_and_call(tup: tuple[Any, ...]) -> list[Instruction]:\n   282\t    insts: list[Instruction] = []\n   283\t    _initial_push_null(insts)\n   284\t    insts.extend(create_load_const(val) for val in tup)\n   285\t    insts.extend(create_call_function(len(tup), False))\n   286\t    return insts\n   287\t\n   288\t\n   289\tclass ContinueExecutionCache:\n   290\t    cache = ExactWeakKeyDictionary()\n   291\t    generated_code_metadata = ExactWeakKeyDictionary()\n   292\t\n   293\t    @classmethod\n   294\t    def lookup(cls, code: types.CodeType, lineno: int, *key: Any) -> types.CodeType:\n   295\t        if code not in cls.cache:\n   296\t            cls.cache[code] = {}\n   297\t        key = tuple(key)\n   298\t        if key not in cls.cache[code]:\n   299\t            cls.cache[code][key] = cls.generate(code, lineno, *key)\n   300\t        return cls.cache[code][key]\n"

  "thought": "KEY INSIGHT! At line 348:\n```python\nmeta.instructions = copy.deepcopy(instructions)\n```\n\nThis stores a DEEP COPY of the original function's instructions at the time code1 is generated. So `meta.instructions` = deep copy of original function's instructions, with NEW instruction objects.\n\nAnd the hooks are processed later:\n```python\nfor i in range(nstack):\n    ...\n    if i in hooks:\n        hook_insts, exn_target = hook(code_options, cleanup)\n        ...\n        old_hook_target = offset_to_inst[hook_target_offset]\n        old_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target] = exn_target\n```\n\n`offset_to_inst = {inst.offset: inst for inst in instructions}` - maps offsets to instruction objects from the CURRENT `instructions` list (the one being modified).\n\n`old_hook_target = offset_to_inst[hook_target_offset]` - gets the instruction object from the CURRENT instructions list for the original PUSH_EXC_INFO offset.\n\n`old_hook_target_remap[old_hook_target] = exn_target` - maps old PUSH_EXC_INFO (from current instructions) to new PUSH_EXC_INFO (from hook epilogue).\n\nThen:\n```python\nif old_hook_target_remap:\n    for inst in instructions:\n        if inst.exn_tab_entry and inst.exn_tab_entry.target in old_hook_target_remap:\n            inst.exn_tab_entry.target = old_hook_target_remap[inst.exn_tab_entry.target]\n```\n\nThis remaps the targets in `instructions` (the CURRENT instructions, not `meta.instructions`).\n\nTHEN:\n```python\ninstructions[:] = prefix + instructions\n```\n\nAdds the prefix. Code1 = prefix + original instructions.\n\nAnd `meta.instructions` is a DEEP COPY of the ORIGINAL instructions (before prefix was added and before `old_hook_target_remap` was applied to current instructions). So `meta.instructions` has the ORIGINAL instruction objects, NOT the remapped ones.\n\nNow in `generate_based_on_original_code_object` for a SECOND graph break in code1:\n\nIn `find_new_offset`:\n```python\n(new_target,) = (\n    i2\n    for i1, i2 in zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions))\n    if i1 is target\n)\n```\n\n`target` is found from `instructions` (code1's instructions) as the instruction at the second graph break offset. `meta.instructions` is the deep copy of original instructions.\n\nSince `instructions` for code1 = prefix + deep_copy_of_original (with remapped targets), and `meta.instructions` = original deep copy (without remap, without prefix)...\n\nThe zip from the END: `reversed(code1_instructions)` vs `reversed(meta.instructions)`. The LAST N elements of code1_instructions correspond to meta.instructions (since prefix was PREPENDED). So zip from end finds correct correspondence.\n\nBut: `i1 is target` - `i1` is from `reversed(code1_instructions)`. `target` was found from `instructions` (same list, `i for i in instructions if i.offset == offset`). Same instruction list, same objects. \u2713\n\nOK now in `remap_block_offsets`:\n\n```python\nnew_targets = _filter_iter(\n    zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions)),\n    targets,\n    lambda v1, v2: v1[0] is v2,\n)\nfor new, old in zip(new_targets, targets):\n    block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset\n```\n\n`targets` = list of instruction objects from `instructions` (code1's instructions) that correspond to `old_inst_offsets`.\n\n`old_inst_offsets` = sorted offsets from `setup_fn_target_offsets` that are > `old_start_offset`.\n\nThese are CODE1 OFFSETS that are in the code1's instructions list (the cleanup section).\n\n`_filter_iter` looks for `v1[0] is v2` where v1 is (code1_inst, meta_inst) and v2 is the target instruction.\n\nTarget instructions are in `targets`, which contains instruction objects from `instructions` (code1). So `v1[0] is v2` will be True when `code1_inst is target`.\n\nBut here's the thing: `reversed(instructions)` vs `reversed(meta.instructions)` - we need to zip these correctly. The LAST instructions in code1 are the original function's instructions. And `meta.instructions` is a deep copy of the original instructions. The zip pairs them by position from the end.\n\nIf target is P_inner_original (an instruction from the original function, included in code1's instruction list), then:\n- `i1 is P_inner_original` = True at the corresponding position in reversed(instructions)\n- At that same zip position, `i2` is the corresponding instruction in meta.instructions\n- `new[1]` = i2.offset = original offset of P_inner_original in the ORIGINAL function\n\nWait! `meta.instructions` is a DEEP COPY. So `i2` is a COPY of the original instruction, with the ORIGINAL offset. `new[1].offset` = original function offset (e.g., 214 for inner PUSH_EXC_INFO). \u2713\n\nAnd `block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset`:\n- old = P_inner_original (code1 instruction)\n- old.offset = code1 compiled offset of P_inner_original\n- new[1].offset = 214 (original function offset)\n\nSo block_target_offset_remap[P_inner_original_code1_offset] = 214 \u2713\n\nThis would correctly map the inner PUSH_EXC_INFO's code1 offset back to 214 in the original function. \u2713\n\nBUT WAIT - there's a subtle issue with `old.offset`. `old` is from `targets`, which is the filtered result of `_filter_iter(instructions, old_inst_offsets, lambda inst, o: inst.offset == o)`. \n\n`old_inst_offsets` contains the CODE1 COMPILED OFFSETS (from `setup_fn_target_offsets`). So `_filter_iter` finds instructions in code1 with offsets matching `old_inst_offsets`. These are the instruction objects in code1's instruction list (after `transform_code_object` virtualization).\n\nHmm, but `transform_code_object` compiles the code and then virtualizes it, creating NEW instruction objects with potentially new offsets. The instruction objects in `instructions` when `remap_block_offsets` runs are the NEWLY VIRTUALIZED ones, not the original objects!\n\nLet me think about this more carefully.\n\n`transform_code_object(code, remap_block_offsets)`:\n1. Virtualizes code1: creates new instruction objects from code1's compiled bytecode\n2. Calls remap_block_offsets(new_instructions, ...)\n3. Recompiles the (possibly modified) instructions back to a code object\n\nIn step 2, `instructions` = newly virtualized instructions from code1's bytecode. These are FRESH instruction objects with the compiled offsets.\n\n`meta.instructions` = deep copy of original function's instructions (stored at code1 generation time, with ORIGINAL offsets).\n\nIn `remap_block_offsets`, the `zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions))` pairs:\n- NEW virtualized code1 instructions (from the END)\n- Original deep-copy instructions (from the END)\n\nThe LAST N instructions in code1 correspond to the original function's instructions (just with new offsets). The deep copy has the ORIGINAL offsets. So `new[1].offset` gives the original offset.\n\nAnd `lambda v1, v2: v1[0] is v2` checks `v1[0] is v2` where `v1[0]` is a newly virtualized instruction and `v2` is a target instruction from `targets`. \n\nBut `targets` was built by `_filter_iter(instructions, old_inst_offsets, lambda inst, o: inst.offset == o)`, which finds instructions from `instructions` (newly virtualized) with compiled offsets in `old_inst_offsets`. So `targets` contains newly virtualized instruction objects.\n\n`lambda v1, v2: v1[0] is v2` - v1[0] is from `reversed(instructions)` (newly virtualized), v2 is from `targets` (also from `instructions`). Since `targets` was built by iterating `instructions`, `v1[0] is v2` will be True for exactly those positions where the instruction was selected. \u2713\n\nOK this all works correctly. I still can't find the KeyError!\n\nYou know what, let me just try a completely different approach and look at what changed in recent commits to the relevant files.\n"

The exception table is re-read and `virtualize_exception_table` assigns `exn_tab_entry` to each instruction.\n\nIn code1's compiled bytecode, the exception table has entries based on the `exn_tab_entries` of the instructions in the generated code. Specifically, for the outer setup_with:\n- outer POP_TOP: exn_tab_entry.target = outer PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1\n\nAfter code1 is compiled, the outer POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target is the offset of outer PUSH_EXC_INFO (P_outer_offset). Let me look for what the SIMPLEST FIX could be that makes the nested case work.\n\nLet me look at the `remap_block_offsets` function one more time and think about edge cases.\n\n```python\ndef remap_block_offsets(instructions, code_options):\n    prefix_blocks = []\n    for inst in instructions:\n        if len(prefix_blocks) == len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n            break\n        if inst.opname == \"PUSH_EXC_INFO\":\n            prefix_blocks.append(inst)\n    \n    for inst, o in zip(prefix_blocks, meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap):\n        block_target_offset_remap[cast(int, inst.offset)] = o\n    \n    old_start_offset = cast(int, prefix_blocks[-1].offset) if prefix_blocks else -1\n    \n    old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > old_start_offset)\n    targets = _filter_iter(instructions, old_inst_offsets, lambda inst, o: inst.offset == o)\n    new_targets = _filter_iter(\n        zip(reversed(instructions), reversed(meta.instructions)),\n        targets,\n        lambda v1, v2: v1[0] is v2,\n    )\n    for new, old in zip(new_targets, targets):\n        block_target_offset_remap[old.offset] = new[1].offset\n```\n\nWait. What if `prefix_blocks` is EMPTY but `setup_fn_target_offsets` is non-empty?\n\nIf `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` is empty (len=0), the scan immediately breaks, `prefix_blocks = []`.\n\n`old_start_offset = -1`\n\n`old_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n > -1)` = ALL offsets.\n\nThis is fine - handles the case where there's no prefix context but the old bytecode has `with` blocks.\n\nWhat if `prefix_blocks` is non-empty but `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap` has FEWER entries than expected?\n\nActually `len(prefix_blocks) == len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap)` when the loop breaks, so the zip is always correct.\n\nOK one MORE thing I haven't considered: `setup_fn_target_offsets` can contain DUPLICATE offsets (if two block_stack entries have the same target). So excluding it from old_inst_offsets is fine.\n\nBut if `n == old_start_offset` AND `n` is NOT a prefix_block offset... then:\n- n is not in block_target_offset_remap (not from prefix_blocks zip)\n- n is not in old_inst_offsets (not > old_start_offset)\n- KeyError at line 669!\n\nWhen would `n == old_start_offset` but NOT be a prefix_block offset?\n\n`old_start_offset = prefix_blocks[-1].offset` = offset of the LAST prefix_block.\n\nIf `prefix_blocks[-1].offset == n` for some n in setup_fn_target_offsets:\n- n == prefix_blocks[-1].offset\n- So n IS a prefix_block offset \u2192 it IS in block_target_offset_remap \u2713\n\nSo this case is fine.\n\nWhat about: `n > old_start_offset` but `_filter_iter` FAILS to find it in `instructions`?\n\n`old_inst_offsets` contains CODE1 offsets that are > `old_start_offset`.\n\n`_filter_iter(instructions, old_inst_offsets, lambda inst, o: inst.offset == o)` finds instructions in code1 with those offsets. This exception is caught by the `step` function, and `handle_graph_break` is called.\n\nWhen `fn` is traced and Dynamo tries to inline `reversible`, inside `reversible` it encounters the CALL to `graph_break()`. Dynamo traces the CALL of `graph_break()`, which calls `SkipFunctionVariable.call_function()` which calls `unimplemented_v2(...)`. This raises `Unsupported`.\n\nThe `Unsupported` exception propagates up through the inlining. The outer translator catches it and calls `handle_graph_break` for the CALL instruction in `fn` (the call to `reversible(a)`).\n\nBut WAIT: when inlining fails, Dynamo doesn't simply call `handle_graph_break` for the CALL instruction in `fn`. There's a more complex mechanism involving `InliningInstructionTranslator.run()` and speculation management.\n\nLet me look at this more carefully. When inlining `reversible` and `graph_break()` causes Unsupported inside:\n\nActually, I think the issue might be more subtle. When Dynamo tries to inline `reversible(a)`, it creates a child InliningInstructionTranslator. During tracing of `reversible`:\n1. LOAD_GLOBAL `graph_break` \n2. CALL 0 \u2192 `graph_break.call_function(...)` \u2192 `unimplemented_v2(...)` \u2192 raises Unsupported\n\nThe Unsupported exception propagates up. The outer translator catches it at the step for the CALL instruction (calling `reversible(a)`). Since inlining failed, `handle_graph_break` is called.\n\nNow, `handle_graph_break` compiles the subgraph (everything computed so far: `torch.sin(x)`) and generates the resume function for the CALL instruction.\n\nThis is the FIRST graph break. Code1 is generated with:\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (318, 264) (outer no_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO, inner enable_grad PUSH_EXC_INFO in original)\n- Prefix with hooks for both contexts\n\nWhen code1 runs, it re-enters the contexts (via BEFORE_WITH) and jumps to the old bytecode start (STORE_FAST b).\n\nWhen Dynamo RE-TRACES code1 (for optimization), it traces the prologue, enters no_grad and enable_grad contexts, then the old bytecode.\n\nIn the old bytecode of code1, the instructions from offset 136 (STORE_FAST b) onwards include:\n- STORE_FAST b\n- LOAD_FAST x ... CALL ... sigmoid \u2192 c\n- STORE_FAST c\n- CALL .sum() \u2192 tmp\n- tmp.backward()\n- RETURN_VALUE x.grad\n\nDoes `backward()` cause a graph break? With `backend=\"eager\"`:\n- Dynamo traces `c.sum()` \u2192 compiles this as part of the subgraph\n- `.backward()` is then called on the result\n- Dynamo encounters `backward()` and needs to trace through it\n\nActually, with `backend=\"eager\"`, Dynamo doesn't need to break at `backward()` - it can trace through torch operations and compile them as-is (the eager backend doesn't do any optimization, just runs eagerly).\n\nHmm. But `.backward()` is not a simple torch operation - it triggers autograd execution. When Dynamo encounters this...\n\nActually, with \"eager\" backend, Dynamo compiles the forward pass into a module and then executes it. The backward is computed via PyTorch's autograd. Dynamo might not break at backward() at all!\n\nBut then: if there's no second graph break, code1 successfully executes, and there's no KeyError at all!\n\nSO: maybe the KeyError happens during the FIRST graph break's resume function GENERATION, not during a second graph break.\n\nLet me think: could code1's GENERATION fail?\n\nIn `generate_based_on_original_code_object` (or the regular `generate`), the hooks are called, and the code is compiled. Could `compute_exception_table` fail here?\n\nIn the REGULAR `generate` (code is NOT in generated_code_metadata), when hooks are called:\n- `hook(code_options, cleanup)` = `ReenterWith.__call__(code_options, cleanup)`\n- This generates `with ctx:` block code with BEFORE_WITH\n- The PUSH_EXC_INFO instruction is in the epilogue\n- This is appended to cleanup\n\nThen `instructions[:] = prefix + instructions` assembles the full code1 instruction list.\n\n`transform_code_object(code, update)` compiles this. During compilation, `compute_exception_table` runs. All `exn_tab_entry.target` pointers should be to instructions WITHIN the compiled code.\n\nAre all targets valid? The targets in the prefix's exception table entries point to P_inner and P_outer, which ARE in the instruction list. \u2713\n\nThe targets in the old bytecode have been remapped by `old_hook_target_remap` to P_inner and P_outer (for the ones that pointed to original 264 and 318). \u2713\n\nWhat about instructions in the old bytecode that DON'T have their targets remapped? These are instructions whose exn_tab_entry.target is NOT in `old_hook_target_remap`. In our test case, old_hook_target_remap has entries for both 264 and 318 (since both were in setup_fn_target_offsets). So ALL instructions in the old bytecode that pointed to 264 or 318 are remapped. \u2713\n\nBut what about instructions in the cleanup section (PUSH_EXC_INFO@264, PUSH_EXC_INFO@318 and their cleanup code)? These are part of `instructions` (the original function's instructions, which are appended in their entirety to the prefix). The cleanup section instructions might have their own exception table entries (e.g., [264, 284) \u2192 292, etc.).\n\nThese cleanup instructions in code1's old bytecode have exn_tab_entry.target pointing to other cleanup instructions (within the original function's instruction list). These targets ARE in code1's instruction list (since all original instructions are included). \u2713\n\nOK so code1 generation should work fine. No KeyError.\n\nWhat about code2's generation?\n\nIf there's NO second graph break, code2 is never generated. The test should pass without any issues!\n\nUnless... the test reveals a CORRECTNESS ISSUE (wrong gradients) rather than a KeyError crash?\n\nActually, re-reading the task:\n\"Fix two bugs in PyTorch Dynamo:\n1. KeyError crash in resume_execution.py during resume code generation on Python 3.11+ with nested context managers\n2. Incorrect STORE_ATTR handling in with blocks after graph breaks\"\n\nMaybe bug 1 is triggered by a test DIFFERENT from `test_with_on_graph_break_nested`? And `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` tests bug 1's scenario?\n\nOK let me just look at what changes are needed by studying the code paths more carefully, focusing on what could go WRONG.\n\nLet me look at `test_with_on_graph_break_inst` too:\n```python\ndef test_with_on_graph_break_inst(self):\n    def reversible(x):\n        print(\"Hello world\")  # Cause graph break so inline fails\n        return torch.sin(torch.cos(x))\n\n    def fn(x):\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nThis has only ONE context manager. The graph break is at `reversible(a)`. Code1 resumes at `c = torch.sigmoid(b)`. When Dynamo traces code1... does it have BEFORE_WITH in the prefix?\n\nYes! code1's prefix has BEFORE_WITH for enable_grad. When code1 is traced:\n- Outer BEFORE_WITH processed\n- block_stack: [{enable_grad, target=P_enable_grad}]\n\nThen old bytecode starts: `c = torch.sigmoid(b); c.sum().backward(); return x.grad`.\n\nDoes `backward()` with `torch.enable_grad()` context cause a second graph break?\n\n`c.sum().backward()` requires gradient computation. With `backend=\"eager\"`, this might work fine... Or it might cause a graph break.\n\nActually, I think `.backward()` DOES cause a graph break. When Dynamo encounters `tensor.backward()`, it tries to trace through it. Since `backward()` internally uses the autograd engine (C++ code), Dynamo can't trace through it and causes a graph break.\n\nSo yes, there IS a second graph break at `backward()` in code1!\n\nAt that second graph break:\n- block_stack = [{enable_grad, target=P_enable_grad_code1}] (one entry from prologue)\n- setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_enable_grad_code1,)\n\nFor this SINGLE-CONTEXT case:\n- meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [enable_grad_original_PUSH_EXC_INFO_offset]\n- prefix_blocks scan: finds P_enable_grad_code1 as first (and only) PUSH_EXC_INFO\n- block_target_offset_remap[P_enable_grad_code1] = enable_grad_original_offset \u2713\n\nNo KeyError for single context either.\n\nNOW for the NESTED case (`test_with_on_graph_break_nested`):\nSame analysis shows no KeyError.\n\nBUT WAIT. Does `backward()` cause a second graph break? Maybe it does for `test_with_on_graph_break_inst` (single context) but not for `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` (nested contexts)? Or maybe `backward()` always causes a graph break when inside a `with no_grad(): with enable_grad():` block?\n\nLet me think about this differently. The fact that `test_with_on_graph_break_inst` EXISTS and presumably PASSES means the code handles the single-context case correctly. The nested context case (`test_with_on_graph_break_nested`) is the NEW test being added to fix a BUG.\n\nSo the BUG must be specific to NESTED contexts. And my analysis shows that the remap works correctly for nested contexts... unless I'm wrong about the order of prefix_blocks.\n\nOK let me actually CHECK what the `__call__` method generates for NESTED hooks and what the resulting exception table looks like.\n\nActually, I think I need to look at the `_bytecode_from_template_with_split` function more carefully to understand exactly what PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions are generated and in what ORDER.\n"

What if an old bytecode block target is P_inner or P_outer (a PREFIX PUSH_EXC_INFO), but the filter `n > old_start_offset` excludes it?\n\nIf a `with` block in old bytecode has its PUSH_EXC_INFO remapped (via old_hook_target_remap) to P_inner or P_outer, then block_stack would have target = P_inner or P_outer. These are IN prefix_blocks, so they're covered. But `n > old_start_offset` filter is:\n- P_inner < P_outer = old_start_offset \u2192 P_inner NOT > old_start_offset \u2192 excluded from old_inst_offsets (but it IS in prefix_blocks remap \u2713)\n- P_outer = old_start_offset \u2192 NOT > old_start_offset \u2192 excluded (but it IS in prefix_blocks remap \u2713)\n\nSo the filter correctly separates: prefix targets (\u2264 old_start_offset) are handled by prefix_blocks, old bytecode targets (> old_start_offset) are handled by the filter.\n\nBUT: what if an old bytecode target EQUALS old_start_offset? No, `n > old_start_offset` is strict, so n == old_start_offset is excluded. And old_start_offset is P_outer (last prefix block). If an old bytecode target equals P_outer...\n\nCould an old bytecode target point to P_outer? Yes! If the `with` block in old bytecode is covered by the outer exception range (outer no_grad), and that range has been remapped to P_outer, then `another_ctx POP_TOP.exn_tab_entry.target = P_outer` (if another_ctx is only covered by no_grad, not enable_grad).\n\nIn that case, block_stack might have target = P_outer for the another_ctx entry. And `n = P_outer`. Since `P_outer > P_outer` = False, it's excluded. But P_outer IS in prefix_blocks. \u2713\n\nOK what if `setup_fn_target_offsets` has n == P_inner (not P_outer = old_start_offset)? P_inner < P_outer, so `P_inner > P_outer` = False, excluded. But P_inner IS in prefix_blocks. \u2713\n\nI keep finding that everything is correctly handled. Let me just accept that:\n1. Bug 1 exists (the task says so)\n2. The exact trigger is complex\n3. The fix involves something in remap_block_offsets\n\nLet me look at whether the `old_inst_offsets` filter should be `n >= old_start_offset` (inclusive) instead of `n > old_start_offset` (exclusive). Or maybe it should be NOT in prefix_blocks.\n\nActually, an alternative fix would be to use `n not in block_target_offset_remap` as the filter (instead of `n > old_start_offset`):\n\n```python\nold_inst_offsets = sorted(n for n in setup_fn_target_offsets if n not in block_target_offset_remap)\n```\n\nThis handles any n that was NOT covered by prefix_blocks, regardless of its offset. This is more robust.\n\nBut then what about targets from old bytecode that coincidentally have the same offset as a prefix block? They wouldn't... no, the prefix blocks are at CODE1 OFFSETS, and the old bytecode targets are also at CODE1 OFFSETS. They wouldn't coincide because prefix instructions and old bytecode instructions have different offsets.\n\nActually wait, there's a subtle issue: when `remap_block_offsets` is called for DIFFERENT `new_offset` values (different second graph breaks in code1), the `block_target_offset_remap` dict is SHARED (it's `meta.block_target_offset_remap[new_offset]`). So for each `new_offset`, there's a fresh `block_target_offset_remap`. \u2713\n\nLet me take a different approach and look for the bug in `setup_or_before_with` or `BEFORE_WITH` processing.\n\nActually, I just realized something. Let me look at what happens when a SECOND GRAPH BREAK occurs IN THE PREFIX of code1 (not in the old bytecode).\n\nNo, that's impossible. The prefix runs IS_TRACING check, and if IS_TRACING is True, it proceeds through BEFORE_WITH and then JUMP_ABSOLUTE. If IS_TRACING is False... wait, IS_TRACING is checked BEFORE the BEFORE_WITH instructions. Actually let me re-read `__call__`.\n"
}

The `lookup` function (line 294):\n```python\n@classmethod\ndef lookup(cls, code, lineno, *key):\n    if code not in cls.cache:\n        cls.cache[code] = {}\n    key = tuple(key)\n    if key not in cls.cache[code]:\n        cls.cache[code][key] = cls.generate(code, lineno, *key)\n    return cls.cache[code][key]\n```\n\nAnd `generate` (line 302):\n```python\n@classmethod\ndef generate(cls, code, lineno, offset, setup_fn_target_offsets, ...):\n    ...\n    if code in ContinueExecutionCache.generated_code_metadata:\n        return cls.generate_based_on_original_code_object(...)\n```\n\nSo `generate` checks if `code` is in `generated_code_metadata`. The difference is:\n1. Single context vs nested contexts\n2. `print()` vs `torch._dynamo.graph_break()` for the first break\n\nIf `test_with_on_graph_break_inst` passes but `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` fails, the issue is specific to NESTED CONTEXTS.\n\nLet me think about WHAT IS DIFFERENT for the second graph break (at `backward()`) between these two cases.\n\nFor `test_with_on_graph_break_inst` (single context):\n- Code1 resumes at `c = torch.sigmoid(b)` with `with torch.enable_grad():` context\n- block_stack in code1's old bytecode: from prologue [{enable_grad, P_enable_grad}]\n- At second graph break (backward()):\n  - setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_enable_grad,)\n  - `generate_based_on_original_code_object(code1, ..., (P_enable_grad,), ...)`\n  - meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap = [enable_grad_original_offset]\n  - prefix_blocks = [P_enable_grad_inst]\n  - block_target_offset_remap[P_enable_grad] = enable_grad_original_offset \u2713\n  - setup_fn_target_offsets remapped to (enable_grad_original_offset,) \u2713\n  - ContinueExecutionCache.lookup(original_fn, ..., (enable_grad_original_offset,), ...) \u2713\n\nFor `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` (nested contexts):\n- Code1 resumes at `c = torch.sigmoid(b)` with `with torch.no_grad(): with torch.enable_grad():` contexts\n- block_stack in code1's old bytecode: from prologue [{no_grad, P_no_grad}, {enable_grad, P_enable_grad}]\n- At second graph break (backward()):\n  - setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_no_grad, P_enable_grad)\n\nWait, WHICH ONE is outer and which is inner in code1?\n\nLet me re-check. For example, if P_inner is in a DIFFERENT instruction list (from a different transform_code_object call)?\n\nActually, this is the key! So after JUMP_ABSOLUTE, Dynamo processes from the jump target (STORE_FAST b in old bytecode).\n\nThe prefix instructions BEFORE JUMP_ABSOLUTE are executed/traced in order. JUMP_ABSOLUTE \u2192 instruction_pointer = offset of STORE_FAST b in code1 (> M+1)\n3. Continues from STORE_FAST b in old bytecode (original fn's instructions from that point)\n\nThe cleanup section [K+1..M] is NOT traced in normal control flow. \u2713\n\nSo Dynamo DOES process the outer and inner BEFORE_WITH in the prefix (steps 1), then jumps to old bytecode. {
  "thought": "This is a critical check at line 2806-2812! For Python 3.11+:\n\n```python\ndef should_compile_partial_graph(self) -> bool:\n    if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):\n        entry = self.current_instruction.exn_tab_entry\n        if entry and (\n            not self.block_stack or entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target\n        ):\n            return False\n```\n\nThis says: if the current instruction is inside an exception range whose target is NOT the top block_stack entry's target, return False (don't compile partial graph).\n\nFor code1's inner BEFORE_WITH:\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry` = None \u2192 condition is False \u2192 passes through\n\nFor original fn's CALL to reversible (which triggers graph break at CALL):\n- `inst.exn_tab_entry.target` = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO@264 (from original fn's exception table)\n- `self.block_stack[-1].target` = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO@264 (from inner BEFORE_WITH processing) \u2713\n- Condition: `entry.target is not self.block_stack[-1].target` \u2192 264_inst is 264_inst \u2192 True \u2192 False \u2192 NOT False \u2192 continues to return True \u2713\n\nFor `should_compile_partial_graph` in code1 at CALL to reversible:\n\nWhen Dynamo traces code1, it builds block_stack from code1's BEFORE_WITH instructions (in prefix). These block_stack entries have target = P_inner_code1 and P_outer_code1 (code1's template PUSH_EXC_INFO instances).\n\nAt the CALL to reversible (in old bytecode of code1), `current_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` = inner PUSH_EXC_INFO in code1's compiled bytecode. This is... what instruction?\n\nIn code1's compiled bytecode, the old bytecode's exception table entries had their targets REMAPPED:\n- `old_hook_target_remap[original_PUSH_EXC_INFO@318] = P_outer_template_inst`\n- `old_hook_target_remap[original_PUSH_EXC_INFO@264] = P_inner_template_inst`\n\nThe CALL to reversible was in the inner exception range (target = @264 in original fn). After remapping, its target = P_inner_template_inst.\n\nWhen code1 is VIRTUALIZED, the exception table is re-computed from compiled bytecode. The compiled bytecode has P_inner_template_inst at some compiled offset (say 100). The virtualized exception table says: instructions covered by range [X, Y) have target = code1_inst_at_offset_100.\n\nBut wait: is `code1_inst_at_offset_100` the SAME OBJECT as P_inner_template_inst?\n\n`virtualize_exception_table` reads the COMPILED bytecode's exception table (which stores OFFSETS), and then looks up the instruction object AT THAT OFFSET in the virtualized instruction list:\n\n```python\nfor inst in instructions:\n    if ...:\n        inst.exn_tab_entry = InstructionExnTabEntry(\n            start=virtualized_start,\n            end=virtualized_end,\n            target=indexof_offset[entry.target],  # instruction at target offset\n            ...\n        )\n```\n\nWhere `indexof_offset = {inst.offset: inst for inst in instructions}`.\n\nThe instruction at the compiled offset of P_inner_template_inst is... the SAME instruction object P_inner_template_inst? YES! Because `virtualize_exception_table` uses the SAME instruction objects that were compiled. The instruction objects are the same throughout (before and after compilation, they're the same Python objects, just with updated `.offset` attributes).\n\nSo `inst.exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_template_inst` (the exact same Python object). \u2713\n\nAnd `self.block_stack[-1].target` was set in `setup_or_before_with` from code1's inner BEFORE_WITH:\n- `target = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target` = inner POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_template_inst (from the compiled code1, virtualized)\n\nWait, but the inner POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target was also set to P_inner_template_inst during SETUP (before compilation). After code1 is compiled and virtualized, inner POP_TOP's exn_tab_entry.target = the instruction at the compiled offset of P_inner_template_inst = P_inner_template_inst (same object).\n\nSo `self.block_stack[-1].target IS P_inner_template_inst`. \u2713\n\nAnd `current_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target IS P_inner_template_inst`. \u2713\n\n`should_compile_partial_graph` returns True for the CALL to reversible in code1. \u2713\n\nEverything works for a SINGLE graph break. But what about the second graph break (at `backward()`)?\n\nWhen code1 has a second graph break at backward():\n- block_stack = [outer_code1, inner_code1] (from code1's prologue)\n- `setup_fn_target_offsets` = (P_outer_template_inst.offset, P_inner_template_inst.offset) [offsets in code1]\n\nThese offsets would be different from original fn offsets (318, 264).\n\nIn `generate_based_on_original_code_object`:\n- `new_offset` = (compiled offset of resume point in code1)\n- `remap_block_offsets` finds prefix_blocks and maps them to original fn offsets\n\nIf I continue this analysis... it should work correctly.\n\nOK I GIVE UP trying to analytically trace the exact bug scenario. Let me look at actual test failures in the git history or any bug report context.\n"

old bytecode targets are after the prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO's\n   647\t                    old_start_offset = (\n   648\t                        cast(int, prefix_blocks[-1].offset) if prefix_blocks else -1\n   649\t                    )\n   650\t                    # NOT for code1 generation (first graph break).\n\nActually, wait. There's something subtle here! For the nested case, the SECOND graph break in code1 triggers `generate_based_on_original_code_object` with setup_fn_target_offsets = (P_outer.offset, P_inner.offset). But the `prefix_blocks` scan looks for PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in code1 COUNTING ONLY the ones that match `meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap`.\n\nBUT: in code1's instruction list, the OLD BYTECODE also contains PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions (from the original function's cleanup code)! Since there's a SINGLE context, setup_fn_target_offsets has ONE element. Remap works fine.\n\nFor `test_with_on_graph_break_nested` to fail:\n- Nested `with torch.no_grad(): with torch.enable_grad():`\n- graph break via `graph_break()`\n- backward()\n\nIf backward() causes a second graph break, code2 is generated from code1 with TWO setup_fn_target_offsets. The remap might fail.\n\nBut I analyzed this and it SHOULDN'T fail. Or what if the `should_compile_partial_graph` check is more restrictive for nested contexts?\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-read `should_compile_partial_graph` for the nested case.\n\nIn code1, the old bytecode has been processed:\n- The CALL to backward() is in the INNER exception range (target = P_inner_template)\n- AND in the OUTER exception range (target = P_outer_template)\n- The virtualized code1 assigns `exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_template_inst` to the CALL to backward() (because the innermost range takes priority)\n\nWait, what does `virtualize_exception_table` do for NESTED exception ranges? If a CALL is covered by BOTH inner range (target=P_inner) and outer range (target=P_outer), which one is assigned to exn_tab_entry?\n\nThe exception table in compiled code1 has entries for different ranges. For the CALL to backward() in code1's old bytecode:\n- It's in the inner exception range (target=P_inner, depth=1, lasti=True)\n- It's ALSO in the outer exception range (target=P_outer, depth=0, lasti=True)\n\nBut Python's exception table is ordered: the INNERMOST range takes precedence. When an exception occurs, Python looks up the exception table and finds the INNERMOST matching range.\n\nWhen `virtualize_exception_table` processes the exception table of compiled code1, it assigns exn_tab_entries based on the compiled table. For the CALL to backward():\n- The entry that covers it is the INNERMOST one: inner range (target=P_inner)\n- `exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_template_inst` \u2713\n\nIn `should_compile_partial_graph`:\n- entry.target = P_inner_template_inst\n- block_stack[-1].target = P_inner_template_inst (inner block is top of stack) \u2713\n- Returns True \u2713\n\nOK wait, but what does \"nested exception ranges\" look like in Python 3.13's exception table?\n\nLet me check... Actually let me just look at the exception table for the ORIGINAL FUNCTION of the nested test.\n"

{
  "command": "python3 << 'EOF'\nimport dis\nfrom dis import _parse_exception_table\n\ndef reversible(x):\n    import torch\n    torch._dynamo.graph_break()\n    return x  # simplified\n\ndef fn_nested(x):\n    import torch\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = x * 2\n            b = a + 1  # pretend this is reversible(a)\n            c = b * 3\n            return c\n\ndef fn_single(x):\n    import torch\n    with torch.enable_grad():\n        a = x * 2\n        b = a + 1\n        c = b * 3\n        return c\n\nprint(\"=== fn_nested bytecode ===\")\nfor instr in dis.Bytecode(fn_nested):\n    print(f\"  {instr.offset:3d} {instr.opname:30s} {str(instr.argrepr):30s}\")\n\nprint()\nprint(\"=== fn_nested exception table ===\")\nfor e in _parse_exception_table(fn_nested.__code__):\n    print(f\"  {e}\")\n\nprint()\nprint(\"=== fn_single bytecode ===\")\nfor instr in dis.Bytecode(fn_single):\n    print(f\"  {instr.offset:3d} {instr.opname:30s} {str(instr.argrepr):30s}\")\n\nprint()\nprint(\"=== fn_single exception table ===\")\nfor e in _parse_exception_table(fn_single.__code__):\n    print(f\"  {e}\")\nEOF\n",
  "security_risk": "LOW"
}

But it SHOULD conceptually be in the OUTER exception range (similar to the original function where inner BEFORE_WITH at @74 is covered by [42,76) with target=outer PUSH_EXC_INFO @202).\n\nWithout exception coverage on inner BEFORE_WITH in code1's prefix, the `setup_or_before_with` condition becomes:\n\n```python\nif inst.exn_tab_entry and (...):\n```\n= `None and (...)` = False \u2192 ELSE BRANCH:\n```python\nassert self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry is not None\ntarget = self.next_instruction.exn_tab_entry.target\n```\n\nnext_instruction = inner POP_TOP in code1's prefix, which HAS exn_tab_entry.target = P_inner_template. \u2713\n\nSo target = P_inner_template. block_stack[-1].target = P_inner_template. \n\nThis is CORRECT for the INNER block entry. But is the OUTER block entry's target CORRECT?\n\nFor outer BEFORE_WITH in code1's prefix:\n- exn_tab_entry = None \u2192 ELSE \u2192 target = outer POP_TOP.exn_tab_entry.target = P_outer_template \u2713\n\nSo block_stack:\n- [outer: {no_grad, P_outer_template}, inner: {enable_grad, P_inner_template}] \u2713\n\nNow for `should_compile_partial_graph` at the CALL to reversible in code1's old bytecode:\n\nIn code1's old bytecode, the CALL to reversible is in the original function's instructions. From the original function's exception table: @96 is covered by [76,110) target=148 (inner PUSH_EXC_INFO). \n\nBut in code1, the CALL to reversible's exn_tab_entry was REMAPPED:\n- old_hook_target_remap = {inst@148: P_inner_template, inst@202: P_outer_template}\n- The CALL to reversible at @96 is in range [76,110) \u2192 target=@148 \u2192 remapped to P_inner_template\n\nWait, but the CALL to reversible is NOT in code1's old bytecode! The FIRST graph break is at CALL to reversible in the ORIGINAL FUNCTION. So code1 resumes AFTER that CALL (at STORE_FAST b). The CALL to reversible is NOT in code1 (it's before the resume offset).\n\nHmm... so what IS the second instruction after code1's resume? It would be STORE_FAST b, then the rest of the code after the graph break. The remaining code doesn't include the CALL to reversible.\n\nSo there's NO second CALL to reversible in code1! The remaining code is:\n- STORE_FAST b (the result of reversible is already on stack from code1's resume)\n- BINARY_OP * for c = b * 3\n- STORE_FAST c\n- LOAD_FAST c\n- SWAP, LOAD_CONST None \u00d7 3, CALL (\u2192 __exit__ of inner context), POP_TOP, RETURN_VALUE \u2190 this is the EXIT of the with block!\n- Then outer context exit...\n\nWait wait wait. The ORIGINAL FUNCTION's code after the graph break at CALL to reversible includes the EXIT of the inner and outer with blocks! This exit code uses the ORIGINAL exception handlers.\n\nFor the code path where execution SUCCEEDS (no exception), the exit of inner `with torch.enable_grad()` is done via:\n- SWAP (swap return value with ctx)\n- LOAD_CONST None \u00d7 3\n- CALL (__exit__(None, None, None))\n- POP_TOP\n- RETURN_VALUE (returns the actual value)\n\nBut code1's \"old bytecode\" starts at STORE_FAST b. The subsequent code includes the normal exit path. This exit path does NOT use exception handling at runtime (it's the normal case).\n\nThe exception handling paths (PUSH_EXC_INFO, WITH_EXCEPT_START, etc.) are in code1's old bytecode but are UNREACHABLE (for the normal exit case). They're there because `update` includes ALL original function instructions, not just the ones from the resume offset onward.\n\nSo in code1, Dynamo would trace:\n- STORE_FAST b (from old bytecode)\n- BINARY_OP * (computes c)\n- STORE_FAST c\n- LOAD_FAST c (loads the return value)\n- SWAP (swaps return value with inner ctx's exit fn)\n- LOAD_CONST None, None, None\n- CALL (__exit__(None, None, None)) \u2190 THIS is what might cause a graph break!\n\nActually, `torch.enable_grad.__exit__(None, None, None)` might be handled by Dynamo! Let me think...\n\nWhen Dynamo traces `CALL` to enable_grad's __exit__:\n- The exit function is a ContextWrappingVariable\n- Dynamo should handle this by calling `ctx.exit(self)` on the ContextWrappingVariable\n\nActually, in the `with` statement semantics, after the body, Python calls __exit__ via:\n- SWAP (exchange TOS with the WithExitFunctionVariable)\n- CALL 3 (__exit__(None, None, None))\n\nWhen Dynamo traces this, the SWAP puts the exit function on TOS, then CALL is processed. The exit function IS known to Dynamo (it's a WithExitFunctionVariable). Dynamo should handle it correctly.\n\nBut what if there's a graph break here? The CALL to __exit__ is a CALL instruction with exn_tab_entry:\n- In code1's old bytecode, CALL to __exit__ (inner) at what range?\n\nLooking at the original function: CALL at @118 (inner __exit__), covered by [110,128) \u2192 target=202 (outer PUSH_EXC_INFO). After remap in code1: target = P_outer_template.\n\nIn `should_compile_partial_graph`:\n- entry.target = P_outer_template\n- block_stack[-1].target = P_inner_template (inner is the TOP of block_stack)\n- `entry.target is not block_stack[-1].target` \u2192 P_outer_template is not P_inner_template \u2192 True!\n- condition: True AND (True) = True \u2192 returns FALSE!\n\nAH HA! `should_compile_partial_graph` returns FALSE for the CALL to inner __exit__!\n\nThis means: if there's a graph break at the CALL to inner __exit__ in code1's old bytecode, `should_compile_partial_graph` would return False \u2192 `store_attr_graph_break` would raise an error, and `handle_graph_break` would not compile.\n\nActually wait, if `should_compile_partial_graph` returns False, what happens?\n\nLooking at `@break_graph_if_unsupported`:\n```python\nif not self.should_compile_partial_graph():\n    unimplemented_v2(...)\n```\n\nIf `should_compile_partial_graph` returns False, it raises Unsupported immediately without calling `handle_graph_break`. So NO resume function is generated, and the graph break propagates UP.\n\nBut wait, this is checked in `store_attr_graph_break`:\n```python\nif not self.should_compile_partial_graph():\n    unimplemented_v2(...)\n```\n\nAnd this is called from `STORE_ATTR` handler. But we're looking at CALL to __exit__, not STORE_ATTR.\n\nFor CALL to __exit__ in code1's old bytecode: if there's a graph break at this CALL, it would go through `handle_graph_break` (for CALL instruction). `handle_graph_break` uses `@break_graph_if_unsupported` decorator which checks `should_compile_partial_graph()`.\n\nActually, let me re-read `break_graph_if_unsupported`:\n```python\ndef break_graph_if_unsupported(push: int) -> Callable[..., Any]:\n    def decorator(inner_fn: Callable[..., Any]) -> Callable[..., Any]:\n        @functools.wraps(inner_fn)\n        def wrapper(self, inst):\n            try:\n                inner_fn(self, inst)\n            except Unsupported as excp:\n                ...\n                if not self.should_compile_partial_graph():\n                    raise\n                handle_graph_break(self, inst, reason)\n        return wrapper\n    return decorator\n```\n\nSo it's: try the inner_fn, if it raises Unsupported, check should_compile_partial_graph \u2192 if False, raise (propagate), else handle_graph_break.\n\nFor CALL to __exit__ in code1's old bytecode:\n- IF __exit__ raises Unsupported \u2192 BUT __exit__ for ContextWrappingVariable shouldn't raise Unsupported, it should be handled correctly by Dynamo.\n- torch.enable_grad.__exit__ is a known operation \u2192 Dynamo handles it \u2192 no Unsupported.\n\nSo no graph break at __exit__! \u2713\n\nBut then... when does the graph break occur in code1?\n\nLet me re-read the test:\n```python\ndef fn(x):\n    with torch.no_grad():\n        with torch.enable_grad():\n            a = torch.sin(x)\n            b = reversible(a)\n            c = torch.sigmoid(b)\n            c.sum().backward()\n            return x.grad\n```\n\nWith `reversible` doing `graph_break()`. The graph break is at the CALL to `reversible(a)`. After the first graph break (at CALL to reversible), the remaining code is:\n```\nc = torch.sigmoid(b)\nc.sum().backward()\nreturn x.grad\n```\n\nNote: `backward()` is INSIDE the `with torch.enable_grad():` block! \n\nNow, when code1 (resume function) is traced by Dynamo:\n- After processing the prologue (BEFORE_WITH for both contexts)\n- Resumes at STORE_FAST b (= reversible's result from stack)\n\nWait, the graph break was at CALL to reversible. The result of CALL (the return value of reversible) is on the stack. Then:\n- STORE_FAST b\n\nNo wait: the CALL to reversible failed (graph break), so its result is NOT on the stack in the compiled graph. But code1 receives `b` as a FUNCTION ARGUMENT (it's part of the stack that's saved). \n\nActually: when `handle_graph_break` is called for the CALL to reversible:\n- stack_pops = push - stack_effect where push is the stack push count of CALL and stack_effect = dis.stack_effect(CALL, arg)\n- For CALL 1 (1 positional arg): stack_effect = -1 (pops 2: null+fn+arg, pushes 1: result) \u2192 net = -1\n  Actually: push is the CURRENT stack size before CALL? No, `push` is the decorator parameter.\n\nLooking at `@break_graph_if_unsupported(push=0)` \u2192 push=0 for CALL. And:\n```python\nstack_pops = push - stack_effect\n```\n= 0 - (-2) = 2 (for CALL with 1 arg: pops null+fn+1arg = 3, pushes 1 result, net = -2... hmm but it depends on the specific CALL)\n\nActually, for CALL n: pops (1+n) + 1 (null or self) = n+2, pushes 1. net = 1 - (n+2) = -(n+1).\n\nFor reversible(a): CALL 1 \u2192 pops 3 (null, fn, arg), pushes 1. stack_effect = -2.\n\nstack_pops = 0 - (-2) = 2 \u2192 compile_subgraph with stack_pops=2.\n\nAfter handle_graph_break, the output code:\n- Compiled subgraph output (up to before CALL to reversible)\n- try_finally setup for no_grad and enable_grad contexts (for 2 nested contexts!)\n- NEW CALL instruction (to reversible)\n- create_call_resume_at (code1 call)\n- Cleanup\n\nWait, but `b = reversible(a)` \u2192 CALL to reversible is the instruction. In `handle_graph_break`, for CALL instruction:\n```python\nif sys.version_info >= (3, 11) and inst.opname == \"CALL\":\n    ...\n    call_insts = create_call_function(inst.arg, False)\n    ...\n    self.output.add_output_instructions(call_insts)\n```\n\nThis creates NEW CALL instructions (without exn_tab_entry). \u2713\n\nThen code1 is called with `b` on the stack (b = result of CALL to reversible).\n\ncode1 resumes at the next instruction after CALL = the next byte after CALL. In Python 3.13 CALL: the next instruction is STORE_FAST b.\n\nSo code1 receives `b` as an argument and starts with STORE_FAST b. \u2713\n\nNow, code1's remaining code (original fn instructions from STORE_FAST b):\n- @96: STORE_FAST b (stores b)\n- torch.sigmoid(b):\n  - LOAD_FAST b\n  - LOAD_GLOBAL torch\n  - LOAD_ATTR sigmoid\n  - CALL\n  - STORE_FAST c\n- c.sum().backward():\n  - LOAD_FAST c\n  - LOAD_ATTR sum\n  - CALL\n  - LOAD_ATTR backward\n  - CALL \u2190 backward() call\n  - POP_TOP\n- return x.grad:\n  - LOAD_FAST x\n  - LOAD_ATTR grad\n  - SWAP (to pass to __exit__)\n  ...\n\nWait, `return x.grad` inside the with block generates:\n- LOAD_FAST x\n- LOAD_ATTR grad\n- SWAP (exchange with exit fn on top)\n- LOAD_CONST None, None, None\n- CALL (__exit__(None, None, None)) \u2190 inner __exit__\n- POP_TOP\n- SWAP (exchange with outer exit fn)\n- LOAD_CONST None, None, None\n- CALL (__exit__(None, None, None)) \u2190 outer __exit__\n- POP_TOP\n- RETURN_VALUE\n\nAll of these are in code1's old bytecode.\n\nNow, when Dynamo traces code1:\n- STORE_FAST b \u2713\n- torch.sigmoid: LOAD_GLOBAL torch, LOAD_ATTR sigmoid, CALL \u2192 graphs compile torch.sigmoid(b)\n- c.sum().backward():\n  - LOAD_FAST c \u2192 result\n  - LOAD_ATTR sum \u2192 creates SumMethod\n  - CALL \u2192 compiles c.sum()\n  - LOAD_ATTR backward \u2192 creates BackwardMethod\n  - CALL \u2190 THIS is the backward() CALL\n\nThe backward() CALL is at some offset in code1's old bytecode. "Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /workspace/torch/_dynamo/bytecode_transformation.py:\n  1739\tdef bytecode_from_template(\n  1740\t    fn: Callable[..., Any],\n  1741\t    varname_map: Optional[Mapping[Any, Any]] = None,\n  1742\t    noreturn: bool = True,\n  1743\t    noprefix: bool = True,\n  1744\t) -> list[Instruction]:\n  1745\t    \"\"\"Generates bytecode from a template function `fn` for use in\n  1746\t    dynamo bytecode generation.\n  1747\t\n  1748\t    For example, we can generate Python-version-independent bytecode\n  1749\t    for looping through a dictionary and copying the values to a new dictionary.\n  1750\t\n  1751\t    def template(d1, d2):\n  1752\t        for k, v in d1.items():\n  1753\t            d2[k] = v\n  1754\t\n  1755\t\n  1756\t    or a try block:\n  1757\t\n  1758\t    def template():\n  1759\t        try:\n  1760\t            dummy1\n  1761\t        except:\n  1762\t            dummy2\n  1763\t            raise\n  1764\t        dummy3\n  1765\t\n  1766\t    Args:\n  1767\t        fn: a function template to generate bytecode from\n  1768\t        varname_map: a mapping of `fn`'s varnames to new names. This\n  1769\t            map will be applied to the generated bytecode's varnames.\n  1770\t            For example, local variables in `fn` can be replaced with\n  1771\t            new names that are generated by `OutputGraph.new_var`.\n  1772\t        noreturn: remove all RETURN_* bytecodes and replace them with a jump\n  1773\t            to the end of the bytecode. NOTE: any items pushed to the stack\n  1774\t            for return WILL remain on the stack! Append a POP_TOP if you don't want\n  1775\t            that item to be present.\n  1776\t        noprefix: remove prefix bytecodes (all bytecode before the first RESUME, inclusive).\n  1777\t    \"\"\"\n  1778\t    insts = cleaned_instructions(fn.__code__)\n  1779\t    clear_instruction_args(insts)\n  1780\t\n  1781\t    if noprefix:\n  1782\t        for i, inst in enumerate(insts):\n  1783\t            if inst.opname == \"RESUME\":\n  1784\t                insts = insts[i + 1 :]\n  1785\t                break\n  1786\t\n  1787\t    for inst in insts:\n  1788\t        # If we don't reset starts_line, then the generated\n  1789\t        # bytecode's line number will be based on fn's.\n  1790\t        inst.starts_line = None\n  1791\t        inst.positions = None\n  1792\t        if varname_map and inst.argval in varname_map:\n  1793\t            inst.argval = varname_map[inst.argval]\n  1794\t\n  1795\t    if noreturn:\n  1796\t        if sys.version_info >= (3, 12):\n  1797\t            # replace RETURN_CONST with LOAD_CONST RETURN_VALUE\n  1798\t            new_insts = []\n  1799\t            for inst in insts:\n  1800\t                if inst.opname == \"RETURN_CONST\":\n  1801\t                    inst.opcode = dis.opmap[\"LOAD_CONST\"]\n  1802\t                    inst.opname = \"LOAD_CONST\"\n  1803\t                    new_insts.append(inst)\n  1804\t                    # no need to propagate target/exn table\n  1805\t                    new_insts.append(create_instruction(\"RETURN_VALUE\"))\n  1806\t                else:\n  1807\t                    new_insts.append(inst)\n  1808\t            insts = new_insts\n  1809\t\n  1810\t        returns = []\n"

Specifically, if there are PUSH_EXC_INFO instructions in the old bytecode that appear AFTER the prefix PUSH_EXC_INFO's, they would be skipped by the prefix_blocks scan (because len(prefix_blocks) == len(meta.prefix_block_target_offset_remap) stops the scan).
